pairs.context_after: VII. Transparency & oversight Transparency plays a pivotal role in any accountability model powered by regulation. This is best achieved, in our view, by establishing a requirement for regular reporting consisting of a combination of clear, quantitative metrics - such as prevalence- and qualitative analysis by which companies’ systems are judged. Companies should be held accountable for the effectiveness of their systems, rather than holding them liable for each individual piece of content. While one-off events or individual pieces of content will test the effectiveness of the system, one-off events should not be the focus when it comes to assessing compliance. Cooperation is needed when such one-off events take place to help form guidance on best practice for systems. Transparency is key to building solutions for content moderation within the diverse network of stakeholder which unlike targets, transparency can provide oversight and accountability without necessarily distorting the behaviour of platforms or creating unintended consequences (such as a potential chilling effect on free speech). There is currently no regarded set of benchmarks for auditing content moderation systems, which is needed to develop trust in the transparency reports, including verification on data regarding how much content is removed from the platform, the reasons for doing so, how much content was identified through proactive means and how often the content appeared on the platform
pairs.article_title: Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
pairs.article_text:
Article 6 — Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
1. Providers of intermediary services shall not be deemed ineligible for the exemptions from liability referred to in Articles 3, 4 and 5 solely because they carry out voluntary own-initiative investigations or other activities aimed at detecting, identifying and removing, or disabling of access to, illegal content, or take the necessary measures to comply with the requirements of Union law, including those set out in this Regulation.
pairs.provision_effect: Article 6 establishes that providers of intermediary services shall not lose their liability exemptions solely because they voluntarily conduct investigations or take measures to detect, identify, remove, or disable access to illegal content, or to comply with Union law.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: Facebook explicitly advocated for the DSA to integrate voluntary enforcement actions into the liability framework with built-in safeguards ensuring that such voluntary measures do not compromise service providers' limited liability — precisely what Article 6 delivers. The provision directly responds to Facebook's specific ask by codifying that voluntary own-initiative investigations do not disqualify providers from liability exemptions, reconciling voluntary responsibility with legal certainty as Facebook requested.

pairs.source_text: services must be of a “mere technical, automatic and passive nature” and the provider must be neutral in relation to the content hosted.2 In Google France, the CJEU held: “In order for the storage by a referencing service provider to come within the scope of of Directive 2000/31, it is further necessary that the conduct of that service provider should be limited to that of an ‘intermediary service provider’ within the meaning intended by the legislature in the context of Section 4 of that directive. In that regard, it follows from in the preamble to Directive 2000/31 that the exemptions from liability established in that directive cover only cases in which the activity of the information society service provider is ‘of a mere technical, automatic and passive nature’, which implies that that service provider ‘has neither knowledge of nor control over the information which is transmitted or stored’.”3 In eBay, the CJEU held: “[T]he mere fact that the operator of an online marketplace store offers for sale on its server, sets the terms of its service, is remunerated for that service and provides general information to its customers cannot have the effect of denying it the exemptions from liability provided for by Directive 2000/31 (see, by analogy, Google France and Google, paragraph 116)
pairs.context_before: The same services advocate today for “clarifying” the scope of Article 14 (by abolishing the distinction between active and passive platforms) and for introducing broader liability shields. The distinction between passive and active is clear and essential As referred to above, in order for a hosting provider to benefit from the ECD liability privilege, the European Court of Justice (CJEU) has ruled, with reference to Recital 421, that the hosting provider’s 1 The exemptions from liability established in this Directive cover only cases where the activity of the information society service provider is limited to the technical process of operating and giving access to a communication network over which information made available by third parties is transmitted or temporarily stored, for the sole purpose of making the transmission more efficient; this activity is of a mere technical, automatic and passive nature, which implies that the information society service provider has neither knowledge of nor control over the information which is transmitted or stored. 4
pairs.context_after: Where, by contrast, the operator has provided assistance which entails, in particular, optimizing the presentation of the offers for sale in question or promoting those offers, it must be considered not to have taken a neutral position between the customer-seller concerned and potential buyers but to have played an active role of such a kind as to give it knowledge of, or control over, the data relating to those offers for sale. It cannot then rely, in the case of those data, on the exemption from liability referred to in Article 14(1) of Directive 2000/31.”4 Finally, in this regard, a German case concerning YouTube is instructive. The Hamburg Court of Appeal confirmed that YouTube does not fall under Article 14 ECD, as YouTube (i) gives individualized music recommendations to users and (ii) suggests next to the viewed videos further (presumably) interesting videos.5 Furthermore, YouTube plays an active role, such as to fall outside of Article 14, by its provision of extensive user-friendly functions, such as search, categories with genres, filtering, marking, playlists, playing functions, recommendations to third parties etc.6 Recital 42 and the CJEU case law is clear and essential in defining the boundaries of Articles 12-15 ECD. Critics have argued that the distinction is obsolete because it was conceived a several years ago, disregarding the further clarifications that practice has brought
pairs.article_text:
Article 3 — ‘Mere conduit
1. Where an information society service is provided that consists of the transmission in a communication network of information provided by a recipient of the service, or the provision of access to a communication network, the service provider shall not be liable for the information transmitted, on condition that the provider:
(a)does not initiate the transmission;
(b)does not select the receiver of the transmission; and
(c)does not select or modify the information contained in the transmission.

2. The acts of transmission and of provision of access referred to in paragraph 1 include the automatic, intermediate and transient storage of the information transmitted in so far as this takes place for the sole purpose of carrying out the transmission in the communication network, and provided that the information is not stored for any period longer than is reasonably necessary for the transmission.

3. This Article shall not affect the possibility for a court or administrative authority, in accordance with Member States' legal systems, of requiring the service provider to terminate or prevent an infringement.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a 'mere conduit' liability exemption for service providers that passively transmit information without initiating, selecting the receiver of, or modifying the transmission.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a recitation of existing CJEU case law (Google France, eBay) describing the current legal framework for intermediary liability exemptions — it does not express a specific directional advocacy position on what the new provision should do. It is background legal description rather than a concrete ask for the provision to be expanded, narrowed, or changed in any particular way.

pairs.source_text: Current liability rules do not disincentivize voluntary action by hosting providers Certain services argue that the existing liability regime disincentivizes them from proactively removing illegal, unlawful, harmful and/or other undesirable content. It is, however, important to recall why this argument is wholly misplaced: It could only have possible application in the case of passive platforms, i.e. intermediary online services that qualify for the liability privileges in the ECD. It is not a consideration for active platforms which, in any case, are not eligible for the liability privileges
pairs.context_before: Critics have argued that the distinction is obsolete because it was conceived a several years ago, disregarding the further clarifications that practice has brought. The reality is that any attempt at defining business ecosystems is likely to become obsolete very quickly, thus creating legal uncertainty and therefore requiring additional case law to clarify the situation. 2 Judgment of the Court (Grand Chamber) of 23 March 2010, Google France, Joined cases C-236/08 to C-238/08, EU:C:2010:159 and Judgment of the Court (Grand Chamber) of 12 July 2011, L’Oréal SA and Others v eBay International AG and Others, Case C-324/09, EU:C:2011:474. 3 Paragraphs 112 and 113. 4 Paragraphs 115 and 116. 5 Court of Appeal (Oberlandesgericht) of Hamburg 1 July 2015, 5 U 87/12 juris para. 338. 6 Court of Appeal (Oberlandesgericht) of Hamburg, op cit., juris para. 346. 5
pairs.context_after: intermediary online services that qualify for the liability privileges in the ECD. It is not a consideration for active platforms which, in any case, are not eligible for the liability privileges. In any event, a certain level of activity in addressing illegal content is already required as a condition of the liability privileges, as the CJEU has held that the hosting defense will be unavailable where the service provider is aware of facts or circumstances on the basis of which a diligent economic operator should have identified the illegality in question and acted to remove or disable access to the relevant information.7 Also, in the Youtube/Uploaded referral to the CJEU, the German Federal Supreme Court (BGH) indicated that taking (proactive) action against illegal content is a factor leading to less liability for the platform, not more.8 We note, also, that in its Communication on Tackling Illegal Content9 and Recommendation on measures to effectively tackle illegal content online,10 the Commission sets out its view that taking voluntary proactive measures does not automatically lead to a loss of the benefit of the safe harbor.11 The CJEU has made it abundantly clear that the active role leading to the loss of the liability privilege is linked to promoting illegal content – not to disabling it, which in any event is currently an obligation once awareness or knowledge is obtained
pairs.article_title: Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
pairs.article_text:
Article 6 — Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
1. Providers of intermediary services shall not be deemed ineligible for the exemptions from liability referred to in Articles 3, 4 and 5 solely because they carry out voluntary own-initiative investigations or other activities aimed at detecting, identifying and removing, or disabling of access to, illegal content, or take the necessary measures to comply with the requirements of Union law, including those set out in this Regulation.
pairs.provision_effect: Article 6 establishes that providers of intermediary services do not lose their liability exemptions solely because they voluntarily take actions to detect, identify, and remove illegal content or comply with Union law requirements.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: The MPA EMEA explicitly argues that the claim that existing liability rules disincentivize voluntary action is "wholly misplaced," suggesting no legislative fix is needed on this point. Article 6 directly addresses and resolves that very concern by codifying that voluntary action does not forfeit liability protection — the exact mechanism MPA EMEA argues is unnecessary and misapplied.

pairs.source_text: 4. A targeted approach to notice and action From a regulatory standpoint, the objective considerations involved in removing the vast majority of instances of illegal content that is infringing IP rights (for which establishing the illegal nature is usually very straightforward) are entirely unlike the more subjective, context-dependent considerations that are routinely involved in taking down other types of illegal content, including terrorist content, hate speech, etc. So far, the Commission has rightfully adopted a “per problem approach” when problems do arise in relation to notice and action, and we believe that this approach has been nuanced and appropriate: 17 See, for example, the European Commission’s letter to Göran Marby, ICANN President, 29 January 2018: https://www.icann.org/en/system/files/correspondence/avramopoulos-et-al-to-marby-29jan18-en.pdf 18 For example Denmark, with respect to the WHOIS data for .dk as established by Section 18 of the Danish Internet Domain Act. See: https://www.icann.org/en/system/files/correspondence/vignal-schjoth-to-plexida- 28may20-en.pdf 10
pairs.context_before: This division includes whether fighting against copyright and trade mark infringement is a “legitimate purpose” and whether data from legal and natural persons should be treated differently. Furthermore, progress on a workable centralized access model for non-public WHOIS data to serve legitimate interests has been stalled. The GDPR was never intended to cut off access to data for legitimate purposes; the unintended consequences it has had on the availability of WHOIS data deserve careful scrutiny. This concern over the lack of access to WHOIS data has already been raised by the Commission17 and is echoed in the European Parliament. This is not a problem that is inherent to the GDPR itself. Some Member States, which are clearly subject to the GDPR, have determined with respect to WHOIS data for their own country code top-level domains that such data serves public interests so that the WHOIS data, even for natural person registrants, must be publicly accessible (including name, address and telephone number).18 We urge that as part of the DSA the Commission declare the public interest nature of WHOIS data for both generic and European country code top-level domains and establish that this data should, as it was for decades prior to May 2018, be publicly accessible. 4
pairs.context_after: 1. Copyright content In the copyright context, Article 17 of the Copyright in the Digital Single Market Directive creates a notice and action procedure for copyright protected content on certain digital services, whereby a “sufficiently substantiated notice” is required, a complaint mechanism is introduced and the complaint should be processed without undue delay and subject to human review. No further action is required in the context of copyright content covered by Article 17. This provision embodies obligations concerning prevention and stay-down that apply to OSCCPs who wish to avoid liability for copyright infringement. 2. Harmful content The Platform to Business Regulation19 introduced procedural rules regarding the adjudication of harmful content. Platforms must provide a “statement of reasons” before or when taking action against their business users. In addition, they must establish an internal complaint-handling system, allowing for appeals. The system must be easily accessible and free of charge while being based on the principles of transparency and equal treatment. Platforms are obliged to duly consider, process swiftly and adequately address complaints. The outcome is to be “communicated … in an individualized manner”. For complaints that cannot be resolved, the platform must provide for an independent mediation process. 3
pairs.article_text:
Article 55 — Interim measures
1. In the context of proceedings which may lead to the adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 58(1), where there is an urgency due to the risk of serious damage for the recipients of the service, the Commission may, by decision, order interim measures against the very large online platform concerned on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement.

2. A decision under paragraph 1 shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
pairs.provision_effect: Authorises the Commission to order interim measures against very large online platforms on the basis of a prima facie finding of infringement where there is urgency due to risk of serious damage to recipients of the service.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text advocates for a targeted, per-problem regulatory approach to notice-and-action for illegal content (particularly IP-infringing content), but does not express any specific position on interim measures or emergency enforcement powers against very large online platforms. The org text is focused on distinguishing IP infringement takedowns from other content types and endorsing a nuanced regulatory approach, which does not constitute a directional stance on the interim measures mechanism established by Article 55.

Terrorist content The proposal for a Regulation preventing the dissemination of terrorist content online introduced a template for removal orders that the competent authorities shall have the power to issue requiring hosting service providers to remove terrorist content or disable access to it. The template includes a statement of reasons, a URL and where necessary additional information allowing the identification of the content as well as information about the redress available to both the hosting provider and the content provider. It also requires hosting providers to set up effective and accessible mechanisms for content providers and to promptly examine every complaint that they receive. Hosting providers are also required to reinstate the content without undue delay where the removal is unjustified and to inform the complainant about the outcome of the examination. ***** 19 Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 June 2019 on promoting fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services 11 Avenue des Arts 46, 1000 Brussels, Belgium • +32 2 7782711 • www.mpaeurope.org
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to suspend services to users who frequently provide manifestly illegal content, and to suspend processing of notices/complaints from those who frequently submit manifestly unfounded notices, with case-by-case assessment and transparent policies.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text describes existing legal frameworks (CDSM Directive, Platform-to-Business Regulation) and their notice-and-action procedures as background context, without expressing a specific directional advocacy position on anti-abuse/suspension mechanisms for platforms. There is no specific stance for or against the misuse-suspension mechanism established in Article 20.

pairs.source_text: 1. Copyright content In the copyright context, of the Copyright in the Digital Single Market Directive creates a notice and action procedure for copyright protected content on certain digital services, whereby a “sufficiently substantiated notice” is required, a complaint mechanism is introduced and the complaint should be processed without undue delay and subject to human review. No further action is required in the context of copyright content covered by . This provision embodies obligations concerning prevention and stay-down that apply to OSCCPs who wish to avoid liability for copyright infringement. 2. Harmful content The Platform to Business Regulation19 introduced procedural rules regarding the adjudication of harmful content. Platforms must provide a “statement of reasons” before or when taking action against their business users. In addition, they must establish an internal complaint-handling system, allowing for appeals. The system must be easily accessible and free of charge while being based on the principles of transparency and equal treatment. Platforms are obliged to duly consider, process swiftly and adequately address complaints. The outcome is to be “communicated … in an individualized manner”. For complaints that cannot be resolved, the platform must provide for an independent mediation process. 3
pairs.context_before: 4. A targeted approach to notice and action From a regulatory standpoint, the objective considerations involved in removing the vast majority of instances of illegal content that is infringing IP rights (for which establishing the illegal nature is usually very straightforward) are entirely unlike the more subjective, context-dependent considerations that are routinely involved in taking down other types of illegal content, including terrorist content, hate speech, etc. So far, the Commission has rightfully adopted a “per problem approach” when problems do arise in relation to notice and action, and we believe that this approach has been nuanced and appropriate: 17 See, for example, the European Commission’s letter to Göran Marby, ICANN President, 29 January 2018: https://www.icann.org/en/system/files/correspondence/avramopoulos-et-al-to-marby-29jan18-en.pdf 18 For example Denmark, with respect to the WHOIS data for .dk as established by Section 18 of the Danish Internet Domain Act. See: https://www.icann.org/en/system/files/correspondence/vignal-schjoth-to-plexida- 28may20-en.pdf 10
pairs.context_after: The outcome is to be “communicated … in an individualized manner”. For complaints that cannot be resolved, the platform must provide for an independent mediation process. 3. Terrorist content The proposal for a Regulation preventing the dissemination of terrorist content online introduced a template for removal orders that the competent authorities shall have the power to issue requiring hosting service providers to remove terrorist content or disable access to it. The template includes a statement of reasons, a URL and where necessary additional information allowing the identification of the content as well as information about the redress available to both the hosting provider and the content provider. It also requires hosting providers to set up effective and accessible mechanisms for content providers and to promptly examine every complaint that they receive. Hosting providers are also required to reinstate the content without undue delay where the removal is unjustified and to inform the complainant about the outcome of the examination. ***** 19 Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 June 2019 on promoting fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services 11 Avenue des Arts 46, 1000 Brussels, Belgium • +32 2 7782711 • www.mpaeurope.org
pairs.article_text:
Article 17 — Internal complaint-handling system
1. Online platforms shall provide recipients of the service, for a period of at least six months following the decision referred to in this paragraph, the access to an effective internal complaint-handling system, which enables the complaints to be lodged electronically and free of charge, against the following decisions taken by the online platform on the ground that the information provided by the recipients is illegal content or incompatible with its terms and conditions:
(a)decisions to remove or disable access to the information;
(b)decisions to suspend or terminate the provision of the service, in whole or in part, to the recipients;
(c)decisions to suspend or terminate the recipients’ account.

2. Online platforms shall ensure that their internal complaint-handling systems are easy to access, user-friendly and enable and facilitate the submission of sufficiently precise and adequately substantiated complaints.

3. Online platforms shall handle complaints submitted through their internal complaint-handling system in a timely, diligent and objective manner. Where a complaint contains sufficient grounds for the online platform to consider that the information to which the complaint relates is not illegal and is not incompatible with its terms and conditions, or contains information indicating that the complainant’s conduct does not warrant the suspension or termination of the service or the account, it shall reverse its decision referred to in paragraph 1 without undue delay.

4. Online platforms shall inform complainants without undue delay of the decision they have taken in respect of the information to which the complaint relates and shall inform complainants of the possibility of out-of-court dispute settlement provided for in Article 18 and other available redress possibilities.

5. Online platforms shall ensure that the decisions, referred to in paragraph 4, are not solely taken on the basis of automated means.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to provide recipients with an effective, free, electronic internal complaint-handling system for at least six months against decisions to remove content, suspend services, or terminate accounts, with timely and objective handling and no solely automated decisions.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The MPA EMEA text does not advocate for or against an internal complaint-handling system for recipients/users of online platforms; instead, it describes existing legal frameworks (the Copyright DSM Directive and the Platform-to-Business Regulation) as background context and argues that no further action is needed in the copyright context. This is descriptive/background text with no specific directional advocacy position on the provision's subject matter.

pairs.article_text:
Article 18 — Out-of-court dispute settlement
1. Recipients of the service addressed by the decisions referred to in Article 17(1), shall be entitled to select any out-of-court dispute that has been certified in accordance with paragraph 2 in order to resolve disputes relating to those decisions, including complaints that could not be resolved by means of the internal complaint-handling system referred to in that Article. Online platforms shall engage, in good faith, with the body selected with a view to resolving the dispute and shall be bound by the decision taken by the body.
The first subparagraph is without prejudice to the right of the recipient concerned to redress against the decision before a court in accordance with the applicable law.

2. The Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State where the out-of-court dispute settlement body is established shall, at the request of that body, certify the body, where the body has demonstrated that it meets all of the following conditions:
(a)it is impartial and independent of online platforms and recipients of the service provided by the online platforms;
(b)it has the necessary expertise in relation to the issues arising in one or more particular areas of illegal content, or in relation to the application and enforcement of terms and conditions of one or more types of online platforms, allowing the body to contribute effectively to the settlement of a dispute;
(c)the dispute settlement is easily accessible through electronic communication technology;
(d)it is capable of settling dispute in a swift, efficient and cost-effective manner and in at least one official language of the Union;
(e)the dispute settlement takes place in accordance with clear and fair rules of procedure.
The Digital Services Coordinator shall, where applicable, specify in the certificate the particular issues to which the body’s expertise relates and the official language or languages of the Union in which the body is capable of settling disputes, as referred to in points (b) and (d) of the first subparagraph, respectively.

3. If the body decides the dispute in favour of the recipient of the service, the online platform shall reimburse the recipient for any fees and other reasonable expenses that the recipient has paid or is to pay in relation to the dispute settlement. If the body decides the dispute in favour of the online platform, the recipient shall not be required to reimburse any fees or other expenses that the online platform paid or is to pay in relation to the dispute settlement.
The fees charged by the body for the dispute settlement shall be reasonable and shall in any event not exceed the costs thereof.
Certified out-of-court dispute settlement bodies shall make the fees, or the mechanisms used to determine the fees, known to the recipient of the services and the online platform concerned before engaging in the dispute settlement.

4. Member States may establish out-of-court dispute settlement bodies for the purposes of paragraph 1 or support the activities of some or all out-of-court dispute settlement bodies that they have certified in accordance with paragraph 2.
Member States shall ensure that any of their activities undertaken under the first subparagraph do not affect the ability of their Digital Services Coordinators to certify the bodies concerned in accordance with paragraph 2.

5. Digital Services Coordinators shall notify to the Commission the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies that they have certified in accordance with paragraph 2, including where applicable the specifications referred to in the second subparagraph of that paragraph. The Commission shall publish a list of those bodies, including those specifications, on a dedicated website, and keep it updated.

6. This Article is without prejudice to Directive 2013/11/EU and alternative dispute resolution procedures and entities for consumers established under that Directive.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a certified out-of-court dispute settlement mechanism for recipients of online platform decisions, requiring platforms to engage in good faith and be bound by the body's decisions.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's text describes existing legal frameworks (the Copyright in the Digital Single Market Directive and the Platform to Business Regulation) and their existing complaint/mediation mechanisms, but does not express a specific advocacy position for or against the creation of a new out-of-court dispute settlement mechanism in the DSA. The text is largely descriptive background context about existing law, with no directional stance on what the Commission should or should not do regarding out-of-court dispute settlement.

pairs.organisation: IFPI - representing the recording industry worldwide
pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3433011 - 30/06/2020 How the Digital Services Act (DSA) Can Help Foster a More Responsible Online Environment June 2020 ABOUT IFPI IFPI represents the interests of the recording industry worldwide. We have some 1,300 members around the world and in all major European markets, ranging from small-to- medium enterprise (SME) to global labels. We work to promote the value of recorded music, to campaign for the rights of record producers and to expand the commercial uses of recorded music in all markets where our members operate. THE ISSUE1 In its announcement of the Digital Services Act (DSA) framework, the European Commission recognizes the need for a safer internet teeming with legal content, and greater accountability of digital services, including and in particular online intermediaries, to help achieve that. We strongly agree. Host providers, access providers, domain registrars and registries, app stores and search engines, for example, are all gatekeepers to or support the distribution of content and should have adequate responsibilities and obligations (“duties of care”) that are essential to tackling illegal content online. They can do better. In recent years, technology has advanced by leaps and bounds, enabling the accomplishment of tasks that would have been unimaginable when the current laws were written
pairs.context_after: They can do better. In recent years, technology has advanced by leaps and bounds, enabling the accomplishment of tasks that would have been unimaginable when the current laws were written. Today, many of these companies are among the world’s most sophisticated and innovative, and well-positioned to stop and prevent the distribution of illegal content. If they fail to fulfill their responsibilities, the risks are significant – the European cultural sectors’ eco-system (in particular when it comes to the creation of new European content) could be harmed, and European economic growth and digital innovation hampered. Europe has made great efforts to update and harmonise copyright across the European Union. However, the measures and procedures to enforce those rights online have not been updated and harmonised. An essential feature of fair and effective online enforcement of rights is the adoption of robust responsibilities and 1 This position paper addresses the key elements of the part of the Commission’s Digital Services Act package concerning responsibilities and obligations of digital services. It does not address issues dealing with potential ex ante rules applicable to large online platforms acting as gatekeepers, or competition aspects. 1
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the investigative and enforcement powers of Digital Services Coordinators, including powers to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text expresses general support for greater accountability of online intermediaries and "duties of care" but does not take any specific directional stance on the investigative or enforcement powers of Digital Services Coordinators. The position is a broad endorsement of the DSA initiative without advocating for or against any specific enforcement mechanism, making it too general to constitute specific alignment with this provision.

pairs.article_title: Competent authorities and Digital Services Coordinators
pairs.article_text:
Article 38 — Competent authorities and Digital Services Coordinators
1. Member States shall designate one or more competent authorities as responsible for the application and enforcement of this Regulation (‘competent authorities’).

2. Member States shall designate one of the competent authorities as their Digital Services Coordinator. The Digital Services Coordinator shall be responsible for all matters relating to application and enforcement of this Regulation in that Member State, unless the Member State concerned has assigned certain specific tasks or sectors to other competent authorities. The Digital Services Coordinator shall in any event be responsible for ensuring coordination at national level in respect of those matters and for contributing to the effective and consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation throughout the Union.
For that purpose, Digital Services Coordinators shall cooperate with each other, other national competent authorities, the Board and the Commission, without prejudice to the possibility for Member States to provide for regular exchanges of views with other authorities where relevant for the performance of the tasks of those other authorities and of the Digital Services Coordinator.
Where a Member State designates more than one competent authority in addition to the Digital Services Coordinator, it shall ensure that the respective tasks of those authorities and of the Digital Services Coordinator are clearly defined and that they cooperate closely and effectively when performing their tasks. The Member State concerned shall communicate the name of the other competent authorities as well as their respective tasks to the Commission and the Board.

3. Member States shall designate the Digital Services Coordinators within two months from the date of entry into force of this Regulation.
Member States shall make publicly available, and communicate to the Commission and the Board, the name of their competent authority designated as Digital Services Coordinator and information on how it can be contacted.

4. The requirements applicable to Digital Services Coordinators set out in Articles 39, 40 and 41 shall also apply to any other competent authorities that the Member States designate pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires Member States to designate one or more competent authorities and a Digital Services Coordinator responsible for the application and enforcement of the DSA Regulation at national level.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's position focuses on advocating for duties of care and greater accountability for online intermediaries (host providers, access providers, app stores, etc.) to tackle illegal content. It does not express any specific stance on the designation of national competent authorities or Digital Services Coordinators, which is what Article 38 establishes. The org text is general background and advocacy on the broader DSA framework, with no directional position on enforcement governance structures.

pairs.organisation: IFPI - representing the recording industry worldwide
pairs.source_text: the 2017 Guidelines on the Enforcement Directive). However, those instruments or voluntary enforcement measures are not sufficient to remedy the problems the recording industry is facing. HOW CAN THE DSA HELP? The DSA framework should include instruments which enhance the accountability of all types of digital services and update their responsibilities and obligations to tackle illegal content online, including copyright infringements, more effectively. We note that this can, and should be achieved by complementing the E-Commerce Directive without opening it: • “Duty of Care”: All digital services, which are being used to infringe copyrights, should be obliged to take reasonable steps to stop, limit and prevent infringements (“duty of care”). The content of those responsibilities and obligations and which steps the digital services including online intermediary service can be reasonably expected to take should depend on the types of services, but all services including smaller ones should be covered. The duty of care should, for instance, include the obligation to stop providing services to illegal websites, and to implement an effective “repeat infringer” policy
pairs.context_before: hosting service providers; Content Delivery Networks (CDNs); search engines; payment providers; domain registrars; app store operators; etc. There is a need to provide for different and truly effective measures and procedures to ensure those digital services play their part in reducing unauthorised uses. In concrete terms this means, for instance, introducing an obligation to ensure accuracy of data that digital services keep about their customers. To date, it is often not possible to pursue direct action against illegal websites because the operators act in complete anonymity, protected by shell companies or domain name privacy services, or both, and the digital services and intermediaries used by these illegal websites do not require their customers to provide proof of their true identity and contact details. The Commission has acknowledged that there is a problem with online enforcement of rights in Europe, and that more should be done to strengthen the current EU enforcement system.2 So far, the Commission has chosen only non-legislative instruments to tackle the problem (e.g. the 2017 Guidelines on the Enforcement Directive). However, those instruments or voluntary enforcement measures are not sufficient to remedy the problems the recording industry is facing
pairs.context_after: The duty of care should, for instance, include the obligation to stop providing services to illegal websites, and to implement an effective “repeat infringer” policy. • “Notice and Stay Down” obligation for passive hosting providers: Online hosting services of a “mere technical, automatic and passive nature”, eligible for liability “safe harbours”, should ensure that illegal content that has been 2 In May 2015 by announcing its Digital Single Market (DSM) Strategy the Commission said that it would make legislative proposals in 2016 “modernising enforcement of intellectual property rights, focusing on commercial-scale infringements (the 'follow the money' approach) as well as its cross- border applicability”. 3
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes investigation and enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators, including the ability to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI advocates for a "duty of care" framework requiring digital services to take reasonable steps to stop copyright infringements, including obligations to cut off services to illegal websites and implement repeat infringer policies. While Article 41 establishes enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators that could theoretically be used to address copyright infringements, the provision focuses on regulatory/supervisory enforcement mechanisms rather than directly imposing duties of care on platforms. It is genuinely unclear whether these enforcement powers satisfy IFPI's specific ask for proactive platform-level obligations, as the provision addresses how regulators can act against infringers rather than mandating the platform-side accountability measures IFPI sought.

pairs.organisation: IFPI - representing the recording industry worldwide
pairs.source_text: • Complement Responsibilities and Obligations without changing the scope of, and eligibility for, “safe harbours”: The definitions and the types of activities and online intermediary services eligible for the safe harbours should remain unchanged. The EU acquis should be consolidated. In particular, the current distinction used in the E-Commerce Directive between “active” and “passive” online intermediaries should not be changed by the DSA to avoid altering the careful balance in the E-Commerce Directive between the need to protect content owners and the need to enable the development of the Internet. For instance, re-defining the criteria for an “active” intermediary in a narrower manner in the DSA, could extend safe harbour privileges to services that were not meant to be entitled to them under the E-Commerce Directive. Amending the scope of the safe harbours would undermine the EU and national jurisprudence (e.g. CJEU decision in L’Oréal v eBay, Google France3) and the recent legislative developments (e.g. the DSM Directive4), which have helpfully clarified the eligibility criteria for safe harbour protection. The DSA should not 3 C-324/09 - L'Oréal and Others; C-236/08 - Google France and Google. 4 Directive (EU) 2019/790 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 April 2019 on copyright and related rights in the Digital Single Market. 4
pairs.context_before: removed does not reappear on their services. We do not however see the need to further regulate the procedural rules regarding notice and take down, such as the form of notices or timeframes for take downs, for the removal of content. They work generally well in the case of our industry, providing flexibility for the different types of services and content. • Improved Transparency Obligations: The transparency requirements under the current EU legislation should be improved, notably by requiring digital services to implement effective Know Your Business Customer (KYBC) policies and by introducing an explicit legal basis for a public WHOIS registry which should, at a very minimum, contain all data already required to be made publicly available under existing law (Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive), as well as such other information that is necessary for right holders to pursue legal actions to protect their copyrights. • Adjusting Copyright Enforcement Measures to the Digital Single Market: Furthermore, because copyright infringements are borderless, the DSA should help right holders in protecting their rights effectively across the EU; without the need to bring actions in each individual Member State against the same infringers, the same content, and the same infringing activity, be it in relation to infringing services directly or against digital services including online intermediaries
pairs.context_after: weaken those criteria but complement and strengthen the responsibilities and obligations of digital services operating in the digital markets. We detail our comments on the above-mentioned elements further in this paper. 1. MORE ROBUST RESPONSIBILITIES AND OBLIGATIONS FOR DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES (“DUTY OF CARE”) A. DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES MUST DO MORE The Issue: Illegal online services use and rely on a variety of digital services, including internet access providers, hosting service providers, Content Delivery Network (CDN) providers, search engines, payment providers, domain registrars, app store operators, operating systems and web browsers, among others. There have been some examples of voluntary cooperation between right holders and intermediaries or other digital services.5 However, such voluntary agreements are no substitutes for a robust legal framework, setting out clear responsibilities and obligations. Experience shows that voluntary agreements are more likely to be negotiated and work, where they provide for a way for digital services to comply with their statutory obligations. The Proposed Solution: EU legislation should establish obligations on all digital services including online intermediaries to take reasonable steps to stop and prevent online copyright infringements
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the subject matter and scope of the Digital Services Act, including a framework for conditional liability exemptions for intermediary services, while explicitly preserving existing EU law including Union law on copyright and related rights (Article 1(5)(c)) and Directive 2000/31/EC (the E-Commerce Directive).
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocated that the DSA should not alter the scope of or eligibility for safe harbours established under the E-Commerce Directive, and that Union copyright law (including the DSM Directive) should not be undermined. Article 1(5) directly responds to this by stating the Regulation is "without prejudice" to both Directive 2000/31/EC and "Union law on copyright and related rights," preserving the existing safe harbour framework and copyright acquis exactly as IFPI requested.

For instance, re-defining the criteria for an “active” intermediary in a narrower manner in the DSA, could extend safe harbour privileges to services that were not meant to be entitled to them under the E-Commerce Directive. Amending the scope of the safe harbours would undermine the EU and national jurisprudence (e.g. CJEU decision in L’Oréal v eBay, Google France3) and the recent legislative developments (e.g. the DSM Directive4), which have helpfully clarified the eligibility criteria for safe harbour protection. The DSA should not 3 C-324/09 - L'Oréal and Others; C-236/08 - Google France and Google. 4 Directive (EU) 2019/790 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 April 2019 on copyright and related rights in the Digital Single Market. 4

The responsibilities and obligations should apply broadly and should extend to all digital services, including online intermediaries, providing services to businesses or consumers residing in the EU. The nature of the obligations and responsibilities, the triggering factors and which steps the online intermediary can be reasonably expected to take, depends on the circumstances and the types of services offered. They should, for example, include an obligation to implement an effective “repeat infringer policy” to avoid that the same account holder or website operator can 5 For example, there are Memorandums of Understanding or Code of Conducts in place in some countries between the advertising industry and right holders, or, in Denmark and Portugal, voluntary agreements between right holders and internet access providers complement existing court and administrative procedures. 5
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes investigation and enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators, including the ability to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocates for EU legislation to establish robust obligations and enforcement mechanisms requiring digital services/online intermediaries to stop and prevent online copyright infringements, arguing voluntary agreements are insufficient. Article 41 directly responds to this by granting Digital Services Coordinators strong investigative and enforcement powers — including ordering cessation of infringements, imposing fines, and restricting access — which constitute the kind of robust legal framework with real enforcement teeth that IFPI called for.

The responsibilities and obligations should apply broadly and should extend to all digital services, including online intermediaries, providing services to businesses or consumers residing in the EU. The nature of the obligations and responsibilities, the triggering factors and which steps the online intermediary can be reasonably expected to take, depends on the circumstances and the types of services offered. They should, for example, include an obligation to implement an effective “repeat infringer policy” to avoid that the same account holder or website operator can 5 For example, there are Memorandums of Understanding or Code of Conducts in place in some countries between the advertising industry and right holders, or, in Denmark and Portugal, voluntary agreements between right holders and internet access providers complement existing court and administrative procedures. 5
pairs.context_before: weaken those criteria but complement and strengthen the responsibilities and obligations of digital services operating in the digital markets. We detail our comments on the above-mentioned elements further in this paper. 1. MORE ROBUST RESPONSIBILITIES AND OBLIGATIONS FOR DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES (“DUTY OF CARE”) A. DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES MUST DO MORE The Issue: Illegal online services use and rely on a variety of digital services, including internet access providers, hosting service providers, Content Delivery Network (CDN) providers, search engines, payment providers, domain registrars, app store operators, operating systems and web browsers, among others. There have been some examples of voluntary cooperation between right holders and intermediaries or other digital services.5 However, such voluntary agreements are no substitutes for a robust legal framework, setting out clear responsibilities and obligations. Experience shows that voluntary agreements are more likely to be negotiated and work, where they provide for a way for digital services to comply with their statutory obligations. The Proposed Solution: EU legislation should establish obligations on all digital services including online intermediaries to take reasonable steps to stop and prevent online copyright infringements
pairs.context_after: continue to infringe copyright in spite of the fact that the service provider knows or is aware of the account to be found to be infringing, e.g. following a notice from the right holders. Thus, once the service provider knows or is aware of repeat infringements coming from the same account or customer, effective actions need to be taken, e.g. temporary suspension of accounts. Digital services should also stop enabling or facilitating the operation of illegal websites by providing them services or means to operate. Once a digital service has actual knowledge, or has become aware of facts or circumstances of which it becomes apparent, that a website to which it provides services infringes intellectual property rights, it should cease providing services to that website for as long as the illegal activity continues. This may require digital services to undertake reasonable checks to investigate the nature of their customers’ business to ensure that unauthorised activities are not being supported. For example, if a website owner wishes to register a website, the name of which raises clear red flags (e.g. www.leakedmusicfor free.com), the domain register should inquire with the registrant about the purpose of the website. Similarly, given that online piracy occurs very often via mobile devices involving mobile apps, app store operators should take a more proactive approach and remove apps which facilitate online copyright infringements
pairs.article_text:
Article 43 — Right to lodge a complaint
1. Recipients of the service shall have the right to lodge a complaint against providers of intermediary services alleging an infringement of this Regulation with the Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State where the recipient resides or is established. The Digital Services Coordinator shall assess the complaint and, where appropriate, transmit it to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment. Where the complaint falls under the responsibility of another competent authority in its Member State, the Digital Service Coordinator receiving the complaint shall transmit it to that authority.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the right for recipients of intermediary services to lodge complaints about infringements of the DSA Regulation with the relevant Digital Services Coordinator.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI's position specifically advocates for obligations on digital intermediaries to prevent online copyright infringements, including repeat infringer policies — a substantive content moderation/IP enforcement ask. Article 43 establishes a procedural complaint mechanism for DSA regulatory infringements by service recipients, which is unrelated to copyright enforcement obligations. There is no substantive connection between IFPI's advocacy and this provision's subject matter.

pairs.article_text:
Article 55 — Interim measures
1. In the context of proceedings which may lead to the adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 58(1), where there is an urgency due to the risk of serious damage for the recipients of the service, the Commission may, by decision, order interim measures against the very large online platform concerned on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement.

2. A decision under paragraph 1 shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
pairs.provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to order interim measures against very large online platforms on the basis of a prima facie finding of infringement where there is urgency due to risk of serious damage to recipients of the service.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI's position advocates for obligations on digital services/online intermediaries to prevent copyright infringement, including repeat infringer policies. The provision concerns the Commission's power to impose interim measures against very large online platforms in the context of DSA compliance proceedings — it is not about copyright infringement obligations at all. The subjects are substantively unrelated, and IFPI's text contains no specific stance on interim enforcement measures by the Commission.

pairs.organisation: IFPI - representing the recording industry worldwide
pairs.source_text: Directive, e.g. the true and verified name, physical address, email address and telephone number of their customers. In case of legal entities, the information should also include up-to-date information of the company directors or other responsible persons. Depending on the type of service, it could also include bank account details. In addition to the initial verification of the details provided, digital services should apply regular checks to ensure that the details remain accurate and up to date. This obligation should also apply to any reseller of the original digital service. There should be sanctions for the failure to comply with the obligation. Further, digital services should terminate services to websites or online services where the information these services have provided turns out to be incorrect (regardless of the KYBC process followed), or where the services do not comply with the transparency obligations, e.g. if a website does not publish information required under of the E- Commerce Directive (see below). II. BETTER ENFORCEMENT OF NON-COMPLIANCE WITH OF THE E-COMMERCE DIRECTIVE EU legislation on transparency should be complemented by better enforcement of non-compliance with of the E-Commerce Directive at national level
pairs.context_before: This seriously undermines online enforcement efforts, including attempts to contact or bring actions against the suspected infringers. The Proposed Solutions: To improve transparency, the DSA should include the following elements: I. “KNOW YOUR BUSINESS CUSTOMER” POLICIES Legislation should require that digital services including online intermediaries implement effective Know Your Business Customer (KYBC) policies, i.e. intermediaries need to ensure they verify the identity of their business customers and have accurate and meaningful information about their customers. At a minimum, such information should include the information that services are required to provide under Article 5 of the E-Commerce 8 See Directive 2005/60/EC of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing. The relevant “Know your customer” obligations have been implemented across the EU, e.g.: Art. 29 of the UK Regulation of 2017 on Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds; section 11 et seq. of the German Money Laundering Act. 8
pairs.context_after: Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive requires all EU-based service providers to “render easily, directly and permanently accessible to” EU consumers and authorities at least the name, geographical address, e-mail address and other information depending on the business model. In IFPI’s experience this obligation is rarely being complied with, in particular by illegal services. There are a number of reasons for this, including that in most Member States there is no effective enforcement and no deterrent consequences when a service is found to be in breach of this obligation. Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive should therefore be complemented to ensure that it applies to all services that are being provided to EU citizens, regardless of the physical location of the business, and there should be deterrent sanctions in case of non- compliance. In addition, digital services should not provide services to websites that do not comply with Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive (see above). 9
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to collect, verify, and publish trader identity information (name, address, contact details, bank account, trade register, and self-certification) before allowing traders to offer products/services to EU consumers, and to suspend services to traders who fail to provide accurate information.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocated for digital services to collect verified identity information (name, physical address, email, telephone, bank account details, and for legal entities, director information), to verify and regularly check that information, and to terminate services where information is incorrect — all of which the provision directly delivers through its trader traceability requirements, verification obligations, and suspension mechanism for non-compliant traders.

pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3433129 - 30/06/2020 Digital Services Act - deepening the internal market and clarifying responsibilities for digital services Inception Impact Assessment Comments by Mediaset S.p.A. The current horizontal framework governing the internet has been adopted 20 years ago. It has allowed the entrance of new players in the European Union which offered innovative services to European consumers. Although these new players have supported the exchange of information and the sharing of opinions and content online, they have also facilitated the spread of illegal and harmful content in the digital space. The legal framework provided in the e-Commerce Directive is based on a simple distinction: while active players must be held responsible for the content present on their services, passive intermediaries – i.e. mere conduit, caching and hosting services – shouldn’t be liable, insofar as they have no control over the content they host or transmit. Online platforms optimising UGC and UUC content by organising it, embedding advertising and monitoring users’ behaviour have stretched this definition claiming they should also benefit from the liability exemption envisaged twenty years ago for merely passive services. The Court of Justice of the European Union has, in several circumstances1, countered this argument and clarified the active/passive distinction adapting it to the technological developments risen over the years
pairs.context_after: Platforms that optimise, index and promote UGC content on their services, to enhance the users’ experience and to monetise UGC content, can’t benefit from the liability exemption provided in Articles 12 to 15 of the e-Commerce Directive (i.e. safe harbor provisions). National Courts have also issued helpful clarifications on the active/passive distinction. In a landmark case2 in 2019, the Italian Supreme Court provided a comprehensive list of practices from which the active nature of the service must be drawn. In its analysis the Italian judges didn’t only focus on the interaction of the platform with the content it stores, but also on how it tracks users on its service. The Court concluded that the active nature of the service could be deducted not only from the filtering, indexing and optimisation of the content, but also from the adoption of behavioural evaluation techniques to increase the users’ loyalty to the service. As the economic gains of these business models derive from both activities – optimising/promoting UGC content and tracking the users – both of these activities should be considered, in a non-cumulative way, to establish the active nature of the service
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes definitions for key terms used in the Digital Services Act regulation, including 'intermediary service', 'online platform', 'illegal content', 'hosting service', and related concepts.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: Mediaset specifically advocates for a clearer active/passive distinction for online platforms, arguing that platforms optimising and monetising user content should not benefit from liability exemptions designed for passive intermediaries. The provision defines 'intermediary service' and 'online platform' but does so in largely neutral/descriptive terms without explicitly codifying the active/passive distinction Mediaset calls for. It is genuinely unclear whether these definitions satisfy, contradict, or ignore Mediaset's specific ask, as the definitions neither explicitly extend nor restrict liability exemptions based on the active/passive distinction.

pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3433310 - 30/06/2020 30 June 2020 Feedback on the Inception Impact Assessment for a Digital Services Act: Deepening the internal market and clarifying responsibilities for digital services eBay welcomes the Commission's intention to adopt a Digital Services Act that would deepen the internal market and clarify responsibilities for digital services The e-Commerce Directive (ECD) was adopted in 2000 to set-up the basic legal framework for information society services: it lays down requirements for the establishment of information society services across the EU, introduces a limited liability framework for hosting providers and specifies that Member States cannot impose a general obligation to monitor the information transmitted or stored by information society services. These principles provide the backbone for a dynamic European digital single market. The Directive has proven highly effective in removing cross-border obstacles for the provision of digital services and achieving an internal market conducive to innovation. The Directive recognizes the opportunities e-commerce provides for consumers and businesses, especially SMEs, and it continues to play an essential role for the growth and continued evolution of the European online economy. For example, 98% of small businesses in the EU using eBay engage in exports. On average these businesses reached 21 different countries in 2018, compared to 4 markets for traditional businesses
pairs.context_after: For example, 98% of small businesses in the EU using eBay engage in exports. On average these businesses reached 21 different countries in 2018, compared to 4 markets for traditional businesses. In addition, approximately 40% of eBay-enabled small businesses export to more than 4 continents. eBay welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the plan to reinforce and clarify certain aspects already established by the ECD: 1. Addressing legal and regulatory fragmentation The Digital Services Act provides an opportunity to reinforce the main aspects of the ECD by codifying the resulting European Court of Justice (ECJ) case law deriving from the Directive. We have observed significant fragmentation between the EU national courts regarding their interpretation of the ECD. This legal uncertainty is highly detrimental to the functioning and development of information society services. It also tends to encourage parties to bring legal action even in situations of perfectly legitimate activity. This is costly to society and detrimental to innovation, entrepreneurship and job creation. eBay supports a harmonized horizontal EU framework for the regulation of illegal content online, in line with the principles of the digital single market, which should aim to establish uniform rules for the provision of digital services
pairs.article_title: Amendments to Directive 2020/XX/EC on Representative Actions for the Protection of the Collective Interests of Consumers
pairs.article_text:
Article 72 — Amendments to Directive 2020/XX/EC on Representative Actions for the Protection of the Collective Interests of Consumers
3. The following is added to Annex I:
“(X) Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council on a Single Market for Digital Services (Digital Services Act) and amending Directive 2000/31/EC”
pairs.provision_effect: Adds the Digital Services Act to the list of EU laws covered by the Representative Actions Directive, enabling consumer organisations to bring collective redress actions for DSA violations.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: eBay's submission is a general welcome of the DSA initiative and background description of the e-Commerce Directive's benefits, with no specific advocacy position on collective redress, representative actions, or the scope of the Representative Actions Directive. The org text contains no directional stance that could be evaluated against this specific provision.

pairs.source_text: liability of hosting providers on user-generated content, and the prohibition of monitoring obligations for the same instance. Unfortunately, national courts have largely ignored that case law by ordering far-reaching injunctions against online intermediaries. Additionally, we welcome the Commission's intention to reinforce cross-border cooperation between national authorities with powers in the field of content moderation. In fact, it is often the case that national regulatory structures are not sufficiently equipped to deal with the new challenges of the platform economy. For example, we find that market surveillance authorities have been historically underfunded, limiting their capacity to monitor and test the safety of products, undermining the single market for goods and the European economy, as compliance with standards and requirements is not efficiently monitored. In addition, their efficiency has further been limited by national boundaries in a world where the Internet has made trade increasingly borderless. Coordination between authorities has significant opportunity for further improvement and efforts are required to better enable intermediary service providers to identify the safety of products. 2. Introducing a viable and flexible responsibility framework It is in the interest of online platforms to ensure illegal content is not found on their website in order to achieve a safe and secure environment, trusted by users
pairs.context_before: Hosting providers, including pure third-party marketplaces, such as eBay, typically have a limited degree of knowledge about the content or data shared through their service by users. We would welcome clearer and more flexible notice and takedown procedures, as well as a clarification of several concepts in the e-Commerce Directive, including the concepts of ‘actual knowledge’ and ‘manifestly illegal content’, as related to liability exemptions. The ECJ has confirmed in a number of landmark rulings the general principle of protection against 1
pairs.context_after: Online platforms have adopted content moderation practices based on platform-specific aspects such as the type of content that is being moderated (i.e. product listings vs. hate speech), own rules and policies, internal handling processes, investment in resources, etc. Considering that such aspects vary from intermediary to intermediary, it is necessary for the good functioning of content moderation processes to allow platforms to moderate content in a platform-specific manner. This calls for minimum requirements, rather than overly prescriptive “one-size-fits-all” obligations. Although the e-Commerce Directive establishes a limited liability regime for online platforms, its principles have never stood in the way of intermediaries taking far- reaching voluntary action to address illegal content (a recent example can be seen in the platforms’ strong and efficient response to Covid-19 related scams). Voluntary and proactive actions should be covered by the liability exemption and no general monitoring obligations should be imposed in the context of content moderation. This allows platforms like eBay to identify new trends regarding illegal content, and adapt our policies accordingly without incurring the risk of becoming directly liable for such third-party content
pairs.provision_effect: Requires the Commission to support and promote the development of voluntary industry standards for electronic submissions, APIs, auditing, interoperability of ad repositories, and data transmission between advertising intermediaries.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's text focuses on liability frameworks for hosting providers, monitoring obligations, cross-border cooperation between national authorities, and market surveillance capacity — none of which relate to the voluntary technical standardisation activities covered by Article 34. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that connects to the development of standards for APIs, ad repositories, or data transmission obligations.

Internet service providers should not be subject to general monitoring obligations. 4. Actual knowledge should be triggered when an online service provider receives specific and clearly identifiable information, meaning a notification that satisfies the following conditions: • the complainant should be clearly identified and in a legitimate position to send a notification (e.g. the owner of IP rights on the item, or a market surveillance authority for unsafe products); • the notification should be made in writing and the complainant should provide adequate information of the specific material alleged to be infringing (e.g. link to the listing); • it should be sent to an email address or other secure method reserved for this purpose by the service provider; • it should clearly specify which information or activity the complaint relates to; • it should include details, including legal ground to demonstrate the unlawful nature of the activity or content in question. 5. Measures to take down content found to be infringing should be fair and equitable and should not be unnecessarily complicated or costly, or entail unreasonable time-limits or unwarranted delays. 6

In other words, this setup provides the legal certainty required to continue and enhance efforts towards ensuring that our marketplace is safe for all users, without leading to over- blocking to avoid liability for goods and services over which we do not have any direct control (as opposed to retail platforms). Internet service providers are not liable for the information stored at the request of a recipient of the service, if it does not have ‘actual knowledge’ of illegal activity or information and upon obtaining such knowledge, acts expeditiously to remove or to disable access to the information. 2

Measures to take down content found to be infringing should be fair and equitable and should not be unnecessarily complicated or costly, or entail unreasonable time-limits or unwarranted delays. 6. Counter-notice mechanisms should be included in the DSA to respect the rights of content providers and allow them the possibility to give their views to the hosting service provider on the alleged illegality of their content. The fact that online platforms are not in a position to decide on the legality or illegality of content when facts are disputed should be taken into consideration. Content should be removed after being subject to an injunction, in which a Court confirms its illegality. 3. Introducing harmonized content moderation procedures Marketplaces have a longstanding record of cooperating with regulators, rights owners and other stakeholders in order to proactively address illegal content and provide for a safe environment for customers to trade in. Those efforts are constantly updated and are promoted by a regulatory framework that protects service providers against undue liability for third party content and encourages them to step in where they are best placed to do so in order to prevent abuse and illegal activity. pairs.article_text:
Article 14 — Notice and action mechanisms
1. Providers of hosting services shall put mechanisms in place to allow any individual or entity to notify them of the presence on their service of specific items of information that the individual or entity considers to be illegal content. Those mechanisms shall be easy to access, user-friendly, and allow for the submission of notices exclusively by electronic means.

2. The mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1 shall be such as to facilitate the submission of sufficiently precise and adequately substantiated notices, on the basis of which a diligent economic operator can identify the illegality of the content in question. To that end, the providers shall take the necessary measures to enable and facilitate the submission of notices containing all of the following elements:
(a)an explanation of the reasons why the individual or entity considers the information in question to be illegal content;
(b)a clear indication of the electronic location of that information, in particular the exact URL or URLs, and, where necessary, additional information enabling the identification of the illegal content;
(c)the name and an electronic mail address of the individual or entity submitting the notice, except in the case of information considered to involve one of the offences referred to in Articles 3 to 7 of Directive 2011/93/EU;
(d)a statement confirming the good faith belief of the individual or entity submitting the notice that the information and allegations contained therein are accurate and complete.

3. Notices that include the elements referred to in paragraph 2 shall be considered to give rise to actual knowledge or awareness for the purposes of Article 5 in respect of the specific item of information concerned.

4. Where the notice contains the name and an electronic mail address of the individual or entity that submitted it, the provider of hosting services shall promptly send a confirmation of receipt of the notice to that individual or entity.

5. The provider shall also, without undue delay, notify that individual or entity of its decision in respect of the information to which the notice relates, providing information on the redress possibilities in respect of that decision.

6. Providers of hosting services shall process any notices that they receive under the mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1, and take their decisions in respect of the information to which the notices relate, in a timely, diligent and objective manner. Where they use automated means for that processing or decision-making, they shall include information on such use in the notification referred to in paragraph 4.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires hosting service providers to implement notice mechanisms for reporting illegal content, specifying that valid notices must include the notifier's identity, precise location of content, reasons for illegality, and a good-faith statement, with such notices triggering 'actual knowledge' under Article 5.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: eBay explicitly advocated that 'actual knowledge' should be triggered only by specific, clearly identifiable notifications meeting defined conditions (identified complainant, written notice with adequate information, specific content location, legal grounds for unlawfulness) — which closely mirrors the provision's requirements in Article 14(2)(a)-(d) and the 'actual knowledge' trigger in Article 14(3). The provision delivers substantially what eBay asked for in terms of the conditions that must be met before a notice gives rise to actual knowledge.
