pairs.article_title: Enhanced supervision for very large online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 50 — Enhanced supervision for very large online platforms
1. Where the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment adopts a decision finding that a very large online platform has infringed any of the provisions of Section 4 of Chapter III, it shall make use of the enhanced supervision system laid down in this Article. It shall take utmost account of any opinion and recommendation of the Commission and the Board pursuant to this Article.
The Commission acting on its own initiative, or the Board acting on its own initiative or upon request of at least three Digital Services Coordinators of destination, may, where it has reasons to suspect that a very large online platform infringed any of those provisions, recommend the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment to investigate the suspected infringement with a view to that Digital Services Coordinator adopting such a decision within a reasonable time period.

2. When communicating the decision referred to in the first subparagraph of paragraph 1 to the very large online platform concerned, the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall request it to draw up and communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, the Commission and the Board, within one month from that decision, an action plan, specifying how that platform intends to terminate or remedy the infringement. The measures set out in the action plan may include, where appropriate, participation in a code of conduct as provided for in Article 35.

3. Within one month following receipt of the action plan, the Board shall communicate its opinion on the action plan to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment. Within one month following receipt of that opinion, that Digital Services Coordinator shall decide whether the action plan is appropriate to terminate or remedy the infringement.
Where the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment has concerns on the ability of the measures to terminate or remedy the infringement, it may request the very large online platform concerned to subject itself to an additional, independent audit to assess the effectiveness of those measures in terminating or remedying the infringement. In that case, that platform shall send the audit report to that Digital Services Coordinator, the Commission and the Board within four months from the decision referred to in the first subparagraph. When requesting such an additional audit, the Digital Services Coordinator may specify a particular audit organisation that is to carry out the audit, at the expense of the platform concerned, selected on the basis of criteria set out in Article 28(2).

4. The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall communicate to the Commission, the Board and the very large online platform concerned its views as to whether the very large online platform has terminated or remedied the infringement and the reasons thereof. It shall do so within the following time periods, as applicable:
(a)within one month from the receipt of the audit report referred to in the second subparagraph of paragraph 3, where such an audit was performed;
(b)within three months from the decision on the action plan referred to in the first subparagraph of paragraph 3, where no such audit was performed;
(c)immediately upon the expiry of the time period set out in paragraph 2, where that platform failed to communicate the action plan within that time period.
Pursuant to that communication, the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall no longer be entitled to take any investigatory or enforcement measures in respect of the relevant conduct by the very large online platform concerned, without prejudice to Article 66 or any other measures that it may take at the request of the Commission.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes an enhanced supervision system for very large online platforms found to have infringed specific provisions, involving action plans, independent audits, and coordinated oversight between the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, the Commission, and the Board.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The org advocates for a centralised EU-level Taskforce (within the Commission) to take enforcement decisions regarding large online platforms under a proposed Ex Ante Regulation, emphasising the benefits of centralised enforcement to avoid inconsistencies. The provision establishes an enhanced supervision mechanism that involves the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment as the primary enforcer, with the Commission and Board playing advisory/oversight roles — a more decentralised model than what the org proposed. There is a genuine connection (both concern enforcement mechanisms for large online platforms), but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply diverges from the org's specific ask for a centralised EU Taskforce, making the direction ambiguous.

pairs.article_text:
Article 19 — Trusted flaggers
1. Online platforms shall take the necessary technical and organisational measures to ensure that notices submitted by trusted flaggers through the mechanisms referred to in Article 14, are processed and decided upon with priority and without delay.

2. The status of trusted flaggers under this Regulation shall be awarded, upon application by any entities, by the Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State in which the applicant is established, where the applicant has demonstrated to meet all of the following conditions:
(a)it has particular expertise and competence for the purposes of detecting, identifying and notifying illegal content;
(b)it represents collective interests and is independent from any online platform;
(c) it carries out its activities for the purposes of submitting notices in a timely, diligent and objective manner.

3. Digital Services Coordinators shall communicate to the Commission and the Board the names, addresses and electronic mail addresses of the entities to which they have awarded the status of the trusted flagger in accordance with paragraph 2.

4. The Commission shall publish the information referred to in paragraph 3 in a publicly available database and keep the database updated.

5. Where an online platform has information indicating that a trusted flagger submitted a significant number of insufficiently precise or inadequately substantiated notices through the mechanisms referred to in Article 14, including information gathered in connection to the processing of complaints through the internal complaint-handling systems referred to in Article 17(3), it shall communicate that information to the Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to the entity concerned, providing the necessary explanations and supporting documents.

6. The Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to an entity shall revoke that status if it determines, following an investigation either on its own initiative or on the basis information received by third parties, including the information provided by an online platform pursuant to paragraph 5, that the entity no longer meets the conditions set out in paragraph 2. Before revoking that status, the Digital Services Coordinator shall afford the entity an opportunity to react to the findings of its investigation and its intention to revoke the entity’s status as trusted flagger

7. The Commission, after consulting the Board, may issue guidance to assist online platforms and Digital Services Coordinators in the application of paragraphs 5 and 6.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a trusted flagger system whereby designated entities receive priority processing of illegal content notices from online platforms, with conditions for awarding and revoking trusted flagger status managed by Digital Services Coordinators.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's text focuses exclusively on advocating for an EU-level enforcement Taskforce for large online platforms under an Ex Ante Regulation and a New Competition Tool, with no substantive position on a trusted flagger mechanism or notice-and-action systems. The subjects are unrelated, making this noise.

pairs.organisation: Federation of German Consumer Organisations (Verbraucherzentrale Bundesverband - vzbv)
pairs.source_text: Verbraucherzentrale Bundesverband e.V. 12 l 14 Ex Ante Regulatory Instrument for Large Online Platforms and a New Competition Tool It would be essential for the Taskforce to be vested with appropriate legal powers (e.g. for carrying out investigation and enforcement) and endowed with sufficient financial, human and technical resources to carry out this new task. V. THE NEW COMPETITION TOOL DG Competition should be endowed with the legal powers and tools to deal with structural competition issues. Among others, these must include carrying out market investigations and impose appropriate remedies, even in the absence of a dominant single player. Therefore, vzbv proposes a market structure-based NCT with a horizontal scope. It should allow the European Commission to impose behavioral and, where appropriate, structural remedies to improve the functioning of markets to the benefit of consumers and other market actors, independently of the finding of an infringement of or 102 TFEU. 1. SCOPE OF THE NEW COMPETITION TOOL vzbv holds that the scope of the NCT should be rather broad in order to fulfil the policy goals of the two initiatives. 1.1 Horizontal Approach more suitable than limited Scope The European Commission should design the NCT with a horizontal approach instead of a limited scope. Although today many of the severe limitations of competition rules and enforcement are found in digital markets, limiting the NCT’s scope would not seem appropriate
pairs.context_before: ___________________________________________________________________________________________ 2 e.g Directorate-General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology (DG CONNECT), Directorate-General for Internal Market, Industry, Entrepreneurship and SMEs (DG GROW), the Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers (DG JUST)
pairs.context_after: Although today many of the severe limitations of competition rules and enforcement are found in digital markets, limiting the NCT’s scope would not seem appropriate. The main reason is that the lines between digital and “traditional” non-digital markets are blurring and it can’t be predicted which sectors will raise structural concerns in the future. Also, the power of competition law results from its universality: It is applicable to all sectors and areas of economic life. That principle must not be given up. Adopting the NCT with a horizontal scope does not mean it must be applied in all sectors at present but it would make the NCT future proof if fair competition in further markets would be undermined by the structural problems the NCT aims to address. 1.2 Market Structure-based Approach more suitable than Dominance-based Approach The current European competition legislation does not allow for addressing some specific new structural challenges to competition. These include monopolisation strategies by non-dominant platforms with market power or parallel leveraging strategies by platform-players into multiple adjacent markets. If a significant part of a digital market is controlled by a strong platform, this must not necessarily be a “classic” dominant firm. The control of ecosystems or of central strategic positions in markets (or “interfaces” between markets) allows non-dominant players to control suppliers’ access to significant segments of consumers and vice versa
pairs.article_text:
Article 58 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision where it finds that the very large online platform concerned does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)the relevant provisions of this Regulation;
(b)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 55;
(c)commitments made binding pursuant to Article 56,

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the very large online platform concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures that it considers taking, or that it considers that the very large online platform concerned should take, in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1 the Commission shall order the very large online platform concerned to take the necessary measures to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 within a reasonable time period and to provide information on the measures that that platform intends to take to comply with the decision.

4. The very large online platform concerned shall provide the Commission with a description of the measures it has taken to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 upon their implementation.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a non-compliance decision mechanism whereby the Commission can formally find and order very large online platforms to remedy violations of the regulation, interim measures, or binding commitments.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The org advocates for the Commission to be endowed with strong legal powers for investigation and enforcement against large online platforms, including the ability to impose behavioural and structural remedies. Article 58 does establish a formal non-compliance and enforcement mechanism, which is directionally consistent with the org's call for robust enforcement powers. However, the org's position focuses specifically on a New Competition Tool (NCT) with a market-structure/competition law framing and the ability to impose remedies even absent a finding of dominance — which is a distinct instrument from the DSA-style non-compliance mechanism in Article 58. It is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, partially satisfies, or is simply a different instrument from what the org was advocating for.

pairs.source_text: of the Directive explains it very clearly: « The exemptions from liability established in this Directive cover only cases where the activity of the information society service provider is limited to the technical process of operating and giving access to a communication network over which information made available by third parties is transmitted or temporarily stored, for the sole purpose of making the transmission more efficient; this activity is of a mere technical, automatic and passive nature, which implies that the information society service provider has neither knowledge of nor control over the information which is transmitted or stored. » In a nutshell, if online marketplaces are passive, they are exempt from being liable regarding the third-party information they host. Conversely, if it appears that online marketplaces have taken an active role in the handling of the information provided by the third-party seller, then they become liable for it. This interpretation was confirmed by the European Court of Justice in Case C-324/09 (L’Oréal vs. eBay)1, as it ruled that: 1 Case C-324/09, paragraphs 116 and 123 P. 2
pairs.context_before: • In 2019, Which?, (the British consumer association) reported in a study that 66% of the 250 products bought from online marketplaces AliExpress, Amazon Marketplace, eBay and Wish have failed safety tests. But how did we come to this? The liability regime of online platforms is governed by the 2000 e-Commerce Directive and in particular by article 14 (“Hosting”). Article 14 (“Hosting”): Article 14 (1) provides that “where an information society service is provided that consists of the storage of information provided by a recipient of the service, Member States shall ensure that the service provider (e.g. online market place) is not liable for the information stored at the request of a recipient of the service, on condition that: • It does not have actual knowledge of illegal activity or information and, as regards claims for damages, is not aware of facts or circumstances from which the illegal activity or information is apparent; • Or upon obtaining such knowledge or awareness, it acts expeditiously to remove or to disable access to the information. This means that when an online marketplace limits its activities to hosting information provided by a third party (storage of information), it is not liable for this information, on the condition that it is not aware of illegal activity or that if aware it must act to remove it or prevent access to it
pairs.context_after: « Where the operator (online market place) has provided assistance which entails, in particular, optimising the presentation of the offers for sale in question or promoting those offers, it must be considered not to have taken a neutral position between the customer-seller concerned and potential buyers but to have played an active role of such a kind as to give it knowledge of, or control over, the data relating to those offers for sale. It cannot then rely, in the case of those data, on the exemption from liability referred to in Article 14(1) of Directive 2000/31. » (paragraph 116) And that: « Article 14(1) of Directive 2000/31 applies to the operator of an online marketplace where that operator has not played an active role allowing it to have knowledge or control of the data stored. The operator plays such a role when it provides assistance which entails, in particular, optimising the presentation of the offers for sale in question or promoting them
pairs.article_text:
Article 3 — ‘Mere conduit
1. Where an information society service is provided that consists of the transmission in a communication network of information provided by a recipient of the service, or the provision of access to a communication network, the service provider shall not be liable for the information transmitted, on condition that the provider:
(a)does not initiate the transmission;
(b)does not select the receiver of the transmission; and
(c)does not select or modify the information contained in the transmission.

2. The acts of transmission and of provision of access referred to in paragraph 1 include the automatic, intermediate and transient storage of the information transmitted in so far as this takes place for the sole purpose of carrying out the transmission in the communication network, and provided that the information is not stored for any period longer than is reasonably necessary for the transmission.

3. This Article shall not affect the possibility for a court or administrative authority, in accordance with Member States' legal systems, of requiring the service provider to terminate or prevent an infringement.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes that service providers acting as 'mere conduits' (transmitting information without initiating, selecting receiver, or modifying content) are not liable for that information.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is descriptive and explanatory in nature — it summarises existing law (the Directive's recital and ECJ case law) regarding the passive/active distinction for liability exemptions, without making a specific directional advocacy ask about what the provision should do or change. This constitutes background description of existing legal framework rather than a substantive lobbying position on the 'mere conduit' article itself.

pairs.source_text: Which texts should be amended to change this? Firstly, it should be stressed that non-binding rules or voluntary commitments have virtually no effect on the the endemic problem of non compliance/product safety/illegal content on online platforms. The Product Safety Pledge11 is a blatant example of this. Aside from its voluntary nature, the Product Safety Pledge was signed up by only six online marketplaces while there are thousands of them. In addition, the methodology and review12 of the commitments do not live up to expectations. The implementation report of the Pledge is a simple document that quote figures without sources. It appears on the Commission's website but is not an official EU document. As a conclusion, binding rules are the only option available to take up the challenges we face with online marketplaces. Now today, despite the fact that binding rules exist that make online marketplaces liable for the products that are sold through their website by third-party sellers, they are not applied (see above)
pairs.context_before: As an example, in 2017, the number of parcels arriving only to Arlanda airport in Stockholm amounted to 150,000 per day. In Denmark, more than 43,000 parcels arrived from China every day in 2018. Finally, it is worth mentioning that this new Regulation does not even specify that online marketplaces need to verify whether third country sellers adhere to these rules and have in fact appointed an efficient authorized representative. 10 Draft guidelines for the practical implementation of article 4 of the Regulation on market surveillance and compliance of products, p.8 P. 8
pairs.context_after: Now today, despite the fact that binding rules exist that make online marketplaces liable for the products that are sold through their website by third-party sellers, they are not applied (see above). Consequently, something must be changed in EU law: • The revised e-Commerce Directive and General Product Safety Directive The revision of the e-Commerce Directive and the General Products Safety Directive offers an opportunity to clearly state that if an online market place takes an active role in the sales transaction, they fall into one of the categories of economic operator involved in the product supply chain and as such are subject to the corresponding obligations. In particular, the e-Commerce Directive must clarifiy the definition of active/passive so that one can easily define whether an online marketplace plays an active role and therefore is liable for products sold by third party sellers and cannot benefit from the liability exemption provided in Article 14. • Guidance documents such as the Blue Guide or the Guidelines for Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 on market surveillance and compliance of products These guidance documents should also reflect this change. 11 On 25th June 2018, several online marketplaces have voluntarily committed to improving the safety of non-food consumer products sold on their online marketplaces by third party sellers by signing up to a Product Safety Pledge
pairs.article_text:
Article 56 — Commitments
1. If, during proceedings under this Section, the very large online platform concerned offers commitments to ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, the Commission may by decision make those commitments binding on the very large online platform concerned and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen the proceedings:
(a)where there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)where the very large online platform concerned acts contrary to its commitments; or
(c)where the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

3. Where the Commission considers that the commitments offered by the very large online platform concerned are unable to ensure effective compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, it shall reject those commitments in a reasoned decision when concluding the proceedings.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism whereby very large online platforms can offer voluntary commitments to the Commission, which may be made binding by Commission decision, effectively closing enforcement proceedings.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: EDRA explicitly argues that voluntary commitments and non-binding rules are ineffective and that only binding rules can address non-compliance on online platforms, citing the Product Safety Pledge as a failed example. The provision creates a commitments mechanism where platforms can offer voluntary undertakings to resolve proceedings — precisely the type of approach EDRA argues against, even though the Commission can make those commitments binding by decision.

pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)4777318 - 12/09/2020 How the Digital Services Act (DSA) Can Help Foster a More Responsible Online Environment June 2020 ABOUT IFPI IFPI represents the interests of the recording industry worldwide. We have some 1,300 members around the world and in all major European markets, ranging from small-to- medium enterprise (SME) to global labels. We work to promote the value of recorded music, to campaign for the rights of record producers and to expand the commercial uses of recorded music in all markets where our members operate. THE ISSUE1 In its announcement of the Digital Services Act (DSA) framework, the European Commission recognizes the need for a safer internet teeming with legal content, and greater accountability of digital services, including and in particular online intermediaries, to help achieve that. We strongly agree. Host providers, access providers, domain registrars and registries, app stores and search engines, for example, are all gatekeepers to or support the distribution of content and should have adequate responsibilities and obligations (“duties of care”) that are essential to tackling illegal content online. They can do better. In recent years, technology has advanced by leaps and bounds, enabling the accomplishment of tasks that would have been unimaginable when the current laws were written
pairs.context_after: They can do better. In recent years, technology has advanced by leaps and bounds, enabling the accomplishment of tasks that would have been unimaginable when the current laws were written. Today, many of these companies are among the world’s most sophisticated and innovative, and well-positioned to stop and prevent the distribution of illegal content. If they fail to fulfill their responsibilities, the risks are significant – the European cultural sectors’ eco-system (in particular when it comes to the creation of new European content) could be harmed, and European economic growth and digital innovation hampered. Europe has made great efforts to update and harmonise copyright across the European Union. However, the measures and procedures to enforce those rights online have not been updated and harmonised. An essential feature of fair and effective online enforcement of rights is the adoption of robust responsibilities and 1 This position paper addresses the key elements of the part of the Commission’s Digital Services Act package concerning responsibilities and obligations of digital services. It does not address issues dealing with potential ex ante rules applicable to large online platforms acting as gatekeepers, or competition aspects. 1
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes investigation and enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators, including the ability to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text expresses only a general endorsement of greater accountability and "duties of care" for online intermediaries, without taking any specific directional stance on the investigative or enforcement powers of Digital Services Coordinators. The position is too broad and general — it does not advocate for or against any specific enforcement mechanism, power, or safeguard that Article 41 establishes.

pairs.article_title: Competent authorities and Digital Services Coordinators
pairs.article_text:
Article 38 — Competent authorities and Digital Services Coordinators
1. Member States shall designate one or more competent authorities as responsible for the application and enforcement of this Regulation (‘competent authorities’).

2. Member States shall designate one of the competent authorities as their Digital Services Coordinator. The Digital Services Coordinator shall be responsible for all matters relating to application and enforcement of this Regulation in that Member State, unless the Member State concerned has assigned certain specific tasks or sectors to other competent authorities. The Digital Services Coordinator shall in any event be responsible for ensuring coordination at national level in respect of those matters and for contributing to the effective and consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation throughout the Union.
For that purpose, Digital Services Coordinators shall cooperate with each other, other national competent authorities, the Board and the Commission, without prejudice to the possibility for Member States to provide for regular exchanges of views with other authorities where relevant for the performance of the tasks of those other authorities and of the Digital Services Coordinator.
Where a Member State designates more than one competent authority in addition to the Digital Services Coordinator, it shall ensure that the respective tasks of those authorities and of the Digital Services Coordinator are clearly defined and that they cooperate closely and effectively when performing their tasks. The Member State concerned shall communicate the name of the other competent authorities as well as their respective tasks to the Commission and the Board.

3. Member States shall designate the Digital Services Coordinators within two months from the date of entry into force of this Regulation.
Member States shall make publicly available, and communicate to the Commission and the Board, the name of their competent authority designated as Digital Services Coordinator and information on how it can be contacted.

4. The requirements applicable to Digital Services Coordinators set out in Articles 39, 40 and 41 shall also apply to any other competent authorities that the Member States designate pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires Member States to designate one or more competent authorities and a Digital Services Coordinator responsible for the application and enforcement of the DSA regulation at national level.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text focuses on advocating for duties of care and greater accountability of online intermediaries (host providers, access providers, app stores, etc.) to tackle illegal content online. It does not express any specific position on the designation of national competent authorities or Digital Services Coordinators, which is what Article 38 establishes. The org text is general advocacy for a safer internet and stronger obligations on platforms, with no directional stance on enforcement governance structures.

pairs.article_text:
Article 44 — Activity reports
1. Digital Services Coordinators shall draw up an annual report on their activities under this Regulation. They shall make the annual reports available to the public, and shall communicate them to the Commission and to the Board.

2. The annual report shall include at least the following information:
(a)the number and subject matter of orders to act against illegal content and orders to provide information issued in accordance with Articles 8 and 9 by any national judicial or administrative authority of the Member State of the Digital Services Coordinator concerned;
(b)the effects given to those orders, as communicated to the Digital Services Coordinator pursuant to Articles 8 and 9.

3. Where a Member State has designated several competent authorities pursuant to Article 38, it shall ensure that the Digital Services Coordinator draws up a single report covering the activities of all competent authorities and that the Digital Services Coordinator receives all relevant information and support needed to that effect from the other competent authorities concerned.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires Digital Services Coordinators to produce and publish annual activity reports covering orders to act against illegal content and orders to provide information issued by national authorities.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text expresses a general endorsement of the DSA framework and the need for greater accountability of online intermediaries, but does not take any specific advocacy position on reporting obligations or activity reports by Digital Services Coordinators. The position is too broad and general to constitute a specific directional stance relative to this provision.

pairs.source_text: the 2017 Guidelines on the Enforcement Directive). However, those instruments or voluntary enforcement measures are not sufficient to remedy the problems the recording industry is facing. HOW CAN THE DSA HELP? The DSA framework should include instruments which enhance the accountability of all types of digital services and update their responsibilities and obligations to tackle illegal content online, including copyright infringements, more effectively. We note that this can, and should be achieved by complementing the E-Commerce Directive without opening it: • “Duty of Care”: All digital services, which are being used to infringe copyrights, should be obliged to take reasonable steps to stop, limit and prevent infringements (“duty of care”). The content of those responsibilities and obligations and which steps the digital services including online intermediary service can be reasonably expected to take should depend on the types of services, but all services including smaller ones should be covered. The duty of care should, for instance, include the obligation to stop providing services to illegal websites, and to implement an effective “repeat infringer” policy
pairs.context_before: hosting service providers; Content Delivery Networks (CDNs); search engines; payment providers; domain registrars; app store operators; etc. There is a need to provide for different and truly effective measures and procedures to ensure those digital services play their part in reducing unauthorised uses. In concrete terms this means, for instance, introducing an obligation to ensure accuracy of data that digital services keep about their customers. To date, it is often not possible to pursue direct action against illegal websites because the operators act in complete anonymity, protected by shell companies or domain name privacy services, or both, and the digital services and intermediaries used by these illegal websites do not require their customers to provide proof of their true identity and contact details. The Commission has acknowledged that there is a problem with online enforcement of rights in Europe, and that more should be done to strengthen the current EU enforcement system.2 So far, the Commission has chosen only non-legislative instruments to tackle the problem (e.g. the 2017 Guidelines on the Enforcement Directive). However, those instruments or voluntary enforcement measures are not sufficient to remedy the problems the recording industry is facing
pairs.context_after: The duty of care should, for instance, include the obligation to stop providing services to illegal websites, and to implement an effective “repeat infringer” policy. • “Notice and Stay Down” obligation for passive hosting providers: Online hosting services of a “mere technical, automatic and passive nature”, eligible for liability “safe harbours”, should ensure that illegal content that has been 2 In May 2015 by announcing its Digital Single Market (DSM) Strategy the Commission said that it would make legislative proposals in 2016 “modernising enforcement of intellectual property rights, focusing on commercial-scale infringements (the 'follow the money' approach) as well as its cross- border applicability”. 3
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes investigation and enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators, including the ability to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI advocates for a "duty of care" framework requiring digital services to take reasonable steps to stop copyright infringements, including obligations to cut off services to illegal websites and implement repeat infringer policies. While Article 41 establishes enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators that could theoretically be used to address copyright infringements, the provision focuses on regulatory/supervisory enforcement mechanisms rather than directly imposing duties of care on platforms. It is genuinely unclear whether these enforcement powers satisfy IFPI's specific ask for proactive platform-level obligations, as the provision addresses how regulators can act against infringers rather than mandating the platform-side accountability measures IFPI sought.

pairs.source_text: • Complement Responsibilities and Obligations without changing the scope of, and eligibility for, “safe harbours”: The definitions and the types of activities and online intermediary services eligible for the safe harbours should remain unchanged. The EU acquis should be consolidated. In particular, the current distinction used in the E-Commerce Directive between “active” and “passive” online intermediaries should not be changed by the DSA to avoid altering the careful balance in the E-Commerce Directive between the need to protect content owners and the need to enable the development of the Internet. For instance, re-defining the criteria for an “active” intermediary in a narrower manner in the DSA, could extend safe harbour privileges to services that were not meant to be entitled to them under the E-Commerce Directive. Amending the scope of the safe harbours would undermine the EU and national jurisprudence (e.g. CJEU decision in L’Oréal v eBay, Google France3) and the recent legislative developments (e.g. the DSM Directive4), which have helpfully clarified the eligibility criteria for safe harbour protection. The DSA should not 3 C-324/09 - L'Oréal and Others; C-236/08 - Google France and Google. 4 Directive (EU) 2019/790 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 April 2019 on copyright and related rights in the Digital Single Market. 4
pairs.context_before: removed does not reappear on their services. We do not however see the need to further regulate the procedural rules regarding notice and take down, such as the form of notices or timeframes for take downs, for the removal of content. They work generally well in the case of our industry, providing flexibility for the different types of services and content. • Improved Transparency Obligations: The transparency requirements under the current EU legislation should be improved, notably by requiring digital services to implement effective Know Your Business Customer (KYBC) policies and by introducing an explicit legal basis for a public WHOIS registry which should, at a very minimum, contain all data already required to be made publicly available under existing law (Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive), as well as such other information that is necessary for right holders to pursue legal actions to protect their copyrights. • Adjusting Copyright Enforcement Measures to the Digital Single Market: Furthermore, because copyright infringements are borderless, the DSA should help right holders in protecting their rights effectively across the EU; without the need to bring actions in each individual Member State against the same infringers, the same content, and the same infringing activity, be it in relation to infringing services directly or against digital services including online intermediaries
pairs.context_after: weaken those criteria but complement and strengthen the responsibilities and obligations of digital services operating in the digital markets. We detail our comments on the above-mentioned elements further in this paper. 1. MORE ROBUST RESPONSIBILITIES AND OBLIGATIONS FOR DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES (“DUTY OF CARE”) A. DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES MUST DO MORE The Issue: Illegal online services use and rely on a variety of digital services, including internet access providers, hosting service providers, Content Delivery Network (CDN) providers, search engines, payment providers, domain registrars, app store operators, operating systems and web browsers, among others. There have been some examples of voluntary cooperation between right holders and intermediaries or other digital services.5 However, such voluntary agreements are no substitutes for a robust legal framework, setting out clear responsibilities and obligations. Experience shows that voluntary agreements are more likely to be negotiated and work, where they provide for a way for digital services to comply with their statutory obligations. The Proposed Solution: EU legislation should establish obligations on all digital services including online intermediaries to take reasonable steps to stop and prevent online copyright infringements
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the subject matter and scope of the Digital Services Act, including a framework for conditional liability exemptions for intermediary services, while explicitly preserving existing EU law including Union law on copyright and related rights (Article 1(5)(c)) and Directive 2000/31/EC (the E-Commerce Directive).
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocated that the DSA should not alter the scope of or eligibility for safe harbours established under the E-Commerce Directive, and that Union copyright law (including the DSM Directive) should not be undermined. Article 1(5) directly responds to this by stating the Regulation is "without prejudice" to both Directive 2000/31/EC and "Union law on copyright and related rights," preserving the existing safe harbour framework and copyright acquis exactly as IFPI requested.

The responsibilities and obligations should apply broadly and should extend to all digital services, including online intermediaries, providing services to businesses or consumers residing in the EU. The nature of the obligations and responsibilities, the triggering factors and which steps the online intermediary can be reasonably expected to take, depends on the circumstances and the types of services offered. They should, for example, include an obligation to implement an effective “repeat infringer policy” to avoid that the same account holder or website operator can 5 For example, there are Memorandums of Understanding or Code of Conducts in place in some countries between the advertising industry and right holders, or, in Denmark and Portugal, voluntary agreements between right holders and internet access providers complement existing court and administrative procedures. 5

pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocates for EU legislation to establish robust obligations and enforcement mechanisms requiring digital services/online intermediaries to stop and prevent online copyright infringements, arguing voluntary agreements are insufficient. Article 41 directly responds to this by granting Digital Services Coordinators strong investigative and enforcement powers — including ordering cessation of infringements, imposing fines, and restricting access — which constitute the kind of robust legal framework with real enforcement teeth that IFPI called for.

pairs.context_after: continue to infringe copyright in spite of the fact that the service provider knows or is aware of the account to be found to be infringing, e.g. following a notice from the right holders. Thus, once the service provider knows or is aware of repeat infringements coming from the same account or customer, effective actions need to be taken, e.g. temporary suspension of accounts. Digital services should also stop enabling or facilitating the operation of illegal websites by providing them services or means to operate. Once a digital service has actual knowledge, or has become aware of facts or circumstances of which it becomes apparent, that a website to which it provides services infringes intellectual property rights, it should cease providing services to that website for as long as the illegal activity continues. This may require digital services to undertake reasonable checks to investigate the nature of their customers’ business to ensure that unauthorised activities are not being supported. For example, if a website owner wishes to register a website, the name of which raises clear red flags (e.g. www.leakedmusicfor free.com), the domain register should inquire with the registrant about the purpose of the website. Similarly, given that online piracy occurs very often via mobile devices involving mobile apps, app store operators should take a more proactive approach and remove apps which facilitate online copyright infringements
pairs.article_text:
Article 43 — Right to lodge a complaint
1. Recipients of the service shall have the right to lodge a complaint against providers of intermediary services alleging an infringement of this Regulation with the Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State where the recipient resides or is established. The Digital Services Coordinator shall assess the complaint and, where appropriate, transmit it to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment. Where the complaint falls under the responsibility of another competent authority in its Member State, the Digital Service Coordinator receiving the complaint shall transmit it to that authority.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the right for recipients of intermediary services to lodge complaints about infringements of the DSA Regulation with the relevant Digital Services Coordinator.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI's position specifically advocates for obligations on digital intermediaries to prevent online copyright infringements, including repeat infringer policies — a substantive content moderation/IP enforcement ask. Article 43 establishes a procedural complaint mechanism for DSA regulatory infringements by service recipients, which is unrelated to copyright enforcement obligations. There is no substantive connection between IFPI's advocacy and this provision's subject matter.

pairs.source_text: The Proposed Solution: EU legislation should establish obligations on all digital services including online intermediaries to take reasonable steps to stop and prevent online copyright infringements. The responsibilities and obligations should apply broadly and should extend to all digital services, including online intermediaries, providing services to businesses or consumers residing in the EU. The nature of the obligations and responsibilities, the triggering factors and which steps the online intermediary can be reasonably expected to take, depends on the circumstances and the types of services offered. They should, for example, include an obligation to implement an effective “repeat infringer policy” to avoid that the same account holder or website operator can 5 For example, there are Memorandums of Understanding or Code of Conducts in place in some countries between the advertising industry and right holders, or, in Denmark and Portugal, voluntary agreements between right holders and internet access providers complement existing court and administrative procedures. 5
pairs.context_before: weaken those criteria but complement and strengthen the responsibilities and obligations of digital services operating in the digital markets. We detail our comments on the above-mentioned elements further in this paper. 1. MORE ROBUST RESPONSIBILITIES AND OBLIGATIONS FOR DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES (“DUTY OF CARE”) A. DIGITAL SERVICES, INCLUDING ONLINE INTERMEDIARIES MUST DO MORE The Issue: Illegal online services use and rely on a variety of digital services, including internet access providers, hosting service providers, Content Delivery Network (CDN) providers, search engines, payment providers, domain registrars, app store operators, operating systems and web browsers, among others. There have been some examples of voluntary cooperation between right holders and intermediaries or other digital services.5 However, such voluntary agreements are no substitutes for a robust legal framework, setting out clear responsibilities and obligations. Experience shows that voluntary agreements are more likely to be negotiated and work, where they provide for a way for digital services to comply with their statutory obligations. The Proposed Solution: EU legislation should establish obligations on all digital services including online intermediaries to take reasonable steps to stop and prevent online copyright infringements
pairs.context_after: continue to infringe copyright in spite of the fact that the service provider knows or is aware of the account to be found to be infringing, e.g. following a notice from the right holders. Thus, once the service provider knows or is aware of repeat infringements coming from the same account or customer, effective actions need to be taken, e.g. temporary suspension of accounts. Digital services should also stop enabling or facilitating the operation of illegal websites by providing them services or means to operate. Once a digital service has actual knowledge, or has become aware of facts or circumstances of which it becomes apparent, that a website to which it provides services infringes intellectual property rights, it should cease providing services to that website for as long as the illegal activity continues. This may require digital services to undertake reasonable checks to investigate the nature of their customers’ business to ensure that unauthorised activities are not being supported. For example, if a website owner wishes to register a website, the name of which raises clear red flags (e.g. www.leakedmusicfor free.com), the domain register should inquire with the registrant about the purpose of the website. Similarly, given that online piracy occurs very often via mobile devices involving mobile apps, app store operators should take a more proactive approach and remove apps which facilitate online copyright infringements
pairs.article_text:
Article 55 — Interim measures
1. In the context of proceedings which may lead to the adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 58(1), where there is an urgency due to the risk of serious damage for the recipients of the service, the Commission may, by decision, order interim measures against the very large online platform concerned on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement.

2. A decision under paragraph 1 shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
pairs.provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to order interim measures against very large online platforms on the basis of a prima facie finding of infringement where there is urgency due to risk of serious damage to recipients of the service.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: IFPI's position advocates for obligations on digital services/online intermediaries to prevent copyright infringement, including repeat infringer policies. The provision concerns the Commission's power to impose interim measures against very large online platforms in the context of DSA compliance proceedings — it is not about copyright infringement obligations at all. The subjects are substantively unrelated, and IFPI's text contains no specific stance on interim enforcement measures by the Commission.

pairs.source_text: Directive, e.g. the true and verified name, physical address, email address and telephone number of their customers. In case of legal entities, the information should also include up-to-date information of the company directors or other responsible persons. Depending on the type of service, it could also include bank account details. In addition to the initial verification of the details provided, digital services should apply regular checks to ensure that the details remain accurate and up to date. This obligation should also apply to any reseller of the original digital service. There should be sanctions for the failure to comply with the obligation. Further, digital services should terminate services to websites or online services where the information these services have provided turns out to be incorrect (regardless of the KYBC process followed), or where the services do not comply with the transparency obligations, e.g. if a website does not publish information required under of the E- Commerce Directive (see below). II. BETTER ENFORCEMENT OF NON-COMPLIANCE WITH OF THE E-COMMERCE DIRECTIVE EU legislation on transparency should be complemented by better enforcement of non-compliance with of the E-Commerce Directive at national level
pairs.context_before: This seriously undermines online enforcement efforts, including attempts to contact or bring actions against the suspected infringers. The Proposed Solutions: To improve transparency, the DSA should include the following elements: I. “KNOW YOUR BUSINESS CUSTOMER” POLICIES Legislation should require that digital services including online intermediaries implement effective Know Your Business Customer (KYBC) policies, i.e. intermediaries need to ensure they verify the identity of their business customers and have accurate and meaningful information about their customers. At a minimum, such information should include the information that services are required to provide under Article 5 of the E-Commerce 8 See Directive 2005/60/EC of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing. The relevant “Know your customer” obligations have been implemented across the EU, e.g.: Art. 29 of the UK Regulation of 2017 on Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds; section 11 et seq. of the German Money Laundering Act. 8
pairs.context_after: Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive requires all EU-based service providers to “render easily, directly and permanently accessible to” EU consumers and authorities at least the name, geographical address, e-mail address and other information depending on the business model. In IFPI’s experience this obligation is rarely being complied with, in particular by illegal services. There are a number of reasons for this, including that in most Member States there is no effective enforcement and no deterrent consequences when a service is found to be in breach of this obligation. Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive should therefore be complemented to ensure that it applies to all services that are being provided to EU citizens, regardless of the physical location of the business, and there should be deterrent sanctions in case of non- compliance. In addition, digital services should not provide services to websites that do not comply with Article 5 of the E-Commerce Directive (see above). 9
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to collect, verify, and publish trader identity information (name, address, contact details, bank account, trade register, and self-certification) before allowing traders to offer products/services to EU consumers, and to suspend services to traders who fail to provide accurate information.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: IFPI explicitly advocated for digital services to collect verified identity information (name, physical address, email, telephone, bank account details, and for legal entities, director information), to verify and regularly check that information, and to terminate services where information is incorrect — all of which the provision directly delivers through its trader traceability requirements, verification obligations, and suspension mechanism for non-compliant traders.

pairs.article_text:
Article 52 — Requests for information
1. In order to carry out the tasks assigned to it under this Section, the Commission may by simple request or by decision require the very large online platforms concerned, as well as any other persons acting for purposes related to their trade, business, craft or profession that may be reasonably be aware of information relating to the suspected infringement or the infringement, as applicable, including organisations performing the audits referred to in Articles 28 and 50(3), to provide such information within a reasonable time period.

2. When sending a simple request for information to the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1), the Commission shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and set the time period within which the information is to be provided, and the penalties provided for in Article 59 for supplying incorrect or misleading information.

3. Where the Commission requires the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) to supply information by decision, it shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and set the time period within which it is to be provided. It shall also indicate the penalties provided for in Article 59 and indicate or impose the periodic penalty payments provided for in Article 60. It shall further indicate the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice of the European Union.

4. The owners of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) or their representatives and, in the case of legal persons, companies or firms, or where they have no legal personality, the persons authorised to represent them by law or by their constitution shall supply the information requested on behalf of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1). Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply the information on behalf of their clients. The latter shall remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

5. At the request of the Commission, the Digital Services Coordinators and other competent authorities shall provide the Commission with all necessary information to carry out the tasks assigned to it under this Section.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to request information from very large online platforms and other persons in the context of investigating suspected infringements, either by simple request or by binding decision.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text contains no substantive advocacy position — it consists entirely of administrative/form-filling content (checkboxes, establishment year, member state listings) with no directional stance on information requests or Commission investigative powers. There is no specific position that can be evaluated against this provision.

pairs.article_text:
Article 10 — Points of contact
1. Providers of intermediary services shall establish a single point of contact allowing for direct communication, by electronic means, with Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board referred to in Article 47 for the application of this Regulation.

2. Providers of intermediary services shall make public the information necessary to easily identify and communicate with their single points of contact.

3. Providers of intermediary services shall specify in the information referred to in paragraph 2, the official language or languages of the Union, which can be used to communicate with their points of contact and which shall include at least one of the official languages of the Member State in which the provider of intermediary services has its main establishment or where its legal representative resides or is established.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires providers of intermediary services to establish a single point of contact for communication with authorities and to publicly disclose contact information and accepted official languages.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is purely administrative/form-filling content (checkboxes, establishment year, member state listings) with no substantive advocacy position on points of contact, communication requirements, or any related mechanism. There is no directional stance that can be evaluated against the provision.

Artikel 5 sollte daher ergänzt werden, um sicherzustellen, dass er für alle Dienstleistungen gilt, die für EU-Bürger erbracht werden, und es sollte abschreckende Sanktionen für den Fall der Nichteinhaltung geben. The following questions are targeted at online intermediaries. 29
pairs.context_before: Im Falle von juristischen Personen sollten die Informationen auch aktuelle Informationen der Geschäftsführer oder anderer verantwortlicher Personen enthalten. Je nach Art des Dienstes könnten sie auch Angaben zur Bankverbindung enthalten. Zusätzlich zur anfänglichen Überprüfung der gemachten Angaben sollte es regelmäßige Überprüfungen geben, um sicherzustellen, dass die Angaben korrekt und aktuell bleiben. Diese Verpflichtung sollte auch für jeden Wiederverkäufer des ursprünglichen digitalen Dienstes gelten. Für die Nichteinhaltung dieser Verpflichtung sollte es Sanktionen geben. Darüber hinaus sollten digitale Dienste Dienste Dienste zu Websites einstellen, wenn sich die Informationen als unrichtig erweisen oder wenn die Dienste keine Informationen veröffentlichen, die gemäß Artikel 5 der E-Commerce-Richtlinie erforderlich sind. Bessere Durchsetzung der Nichteinhaltung von Artikel 5 der E-Commerce-Richtlinie: Die EU- Gesetzgebung sollte durch eine bessere Durchsetzung der Nichteinhaltung von Artikel 5 ergänzt werden. In der Praxis wird dieser Verpflichtung nur selten nachgekommen, insbesondere von illegalen Diensten. Dafür gibt es eine Reihe von Gründen, z.B. gibt es in den meisten Mitgliedstaaten keine wirksame Durchsetzung und keine abschreckenden Konsequenzen, wenn eine Dienstleistung gegen diese Verpflichtung verstößt

pairs.article_text:
Article 42 — Penalties
1. Member States shall lay down the rules on penalties applicable to infringements of this Regulation by providers of intermediary services under their jurisdiction and shall take all the necessary measures to ensure that they are implemented in accordance with Article 41.

2. Penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. Member States shall notify the Commission of those rules and of those measures and shall notify it, without delay, of any subsequent amendments affecting them.

3. Member States shall ensure that the maximum amount of penalties imposed for a failure to comply with the obligations laid down in this Regulation shall not exceed 6 % of the annual income or turnover of the provider of intermediary services concerned. Penalties for the supply of incorrect, incomplete or misleading information, failure to reply or rectify incorrect, incomplete or misleading information and to submit to an on-site inspection shall not exceed 1% of the annual income or turnover of the provider concerned.

4. Member States shall ensure that the maximum amount of a periodic penalty payment shall not exceed 5 % of the average daily turnover of the provider of intermediary services concerned in the preceding financial year per day, calculated from the date specified in the decision concerned.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes that Member States must set effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties for infringements of the Regulation, with maximum caps of 6% of annual turnover for general non-compliance and 1% for information-related failures.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for dissuasive sanctions ("abschreckende Sanktionen") in cases of non-compliance, noting that effective enforcement is currently lacking in most Member States. The provision directly responds to this by mandating that Member States establish effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties with concrete turnover-based caps, which is precisely the kind of deterrent enforcement mechanism the organisation called for.

pairs.article_title: Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 23 — Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
1. In addition to the information referred to in Article 13, online platforms shall include in the reports referred to in that Article information on the following:
(a)the number of disputes submitted to the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies referred to in Article 18, the outcomes of the dispute settlement and the average time needed for completing the dispute settlement procedures;
(b)the number of suspensions imposed pursuant to Article 20, distinguishing between suspensions enacted for the provision of manifestly illegal content, the submission of manifestly unfounded notices and the submission of manifestly unfounded complaints;
(c)any use made of automatic means for the purpose of content moderation, including a specification of the precise purposes, indicators of the accuracy of the automated means in fulfilling those purposes and any safeguards applied.

2. Online platforms shall publish, at least once every six months, information on the average monthly active recipients of the service in each Member State, calculated as an average over the period of the past six months, in accordance with the methodology laid down in the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 25(2).

3. Online platforms shall communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, upon its request, the information referred to in paragraph 2, updated to the moment of such request. That Digital Services Coordinator may require the online platform to provide additional information as regards the calculation referred to in that paragraph, including explanations and substantiation in respect of the data used. That information shall not include personal data.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to lay down templates concerning the form, content and other details of reports pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to report on out-of-court dispute outcomes, suspensions, automated content moderation tools (including accuracy and safeguards), and publish average monthly active user numbers every six months.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text is a survey/questionnaire format asking platforms what transparency information they currently provide — it describes existing practices and poses questions rather than advocating for a specific policy direction. There is no directional stance (e.g., "platforms should be required to publish X" or "mandatory reporting goes too far"); it is essentially background data-gathering with no substantive advocacy position that can be evaluated against the provision.

pairs.article_text:
Article 31 — Data access and scrutiny
1. Very large online platforms shall provide the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, upon their reasoned request and within a reasonable period, specified in the request, access to data that are necessary to monitor and assess compliance with this Regulation. That Digital Services Coordinator and the Commission shall only use that data for those purposes.

2. Upon a reasoned request from the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, very large online platforms shall, within a reasonable period, as specified in the request, provide access to data to vetted researchers who meet the requirements in paragraphs 4 of this Article, for the sole purpose of conducting research that contributes to the identification and understanding of systemic risks as set out in Article 26(1).

3. Very large online platforms shall provide access to data pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2 through online databases or application programming interfaces, as appropriate.

4. In order to be vetted, researchers shall be affiliated with academic institutions, be independent from commercial interests, have proven records of expertise in the fields related to the risks investigated or related research methodologies, and shall commit and be in a capacity to preserve the specific data security and confidentiality requirements corresponding to each request.

5. The Commission shall, after consulting the Board, adopt delegated acts laying down the technical conditions under which very large online platforms are to share data pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2 and the purposes for which the data may be used. Those delegated acts shall lay down the specific conditions under which such sharing of data with vetted researchers can take place in compliance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679, taking into account the rights and interests of the very large online platforms and the recipients of the service concerned, including the protection of confidential information, in particular trade secrets, and maintaining the security of their service.

6. Within 15 days following receipt of a request as referred to in paragraph 1 and 2, a very large online platform may request the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, as applicable, to amend the request, where it considers that it is unable to give access to the data requested because one of following two reasons:
(a)it does not have access to the data;
(b)giving access to the data will lead to significant vulnerabilities for the security of its service or the protection of confidential information, in particular trade secrets.

7. Requests for amendment pursuant to point (b) of paragraph 6 shall contain proposals for one or more alternative means through which access may be provided to the requested data or other data which are appropriate and sufficient for the purpose of the request.
The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission shall decide upon the request for amendment within 15 days and communicate to the very large online platform its decision and, where relevant, the amended request and the new time period to comply with the request.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to provide data access to regulators and vetted academic researchers for compliance monitoring and systemic risk research, with specific conditions on data security, confidentiality, and researcher independence.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text is a fragmented survey response form listing data-sharing categories and questions about platform responsibilities, without expressing any specific directional advocacy position on data access or researcher access to platform data. It contains no substantive stance that can be evaluated against the provision's requirements.

pairs.article_text:
Article 44 — Activity reports
1. Digital Services Coordinators shall draw up an annual report on their activities under this Regulation. They shall make the annual reports available to the public, and shall communicate them to the Commission and to the Board.

2. The annual report shall include at least the following information:
(a)the number and subject matter of orders to act against illegal content and orders to provide information issued in accordance with Articles 8 and 9 by any national judicial or administrative authority of the Member State of the Digital Services Coordinator concerned;
(b)the effects given to those orders, as communicated to the Digital Services Coordinator pursuant to Articles 8 and 9.

3. Where a Member State has designated several competent authorities pursuant to Article 38, it shall ensure that the Digital Services Coordinator draws up a single report covering the activities of all competent authorities and that the Digital Services Coordinator receives all relevant information and support needed to that effect from the other competent authorities concerned.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires Digital Services Coordinators to produce and publish annual activity reports covering orders to act against illegal content and orders to provide information issued by national authorities.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text is a survey response form with fragmented, administrative content about data sharing and platform responsibilities — it contains no specific advocacy position on Digital Services Coordinator reporting obligations or annual activity reports. The text is essentially boilerplate survey structure and general background, not a directional stance on the provision's subject matter.
