Die nationalen Gerichte in der EU haben dies unterschiedlich interpretiert, und bisher hat kein Gericht in der EU zusätzlich zu Angaben über den Absender der Benachrichtigung Informationen verlangt, z.B. über den Rechtsinhaber oder den im Namen des Rechtsinhabers handelnden Agenten, die Beschreibung des Werkes/Inhalts, das/der angeblich verletzt wurde, sowie Einzelheiten über die Verletzung und den Ort. Die EU-Gesetzgebung sollte sicherstellen, dass ein Hosting-Service-Provider bei Bekanntwerden einer Rechtsverletzung (1) alle Kopien desselben Werkes/derselben Tonträgers entfernen und (2) sicherstellen muss, dass dasselbe Werk oder derselbe Tonträger (oder eine Kopie davon) in Zukunft nicht erneut veröffentlicht oder hochgeladen wird (d.h. "Stay Down"-Verpflichtung). 5
pairs.context_before: Beispielsweise könnte eine engere Neudefinition der Kriterien für einen "aktiven" Vermittler in der DSA die Safe- Harbour-Privilegien auf Dienste ausdehnen, die nach der E-Commerce-Richtlinie keinen Anspruch darauf haben sollten. Eine Änderung des Geltungsbereichs der Safe- Harbour-Privilegien würde die Rechtsprechung der EU und der Mitgliedstaaten (z.B. die Entscheidung des CJEU in der Rechtssache L'Oréal gegen eBay, Google France [REFS]) und die jüngsten legislativen Entwicklungen (z.B. die DSM-Richtlinie) untergraben, die die Anspruchskriterien für den Safe-Harbour-Schutz hilfreich geklärt haben. Die DSA sollte diese Kriterien nicht abschwächen, sondern die Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen der auf den digitalen Märkten tätigen digitalen Dienste ergänzen und stärken. 3. What information would be, in your view, necessary and sufficient for users and third parties to send to an online platform in order to notify an illegal activity (sales of illegal 38
pairs.context_after: "Stay Down"-Verpflichtung). 5. How should the reappearance of illegal content, goods or services be addressed, in your view? What approaches are effective and proportionate? 5000 Character (s) maximum Das Wiederauftauchen bereits gemeldeter rechtsverletzender Inhalte ist ein massives Problem für alle Rechteinhaber, einschließlich der Mitgliedsfirmen des BVMI. Die gegenwärtige "Notice and Takedown"-Anforderung ist ein unwirksames Instrument, um der schieren Menge an urheberrechtsverletzenden Inhalten online entgegenzuwirken. Dienstanbieter entfernen oft nur die einzelne URL, die in der Mitteilung genannt wird - anstatt alle verletzenden Kopien derselben Tonaufnahme zu entfernen - aus ihren Diensten und unternehmen nichts, um sicherzustellen, dass verletzende Kopien nicht sofort wieder hochgeladen werden. Gemäß IFPI-Daten für 2018 beziehen sich 88% der IFPI-Mitteilungen auf Inhalte, die bereits zuvor bei demselben Online-Dienst angemeldet wurden. Und trotz all dieser Tausenden von Benachrichtigungen tauchen diese Aufzeichnungen immer wieder regelmäßig an derselben Stelle auf. Obwohl der Dienst hierzu nach deutschen Recht aufgrund der Rapidshare-Rechtsprechung verpflichtet ist (siehe oben), ergreift der Dienst aktuell jedoch keine Maßnahmen, um das Wiederauftauchen von Inhalten zu verhindern, die zuvor gemeldet wurden. Der Dienst kommt somit aktuell seiner „Stay Down“-Verpflichtung nicht nach. 39
pairs.provision_effect: Requires hosting service providers to inform recipients of removal/disabling decisions with a clear, specific statement of reasons including legal grounds, facts, use of automated means, and available redress options, and to publish these decisions in a public database.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on the requirements for a valid takedown notice (what the notifier must provide) and advocates for "stay down" obligations on hosting providers — neither of which is addressed by Article 15, which concerns the statement of reasons that providers must give to recipients when removing content. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the provider's obligation to explain its removal decisions to recipients.

pairs.source_text: Digital services established outside of the Union? b. Sellers established outside of the Union, who reach EU consumers through online platforms? 3000 Character (s) maximum Alle Plattformen und digitalen Dienste einschließlich der Vermittler, die Dienstleistungen für EU-Bürger erbringen, müssen mehr tun, um Urheberrechtsverletzungen zu verhindern. Die EU-Gesetzgebung sollte für alle digitalen Dienste, einschließlich der Online-Vermittler, Verpflichtungen festlegen, angemessene Schritte zu unternehmen, um Online-Urheberrechtsverletzungen zu verhindern. Die Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen sollten weit gefasst sein und sich auf alle digitalen Dienste, einschließlich der Online-Vermittler, erstrecken, die Dienstleistungen für Unternehmen oder Verbraucher mit Wohnsitz in der EU erbringen, unabhängig davon, von wo aus der Inhalt zur Verfügung gestellt oder angeboten wurde. Die Art der Pflichten und Verantwortlichkeiten, die auslösenden Faktoren und die Schritte, die vom Online-Vermittler vernünftigerweise erwartet werden können, hängen von den Umständen und den Arten der angebotenen Dienste ab. Sie sollten z.B. die Verpflichtung enthalten, eine wirksame "Repeat Infringer Policy“ umzusetzen, um zu verhindern, dass derselbe Kontoinhaber oder Website- Betreiber weiterhin Urheberrechte verletzen kann, obwohl der Diensteanbieter weiß oder davon Kenntnis hat, dass das Konto als rechtsverletzend befunden wird, z.B. nach einer Mitteilung der Rechteinhaber
pairs.context_before: 6. Where automated tools are used for detection of illegal content, goods or services, what opportunities and risks does their use represent as regards different types of illegal activities and the specificities of the different types of tools? 3000 character (s) maximum In Bezug auf das Urheberrecht stellen automatisierte Werkzeuge und Technologien zur Unterstützung digitaler Dienste bei der Identifizierung urheberrechtsverletzender Inhalte auf ihren Plattformen eine große Chance dar. Sie sind weithin verfügbar, robust, wenn es um die Identifizierung von urheberrechtsverletzenden Inhalten geht, und es gibt erschwingliche und maßgeschneiderte Lösungen für verschiedene Arten von Diensten und Bedürfnissen. So setzen viele Dienste bereits solche Systeme ein, um sicherzustellen, dass ihre Dienste nicht als Mittel zur Verbreitung urheberrechtsverletzender Inhalte genutzt werden. Entweder können digitale Dienste ihre eigenen Systeme aufbauen (z.B. die Content-ID von YouTube), oder sie können sich auf Drittanbieter wie Audible Magic, BMAT oder Pex verlassen. 7. How should the spread of illegal goods, services or content across multiple platforms and services be addressed? Are there specific provisions necessary for addressing risks brought by: a. Digital services established outside of the Union? b
pairs.context_after: nach einer Mitteilung der Rechteinhaber. Dann müssen wirksame Maßnahmen ergriffen werden, z.B. die vorübergehende Sperrung von Konten. Die Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen sollten im Prinzip unabhängig vom Umfang des digitalen Dienstes gelten. Kleine und mittlere Unternehmen könnten 41
pairs.article_text:
Article 18 — Out-of-court dispute settlement
1. Recipients of the service addressed by the decisions referred to in Article 17(1), shall be entitled to select any out-of-court dispute that has been certified in accordance with paragraph 2 in order to resolve disputes relating to those decisions, including complaints that could not be resolved by means of the internal complaint-handling system referred to in that Article. Online platforms shall engage, in good faith, with the body selected with a view to resolving the dispute and shall be bound by the decision taken by the body.
The first subparagraph is without prejudice to the right of the recipient concerned to redress against the decision before a court in accordance with the applicable law.

2. The Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State where the out-of-court dispute settlement body is established shall, at the request of that body, certify the body, where the body has demonstrated that it meets all of the following conditions:
(a)it is impartial and independent of online platforms and recipients of the service provided by the online platforms;
(b)it has the necessary expertise in relation to the issues arising in one or more particular areas of illegal content, or in relation to the application and enforcement of terms and conditions of one or more types of online platforms, allowing the body to contribute effectively to the settlement of a dispute;
(c)the dispute settlement is easily accessible through electronic communication technology;
(d)it is capable of settling dispute in a swift, efficient and cost-effective manner and in at least one official language of the Union;
(e)the dispute settlement takes place in accordance with clear and fair rules of procedure.
The Digital Services Coordinator shall, where applicable, specify in the certificate the particular issues to which the body’s expertise relates and the official language or languages of the Union in which the body is capable of settling disputes, as referred to in points (b) and (d) of the first subparagraph, respectively.

3. If the body decides the dispute in favour of the recipient of the service, the online platform shall reimburse the recipient for any fees and other reasonable expenses that the recipient has paid or is to pay in relation to the dispute settlement. If the body decides the dispute in favour of the online platform, the recipient shall not be required to reimburse any fees or other expenses that the online platform paid or is to pay in relation to the dispute settlement.
The fees charged by the body for the dispute settlement shall be reasonable and shall in any event not exceed the costs thereof.
Certified out-of-court dispute settlement bodies shall make the fees, or the mechanisms used to determine the fees, known to the recipient of the services and the online platform concerned before engaging in the dispute settlement.

4. Member States may establish out-of-court dispute settlement bodies for the purposes of paragraph 1 or support the activities of some or all out-of-court dispute settlement bodies that they have certified in accordance with paragraph 2.
Member States shall ensure that any of their activities undertaken under the first subparagraph do not affect the ability of their Digital Services Coordinators to certify the bodies concerned in accordance with paragraph 2.

5. Digital Services Coordinators shall notify to the Commission the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies that they have certified in accordance with paragraph 2, including where applicable the specifications referred to in the second subparagraph of that paragraph. The Commission shall publish a list of those bodies, including those specifications, on a dedicated website, and keep it updated.

6. This Article is without prejudice to Directive 2013/11/EU and alternative dispute resolution procedures and entities for consumers established under that Directive.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a certified out-of-court dispute settlement mechanism for recipients of online platform decisions, binding platforms to engage in good faith and abide by the body's rulings.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses specifically on requiring platforms to prevent copyright infringement, including implementing repeat infringer policies — it does not address out-of-court dispute settlement mechanisms for service recipients. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the subject matter of Article 18.

pairs.article_title: Cross-border cooperation among Digital Services Coordinators
pairs.article_text:
Article 45 — Cross-border cooperation among Digital Services Coordinators
1. Where a Digital Services Coordinator has reasons to suspect that a provider of an intermediary service, not under the jurisdiction of the Member State concerned, infringed this Regulation, it shall request the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment to assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation.
Where the Board has reasons to suspect that a provider of intermediary services infringed this Regulation in a manner involving at least three Member States, it may recommend the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment to assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation.

2. A request or recommendation pursuant to paragraph 1 shall at least indicate:
(a)the point of contact of the provider of the intermediary services concerned as provided for in Article 10;
(b)a description of the relevant facts, the provisions of this Regulation concerned and the reasons why the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, suspects that the provider infringed this Regulation;
(c)any other information that the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, considers relevant, including, where appropriate, information gathered on its own initiative or suggestions for specific investigatory or enforcement measures to be taken, including interim measures.

3. The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall take into utmost account the request or recommendation pursuant to paragraph 1. Where it considers that it has insufficient information to act upon the request or recommendation and has reasons to consider that the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, could provide additional information, it may request such information. The time period laid down in paragraph 4 shall be suspended until that additional information is provided.

4. The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall, without undue delay and in any event not later than two months following receipt of the request or recommendation, communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, its assessment of the suspected infringement, or that of any other competent authority pursuant to national law where relevant, and an explanation of any investigatory or enforcement measures taken or envisaged in relation thereto to ensure compliance with this Regulation.

5. Where the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or, where appropriate, the Board, did not receive a reply within the time period laid down in paragraph 4 or where it does not agree with the assessment of the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, it may refer the matter to the Commission, providing all relevant information. That information shall include at least the request or recommendation sent to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, any additional information provided pursuant to paragraph 3 and the communication referred to in paragraph 4.

6. The Commission shall assess the matter within three months following the referral of the matter pursuant to paragraph 5, after having consulted the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment and, unless it referred the matter itself, the Board.

7. Where, pursuant to paragraph 6, the Commission concludes that the assessment or the investigatory or enforcement measures taken or envisaged pursuant to paragraph 4 are incompatible with this Regulation, it shall request the Digital Service Coordinator of establishment to further assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory or enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation, and to inform it about those measures taken within two months from that request.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a cross-border cooperation mechanism among Digital Services Coordinators and the Commission to investigate and enforce compliance with the DSA against intermediary service providers operating across Member States.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation advocates for broad obligations on all digital services and intermediaries serving EU users to prevent copyright infringement, including repeat infringer policies, regardless of where the service is established. Article 45 establishes a cross-border enforcement cooperation mechanism among Digital Services Coordinators, which is relevant to the org's concern about services established outside the EU reaching EU consumers. However, the provision focuses on procedural enforcement coordination rather than substantive copyright obligations, making it genuinely unclear whether this mechanism satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the org's specific asks around copyright infringement prevention duties.

pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the investigative and enforcement powers of Digital Services Coordinators, including powers to require information, conduct inspections, impose fines, order cessation of infringements, and restrict access to services in cases of serious harm.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation advocates for broad obligations on all digital platforms and intermediaries serving EU users to prevent copyright infringements, including implementing effective repeat infringer policies. While Article 41 establishes enforcement powers for Digital Services Coordinators that could in principle be used to address copyright infringements, the provision does not specifically address copyright obligations, repeat infringer policies, or the extraterritorial scope the organisation calls for. There is a real but indirect connection — stronger DSC enforcement powers could serve the org's goal of combating online copyright infringement — but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the organisation's specific advocacy on copyright obligations and repeat infringer policies.

pairs.article_title: Intervention by the Commission and opening of proceedings
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the conditions and procedures under which the Commission may initiate enforcement proceedings against very large online platforms suspected of infringing the Digital Services Act, including coordination with Digital Services Coordinators and the Board.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses specifically on copyright infringement obligations for digital services and online intermediaries, including repeat infringer policies and duties to prevent copyright violations. The provision deals with the Commission's procedural powers to open enforcement proceedings against very large online platforms under the DSA — a governance/enforcement mechanism unrelated to copyright obligations. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that speaks to this Commission intervention procedure.

Digital services established outside of the Union? b

pairs.article_text:
Article 11 — Legal representatives
1. Providers of intermediary services which do not have an establishment in the Union but which offer services in the Union shall designate, in writing, a legal or natural person as their legal representative in one of the Member States where the provider offers its services.

2. Providers of intermediary services shall mandate their legal representatives to be addressed in addition to or instead of the provider by the Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board on all issues necessary for the receipt of, compliance with and enforcement of decisions issued in relation to this Regulation. Providers of intermediary services shall provide their legal representative with the necessary powers and resource to cooperate with the Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board and comply with those decisions.

3. The designated legal representative can be held liable for non-compliance with obligations under this Regulation, without prejudice to the liability and legal actions that could be initiated against the provider of intermediary services.

4. Providers of intermediary services shall notify the name, address, the electronic mail address and telephone number of their legal representative to the Digital Service Coordinator in the Member State where that legal representative resides or is established. They shall ensure that that information is up to date.

5. The designation of a legal representative within the Union pursuant to paragraph 1 shall not amount to an establishment in the Union.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires providers of intermediary services without EU establishment to designate a legal representative in the Union who can be held liable for non-compliance with the Regulation.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that all digital services and intermediaries offering services to EU consumers should be subject to obligations regardless of where they are established, arguing that responsibilities should extend to all digital services serving EU businesses or consumers "unabhängig davon, von wo aus der Inhalt zur Verfügung gestellt oder angeboten wurde" (regardless of where the content is provided from). Article 11 directly responds to this by requiring non-EU-established intermediary service providers to designate a legal representative in the Union, ensuring they are subject to enforcement and compliance obligations — concretely extending the Regulation's reach to services operating from outside the EU, in line with what the organisation advocated.

Die Benutzer sollten Zugang zu allen Arten von Inhalten und Informationen haben und diese Inhalte oder Informationen einschließlich ihrer Meinungen und anderen Äußerungen mit der Öffentlichkeit teilen dürfen, wenn es sich um rechtliche Informationen, Inhalte und Aktivitäten handelt. pairs.source_text: 5000 Character (s) maximum Dies sollte Sache der digitalen Dienste sein, aber die Gesetzgebung könnte sie dazu ermutigen, klare Bedingungen für die Nutzung ihrer Dienste zur Verfügung zu stellen, einschließlich einer starken Urheberrechtspolitik, die die Nutzer über die Maßnahmen informiert, die der Dienst im Falle der Nichteinhaltung ergreifen wird. Die Bedingungen und Konditionen sollten auch ausdrücklich die möglichen Maßnahmen erklären, die im Rahmen einer Politik der wiederholten Rechtsverletzung ergriffen werden können. Sobald ein bestimmtes Stück verletzenden Inhalts entfernt wurde, z.B. nach einer Aufforderung zur Entfernung von Inhalten, die von einem Rechteinhaber gesendet wurde, oder im Falle der Kenntnis anderer Tatsachen und Umstände, die auf Verletzungen hindeuten, sollte der Dienst den betroffenen Nutzer über die Maßnahmen und verfügbaren Gegenmaßnahmen informieren, für die sich der Nutzer entscheiden kann. 18. pairs.article_text:
Article 17 — Internal complaint-handling system
1. Online platforms shall provide recipients of the service, for a period of at least six months following the decision referred to in this paragraph, the access to an effective internal complaint-handling system, which enables the complaints to be lodged electronically and free of charge, against the following decisions taken by the online platform on the ground that the information provided by the recipients is illegal content or incompatible with its terms and conditions:
(a)decisions to remove or disable access to the information;
(b)decisions to suspend or terminate the provision of the service, in whole or in part, to the recipients;
(c)decisions to suspend or terminate the recipients’ account.

2. Online platforms shall ensure that their internal complaint-handling systems are easy to access, user-friendly and enable and facilitate the submission of sufficiently precise and adequately substantiated complaints.

3. Online platforms shall handle complaints submitted through their internal complaint-handling system in a timely, diligent and objective manner. Where a complaint contains sufficient grounds for the online platform to consider that the information to which the complaint relates is not illegal and is not incompatible with its terms and conditions, or contains information indicating that the complainant’s conduct does not warrant the suspension or termination of the service or the account, it shall reverse its decision referred to in paragraph 1 without undue delay.

4. Online platforms shall inform complainants without undue delay of the decision they have taken in respect of the information to which the complaint relates and shall inform complainants of the possibility of out-of-court dispute settlement provided for in Article 18 and other available redress possibilities.

5. Online platforms shall ensure that the decisions, referred to in paragraph 4, are not solely taken on the basis of automated means.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to provide an internal complaint-handling system allowing recipients to challenge decisions to remove content, suspend accounts, or terminate services, and to inform complainants of decisions and available redress options.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that digital services should inform affected users about actions taken (e.g. content removal for copyright infringement) and the countermeasures available to them. Article 17 directly delivers this by requiring platforms to notify complainants of decisions and available redress possibilities, and to provide an accessible internal complaint mechanism — aligning with the org's specific ask for user notification and available counter-measures following enforcement actions.

Plattformen sollten vermeiden, dass ihre Empfehlungssysteme durch skrupellose Mittel von Dritten beeinflusst werden, und sie sollten die verwendeten Algorithmen ständig schulen, um sowohl die Empfehlungsqualität als auch die Verwurzelung von rechtsverletzendem Material zu verbessern. Die Verbreitung von rechtsverletzenden Inhalten im Internet stellt für die Rechteinhaber eine große Herausforderung dar, und jegliche Werbeaktivitäten der Plattformen, auch durch die Verwendung algorithmischer Empfehlungssysteme, würden die Rechteinhaber noch weiter schädigen. 20. pairs.article_text:
Article 29 — Recommender systems
1. Very large online platforms that use recommender systems shall set out in their terms and conditions, in a clear, accessible and easily comprehensible manner, the main parameters used in their recommender systems, as well as any options for the recipients of the service to modify or influence those main parameters that they may have made available, including at least one option which is not based on profiling, within the meaning of Article 4 (4) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

2. Where several options are available pursuant to paragraph 1, very large online platforms shall provide an easily accessible functionality on their online interface allowing the recipient of the service to select and to modify at any time their preferred option for each of the recommender systems that determines the relative order of information presented to them.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to disclose the main parameters of their recommender systems in their terms and conditions, and to provide users with at least one option not based on profiling to modify those parameters.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses specifically on ensuring recommender systems do not promote copyright-infringing content and are not manipulated by third parties — a content-integrity and rights-protection concern. The provision addresses transparency and user control over recommender system parameters, which is a different angle. While both concern recommender systems, the provision does not address the org's specific ask (preventing recommendation of infringing content), and it is genuinely unclear whether this transparency/user-control mechanism would satisfy, contradict, or simply ignore the org's copyright-focused advocacy.

pairs.source_text: 19. In your view, what measures are necessary with regard to algorithmic recommender systems used by online platforms? 5000 Character (s) maximum Online-Plattformen, die algorithmische Empfehlungssysteme verwenden, sollten sicherstellen, dass sie keine urheberrechtsverletzenden Inhalte empfehlen. Plattformen sollten vermeiden, dass ihre Empfehlungssysteme durch skrupellose Mittel von Dritten beeinflusst werden, und sie sollten die verwendeten Algorithmen ständig schulen, um sowohl die Empfehlungsqualität als auch die Verwurzelung von rechtsverletzendem Material zu verbessern. Die Verbreitung von rechtsverletzenden Inhalten im Internet stellt für die Rechteinhaber eine große Herausforderung dar, und jegliche Werbeaktivitäten der Plattformen, auch durch die Verwendung algorithmischer Empfehlungssysteme, würden die Rechteinhaber noch weiter schädigen. 20. In your view, is there a need for enhanced data sharing between online platforms and authorities, within the boundaries set by the General Data Protection Regulation? Please select the appropriate situations, in your view: For supervisory purposes concerning professional users of the platform - e.g. in the context of platform intermediated services such as accommodation or ride-hailing services, for the purpose of labour inspection, for the purpose of tax collection, for the purpose of collecting social security contributions For supervisory purposes of the platforms’ own obligations – e.g
pairs.context_before: Es könnte auch ein Mechanismus vorgesehen werden, dass der Dienst einen Transparenzbericht veröffentlicht, der Statistiken über frühere Maßnahmen und die Entfernung urheberrechtsverletzender Inhalte enthalten könnte. 48
pairs.context_after: with regard to content moderation obligations, transparency requirements, actions taken in electoral contexts and against inauthentic behaviour and foreign interference Specific request of law enforcement authority or the judiciary On a voluntary and/or contractual basis in the public interest or for other purposes 21. Please explain. What would be the benefits? What would be concerns for the companies, consumers or other third parties 5000 Character (s) maximum 22. What types of sanctions would be effective, dissuasive and proportionate for online platforms which systematically fail to comply with their obligations (See also the last module of the consultation)? 5000 Character (s) maximum Die Nichteinhaltung der Sorgfaltspflicht einschließlich der Verpflichtungen in Bezug auf "Know your Business Customer“ oder Transparenzanforderungen sollte zu sinnvollen Sanktionen wie Bußgeldern führen. Das vorgeschlagene System erfordert grundsätzlich keine Änderung des Geltungsbereichs oder der Eignung der in der E-Commerce- Richtlinie festgelegten Safe Harbours. Soweit Online-Plattformen keine wirksamen Maßnahmen ergreifen, um ihre Nutzer im Falle einer Rechtsverletzung identifizieren zu können, sollte auch eine Haftung im Sinne des Art. 17 Abs. 4 DSM-RL in Erwägung 49
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to implement reasonable and proportionate mitigation measures for systemic risks, including adapting recommender systems and limiting advertisement displays, with annual best-practice reports and Commission guidelines.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that platforms using algorithmic recommender systems should ensure they do not recommend copyright-infringing content, continuously train their algorithms to reduce infringing material, and that advertising activities (including via recommender systems) should not further harm rights holders. Article 27 directly responds to this by requiring very large online platforms to adapt their recommender systems as a mitigation measure for systemic risks (Art. 27(1)(a)) and to take targeted measures limiting advertisement displays (Art. 27(1)(b)), aligning with the organisation's specific asks on both recommender systems and advertising.

pairs.source_text: In this section, the focus is on the legal architecture for the liability regime for service providers when it comes to illegal activities conducted by their users. The Commission seeks informed views on the functioning of the current liability exemption regime and the areas where an update might be necessary. 1. How important is the harmonised liability exemption for users’ illegal activities or information for the development of your company? Please rate from 1 star (not important) to 5 stars ( very important) 5 2. The liability regime for online intermediaries is primarily established in the E- Commerce Directive, which distinguishes between different types of services: so called ‘mere conduits’, ‘caching services’, and ‘hosting services’. In your understanding, are these categories sufficiently clear and complete for characterising and regulating today’s digital intermediary services? Please explain. 5000 Character (s) maximum Ja, die Definitionen und die Arten von Aktivitäten und Online-Vermittlungsdiensten, die für die Safe-Harbour-Regelung in Frage kommen, sollten unverändert bleiben. Der EU- Besitzstand sollte konsolidiert werden
pairs.context_before: gezogen werden. Die Identifizierung ist aufgrund des Abschreckungseffekts ein wirksames Mittel zur Verhinderung von Rechtsverletzungen (vgl. EUGH, 15.09.2016, Rs. C-484/14, Rn. 96 – Mc Fadden). Wer sich nicht ernsthaft bemüht dieses Mittel wirksam einzusetzen, hat nicht alle zumutbaren Anstrengungen unternommen, um (weitere) Rechtsverletzungen seiner Nutzer zu verhindern. 23. Are there other points you would like to raise? 3000 Character (s) maximum Nein. II. Reviewing the liability regime of digital services acting as intermediaries? The liability of online intermediaries is a particularly important area of internet law in Europe and worldwide. The E-Commerce Directive harmonises the liability exemptions applicable to online intermediaries in the single market, with specific provisions for different services according to their role: from Internet access providers, to messaging services, to hosting service providers. The previous section of the consultation explored obligations and responsibilities which online platforms and other services can be expected to take – i.e. processes they should put in place to address illegal activities which might be conducted by the users abusing their service. In this section, the focus is on the legal architecture for the liability regime for service providers when it comes to illegal activities conducted by their users
pairs.context_after: Der EU- Besitzstand sollte konsolidiert werden. Insbesondere sollte die derzeit in der E- Commerce-Richtlinie verwendete Unterscheidung zwischen "aktiven" und "passiven" Online-Vermittlern von der DSA nicht geändert werden, um zu vermeiden, dass das sorgfältige Gleichgewicht in der E-Commerce-Richtlinie zwischen der Notwendigkeit, die Eigentümer von Inhalten zu schützen, und der Notwendigkeit, die Entwicklung des Internets zu ermöglichen, verändert wird. Beispielsweise könnte eine engere Neudefinition der Kriterien für einen "aktiven" Vermittler in der DSA die Safe-Harbour- 50
pairs.article_text:
Article 5 — Hosting
1. Where an information society service is provided that consists of the storage of information provided by a recipient of the service the service provider shall not be liable for the information stored at the request of a recipient of the service on condition that the provider:
(a)does not have actual knowledge of illegal activity or illegal content and, as regards claims for damages, is not aware of facts or circumstances from which the illegal activity or illegal content is apparent; or
(b)upon obtaining such knowledge or awareness, acts expeditiously to remove or to disable access to the illegal content.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply where the recipient of the service is acting under the authority or the control of the provider.

3. Paragraph 1 shall not apply with respect to liability under consumer protection law of online platforms allowing consumers to conclude distance contracts with traders, where such an online platform presents the specific item of information or otherwise enables the specific transaction at issue in a way that would lead an average and reasonably well-informed consumer to believe that the information, or the product or service that is the object of the transaction, is provided either by the online platform itself or by a recipient of the service who is acting under its authority or control.

4. This Article shall not affect the possibility for a court or administrative authority, in accordance with Member States' legal systems, of requiring the service provider to terminate or prevent an infringement.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a conditional liability exemption (safe harbour) for hosting service providers who lack knowledge of illegal content or act expeditiously to remove it upon gaining such knowledge.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly states that the definitions and categories of services eligible for the safe harbour regime "should remain unchanged" and that the EU acquis should be "consolidated" — i.e., it advocates for preserving the existing E-Commerce Directive framework without modification. Article 5 of the DSA proposal, however, introduces changes and additions to the hosting safe harbour (e.g., new paragraphs on consumer protection and authority/control), which goes against the organisation's explicit position that these categories and rules should remain as they are.

pairs.article_text:
Article 11 — Legal representatives
1. Providers of intermediary services which do not have an establishment in the Union but which offer services in the Union shall designate, in writing, a legal or natural person as their legal representative in one of the Member States where the provider offers its services.

2. Providers of intermediary services shall mandate their legal representatives to be addressed in addition to or instead of the provider by the Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board on all issues necessary for the receipt of, compliance with and enforcement of decisions issued in relation to this Regulation. Providers of intermediary services shall provide their legal representative with the necessary powers and resource to cooperate with the Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board and comply with those decisions.

3. The designated legal representative can be held liable for non-compliance with obligations under this Regulation, without prejudice to the liability and legal actions that could be initiated against the provider of intermediary services.

4. Providers of intermediary services shall notify the name, address, the electronic mail address and telephone number of their legal representative to the Digital Service Coordinator in the Member State where that legal representative resides or is established. They shall ensure that that information is up to date.

5. The designation of a legal representative within the Union pursuant to paragraph 1 shall not amount to an establishment in the Union.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires providers of intermediary services without EU establishment to designate a legal representative in the Union, who can be held liable for non-compliance with the Regulation.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on the liability exemption regime (safe harbour) for intermediary services and advocates for keeping the existing E-Commerce Directive categories unchanged. It does not express any specific stance on the requirement to designate legal representatives for non-EU-established providers, which is what Article 11 establishes. The subjects are effectively unrelated.

pairs.article_title: Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
pairs.article_text:
Article 6 — Voluntary own-initiative investigations and legal compliance
1. Providers of intermediary services shall not be deemed ineligible for the exemptions from liability referred to in Articles 3, 4 and 5 solely because they carry out voluntary own-initiative investigations or other activities aimed at detecting, identifying and removing, or disabling of access to, illegal content, or take the necessary measures to comply with the requirements of Union law, including those set out in this Regulation.
pairs.provision_effect: Ensures that providers of intermediary services do not lose liability exemptions solely because they voluntarily investigate or take measures to detect and remove illegal content or comply with Union law.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation advocates that the existing safe harbour categories and definitions should remain unchanged and the EU acquis should be consolidated, suggesting a preference for preserving the status quo liability regime. Article 6 introduces a new provision clarifying that voluntary own-initiative actions do not forfeit liability exemptions — this goes beyond mere consolidation but is broadly consistent with preserving safe harbours. However, it is genuinely unclear whether the org would view this new clarification as an acceptable extension of the existing framework or as an unwanted modification to the categories they want kept intact.

pairs.source_text: Privilegien auf Dienste ausdehnen, die nach der E-Commerce-Richtlinie keinen Anspruch darauf haben sollten. Eine Änderung des Geltungsbereichs der Safe-Harbour-Privilegien würde die Rechtsprechung der EU und der Mitgliedstaaten (z.B. CJEU-Entscheidung in L'Oréal v eBay, Google France REFs) und die jüngsten legislativen Entwicklungen (z.B. die DSM-Richtlinie 2019/790) untergraben, die die Anspruchskriterien für den Safe-Harbour- Schutz hilfreich geklärt haben. Die DSA sollte diese Kriterien nicht abschwächen, sondern die Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen der auf den digitalen Märkten tätigen digitalen Dienste ergänzen und stärken. 3. Are there elements that require further legal clarification? 5000 Character (s) maximum Der DSA-Rahmen sollte Instrumente umfassen, die die Verantwortlichkeit aller Arten von digitalen Diensten verbessern und ihre Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen aktualisieren, um illegale Online-Inhalte, einschließlich Urheberrechtsverletzungen, wirksamer zu bekämpfen. Wir stellen fest, dass dies erreicht werden kann und sollte, indem die E-Commerce-Richtlinie ergänzt wird, ohne sie zu öffnen:  Sorgfaltspflicht („Duty of Care“): Alle digitalen Dienste, die zur Verletzung von Urheberrechten genutzt werden, sollten verpflichtet werden, angemessene Maßnahmen zu ergreifen, um Verstöße zu unterbinden, zu begrenzen und zu verhindern ("Sorgfaltspflicht")

pairs.context_after: Der Inhalt dieser Verantwortlichkeiten und Verpflichtungen und die Schritte, die von digitalen Diensten einschließlich des Online-Vermittlungsdienstes vernünftigerweise erwartet werden können, sollten von der Art der Dienste abhängen, aber alle Dienste einschließlich der kleineren sollten erfasst sein.  ''Notice and Stay Down“-Verpflichtung für passive Hosting-Provider, d.h. die Provider sollten sicherstellen, dass alle Kopien der gemeldeten Inhalte aus ihrem Dienst entfernt werden und nicht wieder auftauchen.  Verbesserte Transparenzverpflichtungen: Die Transparenzverpflichtungen im Rahmen der gegenwärtigen EU-Gesetzgebung sollten verbessert werden, insbesondere indem digitale Dienste verpflichtet werden, eine wirksame „Know Your Business Customer“ (KYBC)-Politik umzusetzen, und indem eine ausdrückliche Rechtsgrundlage für ein öffentliches WHOIS-Register eingeführt wird, das zumindest alle Daten enthalten sollte, die bereits nach geltendem Recht öffentlich zugänglich gemacht werden müssen (Artikel 5 der E-Commerce- Richtlinie), sowie solche anderen Informationen, die für die Rechteinhaber erforderlich sind, um rechtliche Schritte zum Schutz ihrer Urheberrechte einzuleiten. Artikel 5 sollte allgemein wirksam durchgesetzt werden, so dass alle Dienste, die mit Verbrauchern konfrontiert sind, verpflichtet sind, relevante und genaue Details an die Endbenutzer weiterzugeben.  Erweiterung der Auskunftspflichten zur Identifizierung der Nutzer (im Sinne des Art
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the scope and aims of the Digital Services Act, including a framework for conditional liability exemptions for intermediary services, due diligence obligations, and enforcement rules, while explicitly preserving existing Union law on copyright and related rights.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that the DSA should not weaken existing safe harbour criteria (as clarified by CJEU case law and the DSM Directive) and should instead complement the e-Commerce Directive by adding due diligence obligations for digital services to combat copyright infringement. Article 1 establishes a framework of conditional liability exemptions and specific due diligence obligations while explicitly preserving Union copyright law (Art. 1(5)(c)), directly responding to the organisation's core demands: maintaining existing safe harbour limits and layering new accountability/due diligence obligations on top of the existing framework without undermining it.

pairs.source_text: In your view, is this approach, balancing risks to different rights and policy objectives, still appropriate today? Is there further clarity needed as to the parameters for ‘general monitoring obligations’? Please explain. 5000 Character (s) maximum Es besteht keine Notwendigkeit, Artikel 15 der E-Commerce-Richtlinie zu ändern, der eine allgemeine Überwachung verbietet. Die Maßnahmen, zu deren Ergreifung die Vermittler verpflichtet werden sollten, stellen keine allgemeine Überwachungspflicht dar, da ihre Pflichten und Obliegenheiten "reaktiv" sind und durch tatsächliche Kenntnis von Verstößen ausgelöst werden. Dies gilt auch für die "Stay Down"-Verpflichtung für Hosting-Service-Provider, die mit Artikel 5 der E-Commerce-Richtlinie in Einklang steht. Die Gewährleistung des "Stay Down" erfordert nur bestimmte Maßnahmen und Aktionen in Bezug auf bestimmte Inhalte. Diese Position wurde im Wesentlichen vom EuGH in einem Verfahren gegen Facebook (CJEU C-18/18 - Eva Glawischnig-Piesczek) und von Gerichten auf nationaler Ebene bestätigt und ist auch im Einklang mit deutschem Recht (siehe oben). 7. Do you see any other points where an upgrade may be needed for the liability regime of digital services acting as intermediaries? 5000 Character (s) maximum Herausgabe von Werbeeinnahmen u.ä. bei illegaler Verwertung 52
pairs.context_before: digitalen Binnenmarkt, insbesondere grenzüberschreitende Maßnahmen und wirksamere Unterlassungsklagen: Da Urheberrechtsverletzungen nicht an Landesgrenzen halt machen, sollte der DSA darüber hinaus den Rechteinhabern helfen, ihre Rechte EU-weit wirksam zu schützen, ohne dass in jedem einzelnen Mitgliedstaat gegen dieselben Rechtsverletzer, denselben Inhalt und dieselbe rechtsverletzende Tätigkeit geklagt werden muss, sei es in Bezug auf rechtsverletzende Dienste direkt oder gegen digitale Dienste einschließlich Online-Vermittler. 4. Does the current legal framework dis-incentivize service providers to take proactive measures against illegal activities? If yes, please provide your view on how disincentives could be corrected. 5000 Character (s) maximum Alle kooperativen digitalen Dienste sollten ein Interesse daran haben, dass ihre Dienste nicht dazu benutzt werden, Urheberrechtsverletzungen zu begehen. 5. Do you think that the concept characterising intermediary service providers as playing a role of a 'mere technical, automatic and passive nature' in the transmission of information (recital 42 of the E-Commerce Directive) is sufficiently clear and still valid? Please explain. 5000 Character (s) maximum Ja, siehe oben. 6. The E-commerce Directive also prohibits Member States from imposing on intermediary service providers general monitoring obligations or obligations to seek facts or circumstances of illegal activities conducted on their service by their users
pairs.context_after: Bestimmte Plattformen erzielen mit den Inhalten ihrer Nutzer erhebliche Werbeeinnahmen, z.B. durch das (automatische) Einspielen von Werbespots in Videoinhalte (sog. Monetarisierung). Dieses Monetarisierungssystem umfasst zum Teil auch Inhalte, die illegal online gestellt wurden. Diese mit der illegalen Verwertung erzielten Einnahmen werden von den Plattformen regelmäßig einbehalten und nicht an den Rechteinhaber herausgegeben. Der Rechteinhaber kann zwar Löschung oder Sperrung der Inhalte verlangen, er erhält aber regelmäßig keinen Schadensersatz vom Uploader (da dieser anonym bleibt, da sein Name nicht verifiziert ist und Auskünfte über weitere Daten von der Plattform nicht herausgegeben werden). Zugleich erhält er auch von der Plattform keine Kompensation, da diese sich bezüglich Schadenersatz auf Haftungsprivilegien beruft und sich zudem sogar zur Einbehaltung der mittels der Rechtsverletzungen erzielen Werbeeinnahmen als berechtigt ansieht. Insofern sollte auf EU-Ebene zumindest sichergestellt werden, dass die im Zusammenhang mit Rechtsverletzungen erzielten Einnahmen generell nicht von den Plattformen einbehalten werden dürfen, sondern an die Rechteinhaber generell und vollständig (als Teil der Schadenskompensation) ausbezahlt werden müssen. Es darf für Online-Plattformen generell nicht von finanziellem Vorteil sein, wenn über ihre Plattform Rechtsverletzungen begangen werden
pairs.article_title: No general monitoring or active fact-finding obligations
pairs.article_text:
Article 7 — No general monitoring or active fact-finding obligations
1. No general obligation to monitor the information which providers of intermediary services transmit or store, nor actively to seek facts or circumstances indicating illegal activity shall be imposed on those providers.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes that providers of intermediary services have no general obligation to monitor transmitted or stored information, nor to actively seek out illegal activity.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly states there is no need to change Article 15 of the E-Commerce Directive (which prohibits general monitoring), and argues that obligations imposed on intermediaries — including "stay down" duties — do not constitute general monitoring obligations because they are reactive and triggered by actual knowledge. The provision directly preserves this no-general-monitoring principle, which the organisation advocated for maintaining, making this a clear case of alignment.

To what extent do you agree with the following statements? Fully Somewhat Neither Somewhat Fully I don’t agree agree agree disagree disagree know/not nor reply disagree Consumers have sufficient choices and alternatives to the offerings of online platforms. It is easy for consumers to switch between services provided by online platform companies and use same or similar services provider by other online platform companies (“multi- home”). It is easy for individuals to port their data in an useful form for alternative service providers outside of an online 54
pairs.context_before: The Communication ’Shaping Europe’s Digital Future’ also flagged that ‘competition policy alone cannot address all the systemic problems that may arise in the platform economy’. Stakeholders are invited to provide their views on potential new competition instruments through a separate, dedicated open public consultation that will be launched soon. In parallel, the Commission is also engaged in a process of reviewing EU competition rules and ensuring they are fit for the modern economy and the digital age. As part of that process, the Commission has launched a consultation on the proposal for a New Competition Tool aimed at addressing the gaps identified in enforcing competition rules. The initiative intends to address as specific objectives the structural competition problems that prevent markets from functioning properly and that can tilt the level playing field in favour of only a few market players. This could cover certain digital or digitally-enabled markets, as identified in the report by the Special Advisers and other recent reports on the role of competition policy, and/or other sectors. As such, the work on a proposed New Competition Tool and the initiative at stake complement each other. The work on the two impact assessments will be conducted in parallel in order to ensure a coherent outcome. In this context, the Commission will take into consideration the feedback received from both consultations

pairs.article_text:
Article 56 — Commitments
1. If, during proceedings under this Section, the very large online platform concerned offers commitments to ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, the Commission may by decision make those commitments binding on the very large online platform concerned and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen the proceedings:
(a)where there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)where the very large online platform concerned acts contrary to its commitments; or
(c)where the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

3. Where the Commission considers that the commitments offered by the very large online platform concerned are unable to ensure effective compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, it shall reject those commitments in a reasoned decision when concluding the proceedings.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a commitments mechanism whereby very large online platforms can offer binding commitments to ensure compliance, with the Commission able to reopen proceedings under specific conditions or reject insufficient commitments.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a survey/consultation response template with no substantive advocacy position on commitments mechanisms for very large online platforms. It contains only survey questions about consumer choice and data portability with no directional stance on the provision's subject matter.

pairs.article_text:
Article 58 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision where it finds that the very large online platform concerned does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)the relevant provisions of this Regulation;
(b)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 55;
(c)commitments made binding pursuant to Article 56,

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the very large online platform concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures that it considers taking, or that it considers that the very large online platform concerned should take, in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1 the Commission shall order the very large online platform concerned to take the necessary measures to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 within a reasonable time period and to provide information on the measures that that platform intends to take to comply with the decision.

4. The very large online platform concerned shall provide the Commission with a description of the measures it has taken to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 upon their implementation.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a non-compliance decision mechanism for the Commission to enforce obligations on very large online platforms, including ordering remedial measures.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a fragment of a survey/consultation response form containing rating scale questions about consumer choice, switching, and data portability — it contains no substantive advocacy position on non-compliance enforcement mechanisms for very large online platforms. The text is essentially administrative/boilerplate consultation material with no directional stance relevant to this provision.

pairs.source_text: the online platforms. Certain large online platform companies create barriers to entry and expansion in the Single Market (gatekeepers). Large online platforms often leverage their assets from their primary activities (customer base, data, technological solutions, skills, financial capital) to expand into other activities. When large online platform companies expand into such new activities, this often poses a risk of reducing innovation and deterring competition from smaller innovative market operators. Which characteristics are relevant in determining the gatekeeper role of large online platform companies? Please rate each criterion identified below from 1 (not relevant) to 5 (very relevant): Large user base Wide geographic coverage in the EU They capture a large share of total revenue of the market you are active/of a sector Impact on a certain sector They build on and exploit strong network effects 56

pairs.article_text:
Article 35 — Codes of conduct
1. The Commission and the Board shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of conduct at Union level to contribute to the proper application of this Regulation, taking into account in particular the specific challenges of tackling different types of illegal content and systemic risks, in accordance with Union law, in particular on competition and the protection of personal data.

2. Where significant systemic risk within the meaning of Article 26(1) emerge and concern several very large online platforms, the Commission may invite the very large online platforms concerned, other very large online platforms, other online platforms and other providers of intermediary services, as appropriate, as well as civil society organisations and other interested parties, to participate in the drawing up of codes of conduct, including by setting out commitments to take specific risk mitigation measures, as well as a regular reporting framework on any measures taken and their outcomes.

3. When giving effect to paragraphs 1 and 2, the Commission and the Board shall aim to ensure that the codes of conduct clearly set out their objectives, contain key performance indicators to measure the achievement of those objectives and take due account of the needs and interests of all interested parties, including citizens, at Union level. The Commission and the Board shall also aim to ensure that participants report regularly to the Commission and their respective Digital Service Coordinators of establishment on any measures taken and their outcomes, as measured against the key performance indicators that they contain.

4. The Commission and the Board shall assess whether the codes of conduct meet the aims specified in paragraphs 1 and 3, and shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of their objectives. They shall publish their conclusions.

5. The Board shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of the objectives of the codes of conduct, having regard to the key performance indicators that they may contain.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a framework for the Commission and the Board to encourage, facilitate, and monitor codes of conduct at Union level to address illegal content, systemic risks, and related challenges on online platforms.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text consists of survey/consultation questions (items 17–21) about data measures, media pluralism, and regulatory authority characteristics for large online platforms — it does not express a specific advocacy position on codes of conduct. There is no directional stance that can be evaluated against the provision's establishment of a codes of conduct framework.

pairs.article_text:
Article 31 — Data access and scrutiny
1. Very large online platforms shall provide the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, upon their reasoned request and within a reasonable period, specified in the request, access to data that are necessary to monitor and assess compliance with this Regulation. That Digital Services Coordinator and the Commission shall only use that data for those purposes.

2. Upon a reasoned request from the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, very large online platforms shall, within a reasonable period, as specified in the request, provide access to data to vetted researchers who meet the requirements in paragraphs 4 of this Article, for the sole purpose of conducting research that contributes to the identification and understanding of systemic risks as set out in Article 26(1).

3. Very large online platforms shall provide access to data pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2 through online databases or application programming interfaces, as appropriate.

4. In order to be vetted, researchers shall be affiliated with academic institutions, be independent from commercial interests, have proven records of expertise in the fields related to the risks investigated or related research methodologies, and shall commit and be in a capacity to preserve the specific data security and confidentiality requirements corresponding to each request.

5. The Commission shall, after consulting the Board, adopt delegated acts laying down the technical conditions under which very large online platforms are to share data pursuant to paragraphs 1 and 2 and the purposes for which the data may be used. Those delegated acts shall lay down the specific conditions under which such sharing of data with vetted researchers can take place in compliance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679, taking into account the rights and interests of the very large online platforms and the recipients of the service concerned, including the protection of confidential information, in particular trade secrets, and maintaining the security of their service.

6. Within 15 days following receipt of a request as referred to in paragraph 1 and 2, a very large online platform may request the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission, as applicable, to amend the request, where it considers that it is unable to give access to the data requested because one of following two reasons:
(a)it does not have access to the data;
(b)giving access to the data will lead to significant vulnerabilities for the security of its service or the protection of confidential information, in particular trade secrets.

7. Requests for amendment pursuant to point (b) of paragraph 6 shall contain proposals for one or more alternative means through which access may be provided to the requested data or other data which are appropriate and sufficient for the purpose of the request.
The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment or the Commission shall decide upon the request for amendment within 15 days and communicate to the very large online platform its decision and, where relevant, the amended request and the new time period to comply with the request.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a data access and scrutiny regime requiring very large online platforms to provide data to regulators and vetted academic researchers for compliance monitoring and systemic risk research, with specific conditions and safeguards.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text consists of open consultation questions (items 17–21) rather than substantive advocacy positions — it poses questions about what measures could be effective regarding data held by large platforms, media pluralism, and regulatory authority characteristics, without expressing any directional stance. This is essentially administrative/background survey text with no specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against the provision.

pairs.article_text:
Article 32 — Compliance officers
1. Very large online platforms shall appoint one or more compliance officers responsible for monitoring their compliance with this Regulation.

2. Very large online platforms shall only designate as compliance officers persons who have the professional qualifications, knowledge, experience and ability necessary to fulfil the tasks referred to in paragraph 3. Compliance officers may either be staff members of, or fulfil those tasks on the basis of a contract with, the very large online platform concerned.

3. Compliance officers shall have the following tasks:
(a)cooperating with the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment and the Commission for the purpose of this Regulation;
(b)organising and supervising the very large online platform’s activities relating to the independent audit pursuant to Article 28;
(c)informing and advising the management and employees of the very large online platform about relevant obligations under this Regulation;
(d)monitoring the very large online platform’s compliance with its obligations under this Regulation.

4. Very large online platforms shall take the necessary measures to ensure that the compliance officers can perform their tasks in an independent manner.

5. Very large online platforms shall communicate the name and contact details of the compliance officer to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment and the Commission.

6. Very large online platforms shall support the compliance officer in the performance of his or her tasks and provide him or her with the resources necessary to adequately carry out those tasks. The compliance officer shall directly report to the highest management level of the platform.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to appoint qualified compliance officers responsible for monitoring regulatory compliance, cooperating with authorities, and reporting to senior management.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org text consists of consultation survey questions about data measures, media pluralism, and regulatory authority characteristics — it contains no substantive advocacy position on compliance officers or internal compliance mechanisms. There is no directional stance that can be evaluated against this provision.

pairs.context_before: Online advertising has substantially evolved over the past years and represents a major revenue source for many digital services, as well as other businesses present online, and opens unprecedented opportunities for content creators, publishers, etc. To a large extent, maximising revenue streams and optimising online advertising is a major business incentive for the business users of the online platforms and for the data policy of the platforms. At the same time, revenues from online advertising and increased visibility and audience reach are also an important incentive for potentially harmful intents such is the case in online disinformation campaigns, for example. Another emerging issue is linked to the conclusion of so-called ‘smart contracts’ which represent an important innovation for digital and other services, but face some legal uncertainties. This section of the open public consultation seeks to collect data, information on current practices, and informed views on potential issues emerging in the space of online advertising and smart contracts, and invites the respondents to reflect on other areas where further measures may be needed to facilitate innovation in the single market. This does not address privacy and data protection concerns; it is understood that all aspects related to data sharing and data collection are to be regarded with the highest standard of protection of personal data. Online advertising 1

pairs.context_after: Werbung auf illegalen Websites stellt nach wie vor ein ernsthaftes Problem für Rechteinhaber dar, da sie es den Betreibern von rechtsverletzenden Diensten ermöglicht, Geld auf dem Rücken von urheberrechtsverletzenden Inhalten zu verdienen. Während die Werbeindustrie in den letzten Jahren in einigen Gerichtsbarkeiten aktiv war und Mechanismen eingeführt hat, um sicherzustellen, dass Werbung nicht auf illegalen Websites oder in Verbindung mit illegalen Inhalten platziert wird (z.B. gibt es freiwillige Vereinbarungen in den USA, im Vereinigten Königreich und in der EU), kann mehr getan werden. Daher sollten Gesetze eingeführt werden, um allen im Ökosystem der Online-Werbetechnologie - von Werbetreibenden, Werbeagenturen, Anzeigenmaklern bis hin zu DSPs, Börsen, Werbenetzwerken, Ad-Servern und Websites, die Werbung zeigen - Anreize zu bieten, mehr Verantwortung zu übernehmen, d.h. sie sollten einer Sorgfaltspflicht unterliegen. Markeninhaber sind in der Lage, die Anzeigenschaltung zu steuern und zu kontrollieren, wie die Werbefirmen ihre Kampagnen verwalten. Zu den vernünftigen Schritten, die von Werbetreibenden und Vermittlern der Werbebranche unternommen werden könnten, gehören die Umsetzung, Überwachung der Einhaltung und Durchsetzung geeigneter Richtlinien, die ausdrücklich verbieten, dass ihre Werbung oder Dienstleistungen dazu verwendet werden, geistige Eigentumsrechte zu verletzen oder Verletzungen geistiger Eigentumsrechte zu unterstützen oder dazu beizutragen

pairs.article_title: Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 23 — Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
1. In addition to the information referred to in Article 13, online platforms shall include in the reports referred to in that Article information on the following:
(a)the number of disputes submitted to the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies referred to in Article 18, the outcomes of the dispute settlement and the average time needed for completing the dispute settlement procedures;
(b)the number of suspensions imposed pursuant to Article 20, distinguishing between suspensions enacted for the provision of manifestly illegal content, the submission of manifestly unfounded notices and the submission of manifestly unfounded complaints;
(c)any use made of automatic means for the purpose of content moderation, including a specification of the precise purposes, indicators of the accuracy of the automated means in fulfilling those purposes and any safeguards applied.

2. Online platforms shall publish, at least once every six months, information on the average monthly active recipients of the service in each Member State, calculated as an average over the period of the past six months, in accordance with the methodology laid down in the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 25(2).

3. Online platforms shall communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, upon its request, the information referred to in paragraph 2, updated to the moment of such request. That Digital Services Coordinator may require the online platform to provide additional information as regards the calculation referred to in that paragraph, including explanations and substantiation in respect of the data used. That information shall not include personal data.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to lay down templates concerning the form, content and other details of reports pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to report on dispute settlement outcomes, suspensions, automated content moderation, and publish data on monthly active recipients.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a fragment of a consultation questionnaire response about platform workers (self-employed individuals providing services through platforms), covering topics like earnings, flexibility, and collective organisation — entirely unrelated to the DSA transparency reporting obligations for online platforms regarding content moderation and user metrics. There is no substantive advocacy position expressed on the subject matter of this provision.

pairs.article_title: Codes of conduct for online advertising
pairs.article_text:
Article 36 — Codes of conduct for online advertising
1. The Commission shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of conduct at Union level between, online platforms and other relevant service providers, such as providers of online advertising intermediary services or organisations representing recipients of the service and civil society organisations or relevant authorities to contribute to further transparency in online advertising beyond the requirements of Articles 24 and 30.

2. The Commission shall aim to ensure that the codes of conduct pursue an effective transmission of information, in full respect for the rights and interests of all parties involved, and a competitive, transparent and fair environment in online advertising, in accordance with Union and national law, in particular on competition and the protection of personal data. The Commission shall aim to ensure that the codes of conduct address at least:
(a)the transmission of information held by providers of online advertising intermediaries to recipients of the service with regard to requirements set in points (b) and (c) of Article 24;
(b)the transmission of information held by providers of online advertising intermediaries to the repositories pursuant to Article 30.

3. The Commission shall encourage the development of the codes of conduct within one year following the date of application of this Regulation and their application no later than six months after that date.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a framework for the Commission to encourage and facilitate the development of voluntary codes of conduct for online advertising transparency among online platforms, advertising intermediaries, and civil society organisations.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a reproduction of a consultation questionnaire about platform workers' conditions (earnings, flexibility, ratings, collective organisation) and contains no substantive advocacy position on online advertising transparency or codes of conduct. The subjects are entirely unrelated to the legislative provision.

pairs.article_text:
Article 10 — Points of contact
1. Providers of intermediary services shall establish a single point of contact allowing for direct communication, by electronic means, with Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board referred to in Article 47 for the application of this Regulation.

2. Providers of intermediary services shall make public the information necessary to easily identify and communicate with their single points of contact.

3. Providers of intermediary services shall specify in the information referred to in paragraph 2, the official language or languages of the Union, which can be used to communicate with their points of contact and which shall include at least one of the official languages of the Member State in which the provider of intermediary services has its main establishment or where its legal representative resides or is established.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires providers of intermediary services to establish a single point of contact for communication with authorities and to publicly disclose contact information and accepted official languages.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is a reproduction of survey/consultation questions about platform workers' conditions (earnings, flexibility, ratings, ability to organise), with no substantive advocacy position on points of contact between intermediary service providers and regulatory authorities. The subjects are entirely unrelated to Article 10's requirements.

pairs.source_text: 39. In this regard, do you see any obstacles to such negotiations? 3000 Character (s) maximum 40. Are there other points you would like to raise? 3000 Character (s) maximum VI. What governance for reinforcing the Single Market for digital services? The EU’s Single Market offers a rich potential for digital services to scale, including for innovative European companies. Today there is a certain degree of legal fragmentation in the Single Market . One of the main objectives for the Digital Services Act will be to enhance the innovation opportunities and ‘deepen the Single Market for Digital Services’. This section of the consultation intends to collect evidence and views on the current state of the single market and on steps for further improvements for a competitive and vibrant Single market for digital services. This module also inquires about the relative impact of the COVID-19 crisis on digital services in the Union. It then focuses on the appropriate governance and oversight over digital services across the EU and means to enhance the cooperation across authorities for an effective supervision of services and for the equal protection of all citizens across the single market. It also inquires about specific cooperation arrangements such as in the case of consumer protection authorities across the Single Market, or the regulatory oversight and cooperation mechanisms among media regulators. This section is not intended to focus on the enforcement of GDPR provisions

pairs.article_text:
Article 48 — Structure of the Board
1. The Board shall be composed of the Digital Services Coordinators, who shall be represented by high-level officials. Where provided for by national law, other competent authorities entrusted with specific operational responsibilities for the application and enforcement of this Regulation alongside the Digital Services Coordinator shall participate in the Board. Other national authorities may be invited to the meetings, where the issues discussed are of relevance for them.

2. Each Member State shall have one vote. The Commission shall not have voting rights.
The Board shall adopt its acts by simple majority.

3. The Board shall be chaired by the Commission. The Commission shall convene the meetings and prepare the agenda in accordance the tasks of the Board pursuant to this Regulation and with its rules of procedure.

4. The Commission shall provide administrative and analytical support for the activities of the Board pursuant to this Regulation.

5. The Board may invite experts and observers to attend its meetings, and may cooperate with other Union bodies, offices, agencies and advisory groups, as well as external experts as appropriate. The Board shall make the results of this cooperation publicly available.

6. The Board shall adopt its rules of procedure, following the consent of the Commission.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the structure, composition, voting rules, and operational procedures of the Digital Services Board, including the role of the Commission as chair and provider of administrative support.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text is essentially a reproduction of the European Commission's consultation questions and background framing, containing no specific advocacy position on the Board's structure, composition, or governance mechanics. It expresses no directional stance on how the Board should be constituted or operate, making it impossible to assess alignment or opposition with the provision.
