pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to undergo independent annual audits assessing compliance with Chapter III obligations and codes of conduct commitments, with detailed reporting and follow-up requirements.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text focuses on the need for a solid theoretical or empirical basis for regulating large platforms, particularly regarding competition harm, societal effects, data concentration, and the definition of 'large platforms.' This is a general methodological concern about the justification for regulation, not a specific advocacy position on independent auditing mechanisms. There is no directional stance on whether audits should or should not exist, or how they should be structured.

pairs.source_text: Such assessment shall focus on the type of rules that are introduced in the Digital Services Act for which it is too early to take position. It may also take into account experiences from other areas, for instance competition law for which the application of the EU rules are partly decentralised to the National Competition Agencies with a possibility for the European Commission to intervene in cases that raise issues of principle and/or cover several jurisdictions. A decentralised regime shall in any case be supported by effective coordination and cooperation mechanisms between the various national enforcement authorities. As mentioned above, our conclusion is that we want to see a system that continues to be based on the country of origin principle. This needs to be complemented by a robust system for coordination and cooperation between national authorities to avoid risks for fragmentation. Support from the European Commission in this regard is very important. This could include technical tools for enforcement cooperation, guidance documents, trainings, expert group meetings etc. We finally note that recent regulation on digital service providers has generally also targeted digital service providers established outside the
pairs.context_before: 4(5) take a careful approach to this balancing act and that any proposal should be carefully designed and based on robust evidence. 5 Self-employed individuals and platforms The National Board of Trade recognizes that there have been cases of divergent views about who should be considered an employed worker and who should be considered self-employed, within the Swedish and European platform economy. We do however find that it is important to establish that there is not only a need for reform on national level, but also a concrete need for reform on EU level before such a proposal is made. To the extent that national reforms may entail a fragmented regulatory framework for the platform economy, EU measures may be considered to address unjustified barriers to cross-border transactions. 6 Governance and enforcement The National Board of Trade sees a general need to review the governance and enforcement framework for digital services. As for the choice between centralized and decentralized enforcement (i.e. Member State enforcement vs EU level enforcement, or a combination of the two), the National Board of Trade finds it important to carefully weigh in the need for efficient mechanisms that guarantee, at the same time, a uniform application of the EU rules throughout the Union. Such assessment shall focus on the type of rules that are introduced in the Digital Services Act for which it is too early to take position
pairs.context_after: 5(5) union who provide their services in the EU. We do not consider this the wrong approach per se. We do however want to highlight that such regulation must be done in a way that is in accordance with international law, especially the rules of the World Trade Organisation. As a final and general comment, we would like to stress the importance of good legislative principles adapted to a digital environment. In our view, to complete the Digital Single Market, rules must be up-to- date, clear, flexible and well-founded. pairs.article_title: Joint investigations and requests for Commission intervention
pairs.article_text:
Article 46 — Joint investigations and requests for Commission intervention
1. Digital Services Coordinators may participate in joint investigations, which may be coordinated with the support of the Board, with regard to matters covered by this Regulation, concerning providers of intermediary services operating in several Member States.
Such joint investigations are without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the participating Digital Coordinators and the requirements applicable to the performance of those tasks and exercise of those powers provided in this Regulation. The participating Digital Services Coordinators shall make the results of the joint investigations available to other Digital Services Coordinators, the Commission and the Board through the system provided for in Article 67 for the fulfilment of their respective tasks under this Regulation.

2. Where a Digital Services Coordinator of establishment has reasons to suspect that a very large online platform infringed this Regulation, it may request the Commission to take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation in accordance with Section 3. Such a request shall contain all information listed in Article 45(2) and set out the reasons for requesting the Commission to intervene.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for Digital Services Coordinators to conduct joint investigations across Member States and request Commission intervention for very large online platforms suspected of infringing the Regulation.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a decentralised enforcement regime supported by "robust coordination and cooperation mechanisms between national authorities" and highlights the value of Commission intervention in cross-jurisdictional cases (drawing the analogy to competition law). Article 46 directly delivers this by creating joint investigation procedures among national Digital Services Coordinators and a formal mechanism for requesting Commission intervention in cross-border cases, which aligns with the organisation's specific ask for coordinated national enforcement backed by Commission support.

pairs.source_text: This is why we encourage the Commission to take a ‘precision regulation’ approach by ensuring that additional obligations and liability specifically apply to online intermediaries that function as digital platforms for the purpose of exchanging, posting, and hosting publicly accessible content or information, and have the technical means, practical ability, and the right to moderate content. Specifically, we suggest that policymakers define the scope of regulation to apply to: § Online services which disseminate content to the public such as public search engines, consumer application stores, B2C marketplaces and social media platforms. § Online services whose main function is to allow peer-to-peer interactions, including the sharing and dissemination of content between consumers. This means that we would recommend excluding online intermediaries other than online platforms, such as: § Services which do not target consumers, or § Other types of hosting services, which provide the backend infrastructure or that store content or data as part of a service provided to a company or another entity other than a natural person, such as webhosts, B2B services including enterprise cloud services, infrastructure services, DNS services, etc. 7
pairs.context_before: Additionally, certain platforms such as social media, link and share content for the purpose of generating advertising revenue, which is a very different business model than B2B services (generating revenues via on-demand/pay-as-you-go pricing; specific offering, etc.), thereby playing a very different role in the proliferation of illegal and harmful content. A one-size-fits-all approach that would impose the same rules on all digital platforms would create disproportionate burden for many businesses that do not have the ability to control and moderate content, and do not disseminate content to the public such as B2B cloud services. Such an approach would limit the uptake of cloud technologies across businesses, particularly SMEs that may pose too great of a risk for any cloud provider to be prepared to take on should we be required, for example, to proactively monitor our customers’ activities. This could lead to a chilling effect on innovation by SMEs in the EU and damage the broader data economy. a. Defining the scope of the Digital Services Act IBM believes that responsibility to tackle illegal content should rest with companies that are best placed to identify potential problems and take precautionary and remedial measures
pairs.context_after: Imposing content monitoring requirements on cloud service providers would be onerous, disproportionate and is currently not possible for technical, contractual and business model reasons, as well as having serious implications for privacy and security. Only the enterprise customers have absolute control and responsibility over their own content and the services they operate. Therefore, our ability to respond to harmful content will generally be limited to suspending an entire service given our inability to edit individual items of content. b. Know Your Customer (KYC) Policy: IBM strongly supports rules that will protect consumers by preventing dishonest businesses selling illegal products online, but such rules should avoid applying inappropriate constraints on B2B cloud services. Setting stronger consumer protection rules should first take into account the role of digital services that are an active party in the provision of a B2C goods or services, while balancing the need to safeguard the smoothness and speed of online business operations. As an example, digital services which are directed primarily at consumers, which act as the intermediary between the trader and the consumer or which provide the trading interface/platform for the online sale of consumer goods, could be considered as relevant parties
pairs.article_text:
Article 10 — Points of contact
1. Providers of intermediary services shall establish a single point of contact allowing for direct communication, by electronic means, with Member States’ authorities, the Commission and the Board referred to in Article 47 for the application of this Regulation.

2. Providers of intermediary services shall make public the information necessary to easily identify and communicate with their single points of contact.

3. Providers of intermediary services shall specify in the information referred to in paragraph 2, the official language or languages of the Union, which can be used to communicate with their points of contact and which shall include at least one of the official languages of the Member State in which the provider of intermediary services has its main establishment or where its legal representative resides or is established.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires all providers of intermediary services to establish a single point of contact for communication with authorities, the Commission, and the Board, and to make relevant contact information publicly available.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: IBM's advocacy position focuses specifically on narrowing the scope of regulation to apply only to consumer-facing online platforms and excluding B2B/infrastructure services like enterprise cloud and DNS. The provision establishes a points-of-contact requirement for intermediary services broadly, which is an operational/administrative obligation unrelated to IBM's specific ask about scoping and exclusions. IBM expresses no position on contact point requirements.

pairs.source_text: If new rules on automated decision making are considered in the context of content moderation, they should avoid duplication or contradiction with recently adopted provisions in the Omnibus Directive, the Platform-to-Business Relations Regulation, the Copyright Directive and other related legislation. ● How should the spread of illegal goods, services or content across multiple platforms and services be addressed? Are there specific provisions necessary for addressing risks brought by: (a) Digital services established outside of the Union? (b) Sellers established outside of the Union, who reach EU consumers through online platforms? These are essentially enforcement issues that should be dealt with through improved cooperation with 3rd countries, customs and market inspectors. The appearance of new innovative business models is taking place in all regulated sectors These new entrants represent a break with the technological model on which laws and regulations traditionally were based. Therefore, regulation must adapt. Any provider operating in Europe must comply with the same duties and obligations of the country in which it provides its services, regardless of whether it is established or makes its decisions there. ● What would be appropriate and proportionate measures that digital services acting as online intermediaries, other than online platforms, should take – e.g
pairs.context_before: Audio-visual piracy and fake products and services sold on the internet are a very real and frequent problem. All type of digital and physical counterfeits are offered on the internet in an industrialized and massive way. This causes revenue reduction and brand reputation damage to all affected companies while consumers blame brands for not protecting them and their products and services from counterfeiters. In this context, legal measures so far have proven to be insufficient, late, ineffective and costly. An alternative technological tools and measures for removing illegal goods, content and services online is a practical solution to help both companies affected and users to be safe in the Digital Single Market. These technological tools can help detect and remove illegal content and products on the internet, searching continuously for infringements and requesting its removal in an automated and effective way. In the case of audio-visual content, these automatic tools can also expedite the process of identifying illegal content, goods or services online to speed up the removal process. Nevertheless, these systems need to include complaint and redress mechanisms and need to provide guarantees of transparency to avoid over blocking, false positives and possible discrimination. 9
pairs.context_after: ● What would be appropriate and proportionate measures that digital services acting as online intermediaries, other than online platforms, should take – e.g. other types of hosting services, such as web hosts, or services deeper in the Internet stack, like cloud infrastructure services, content distribution services, DNS services, etc.? Rules appropriate for one type of service may be inappropriate for others. What makes sense for content-sharing platforms may not be appropriate, or technically feasible, for a platform that hosts mobile apps. For example, cloud infrastructure and enterprise services tend to have less control (legal or technical) over third-party content on their systems, and in many cases do not have a direct relationship (contractual or otherwise) with those who upload the content. Cloud customers own their data and cloud providers process it based on their instructions. To the extent harmful content exists, obligations to remove or disable access to the content should generally fall first upon the enterprise, third-party provider, or other entity with the most direct relationship with—and therefore the closest proximity to—the person who uploaded the content. 10
pairs.article_text:
Article 35 — Codes of conduct
1. The Commission and the Board shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of conduct at Union level to contribute to the proper application of this Regulation, taking into account in particular the specific challenges of tackling different types of illegal content and systemic risks, in accordance with Union law, in particular on competition and the protection of personal data.

2. Where significant systemic risk within the meaning of Article 26(1) emerge and concern several very large online platforms, the Commission may invite the very large online platforms concerned, other very large online platforms, other online platforms and other providers of intermediary services, as appropriate, as well as civil society organisations and other interested parties, to participate in the drawing up of codes of conduct, including by setting out commitments to take specific risk mitigation measures, as well as a regular reporting framework on any measures taken and their outcomes.

3. When giving effect to paragraphs 1 and 2, the Commission and the Board shall aim to ensure that the codes of conduct clearly set out their objectives, contain key performance indicators to measure the achievement of those objectives and take due account of the needs and interests of all interested parties, including citizens, at Union level. The Commission and the Board shall also aim to ensure that participants report regularly to the Commission and their respective Digital Service Coordinators of establishment on any measures taken and their outcomes, as measured against the key performance indicators that they contain.

4. The Commission and the Board shall assess whether the codes of conduct meet the aims specified in paragraphs 1 and 3, and shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of their objectives. They shall publish their conclusions.

5. The Board shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of the objectives of the codes of conduct, having regard to the key performance indicators that they may contain.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a framework for the Commission and the Board to encourage, facilitate, and assess voluntary codes of conduct at Union level to address illegal content and systemic risks, including KPIs and regular reporting.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's text does not express a specific advocacy position on codes of conduct. Its positions concern avoiding duplication with existing legislation, enforcement of rules for non-EU providers, and proportionate measures for intermediaries — none of which directly address the voluntary codes of conduct mechanism established in Article 35. There is no substantive stance on whether such codes should or should not exist, or how they should be structured.

pairs.source_text: Member State where they are based. Their role would be to identify illegal online content goods and services in order to inform competent national and/or European authorities of their existence and to have these administrative bodies order their removal if the platform has not done so after direct notification of the Trusted flagger. EU Member states should improve cooperation, both, between Member States, by sharing their official lists of validated national trusted flaggers and with third parties when illegal content is identified in order to swift action. 12
pairs.context_before: Customs and market surveillance authorities lack the resources to tackle bad actors in both the offline and online world. We, therefore, urge the Commission to raise awareness and enforcement of product safety and IPR infringements within Europe. Intergovernmental cooperation to act against sellers outside the EU can also be helpful. In order to facilitate expeditious removal, when authorities discover unsafe or counterfeit products, they must provide clear, structured, actionable information to online services. This includes minimum data requirements for RAPEX notices in SafetyGate or specific tracking numbers from seized packages. Blacklists of identified illegal content – e.g. used for blocking – could also be approved by public authorities to ensure legal certainty. Independent and reliable notifiers could be helpful to identify the presence of illegal content online and ensure more effective enforcement against such content and speed up the removal process. This is only relevant for hosting service providers that allow sharing of user-generated-content with the public or broad audience. However, these independent third-parties, such as Trusted Flaggers, should be duly accredited by the 11
pairs.context_after: II. The liability regime Any new legislation should take into account the difference between various business models and the degree of knowledge or control a company or a service have over the content. A more principle-based approach would provide the needed flexibility to better determine a company’s role in content moderation. Adigital is well aware of the responsibility companies hold given the effects online services can have on the society. However, greater responsibility should be first and foremost placed on those that can curate the content. Indeed, not all online platforms occupy the same space in the digital ecosystem: a one-size-fits-all approach that would place the same obligations on all digital services would create disproportionate burdens for many businesses that do not have the ability to access and moderate the content. Such an approach would damage the broader data economy. Any proposals should therefore clearly define services (or activities) in scope and those that are not. A new framework of responsibility could encourage new roles and responsibilities for online service providers to proactively tackle illegal content, encouraging experimentation with what works and how companies can go the extra mile while respecting the unique features of the services and without losing the umbrella of limited liability
pairs.article_title: Notification of suspicions of criminal offences
pairs.article_text:
Article 21 — Notification of suspicions of criminal offences
1. Where an online platform becomes aware of any information giving rise to a suspicion that a serious criminal offence involving a threat to the life or safety of persons has taken place, is taking place or is likely to take place, it shall promptly inform the law enforcement or judicial authorities of the Member State or Member States concerned of its suspicion and provide all relevant information available.

2. Where the online platform cannot identify with reasonable certainty the Member State concerned, it shall inform the law enforcement authorities of the Member State in which it is established or has its legal representative or inform Europol.
For the purpose of this Article, the Member State concerned shall be the Member State where the offence is suspected to have taken place, be taking place and likely to take place, or the Member State where the suspected offender resides or is located, or the Member State where the victim of the suspected offence resides or is located.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to promptly notify law enforcement or judicial authorities when they become aware of information suggesting a serious criminal offence involving a threat to life or safety has occurred or is likely to occur.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: Both the provision and the org position concern the reporting of illegal content/offences to competent authorities, showing a real thematic connection. However, the org's position focuses specifically on a "trusted flagger" mechanism for identifying illegal content and improving cross-border cooperation between Member States, whereas the provision establishes a direct platform-to-law-enforcement notification obligation for serious criminal offences — it is genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply bypasses the org's specific advocacy on trusted flaggers and inter-Member State cooperation.

pairs.source_text: We need appropriate interpretation of these concepts within the specific context of digital markets.. As stated in the joint memorandum of the Benelux competition authorities, DG COMP could provide ex ante guidance on specific issues that represent a particular challenge in the digital markets, such as self-preferencing. The EC should also analyse the impact of the Platform to Business regulation on relationships between online intermediaries and their business users. This includes concerns over differentiated treatment, access to data, self-preferencing and imbalance in negotiating positions. ➔ Speed up antitrust enforcement As demonstrated in the recent Broadcom case, the European Commission could use more extensively interim measures limited in time to prevent serious damage for competition from occurring in certain markets. The Commission could also impose time limits for the negotiation of remedies to avoid unnecessary delays in cases that can alter the structure of the affected markets. It is also worth remembering the pace of enforcement is directly linked to the need to reach robust and evidence-based decisions, as wrong decisions may negatively impact the competition in the market. The ex-ante framework should not be used as a way to promote faster decision-making at the expense of robust analysis, especially when these decisions involve complex, multi-sided markets
pairs.context_before: In 2019, 158 companies have grown to an evaluation of over 1bn USD, many of them being online platforms, marketplaces and certainly all being data-driven. As a general remark, there is a risk of overlap between the ex ante legal framework for online platforms acting as gatekeepers and the new competition tool. ➔ Determination of perceived gatekeeper platforms Introducing a new legal concept of "gatekeeper", vaguely defined, will result in legal uncertainty. Also, weak competition is not only related to the market share/size of companies. Clear legal definitions and certainty for business are needed to create an environment conducive to innovation and business growth. Moreover, there are a number of actions that could contribute to addressing the concerns underlined by the European Commission: ➔ Addressing widespread practices in digital markets Most practices that have attracted regulatory attention, such as bundling, tying or self- preferencing are well known and apply to various economic sectors, including digital markets. 15
pairs.context_after: The ex-ante framework should not be used as a way to promote faster decision-making at the expense of robust analysis, especially when these decisions involve complex, multi-sided markets. ➔ Measures pertaining to online platforms The European Commission in the inception impact assessments and in its Communications on shaping the EU’s digital future and the Data strategy, identifies the accumulation of data as a key factor for the creation of economies of scope and scale, the existence of strong network effects, in essence for the success of a handful of large online platforms. Therefore, the European Commission should move swiftly with the creation of “European dataspaces” as envisioned in the Data Strategy and support the creation of “data pools” in Europe. At the same time, building on Article 20 of the GDPR, the European Commission could bring forward legislation on data sharing and data access in compliance with data protection rules as well as IP and trade secrets. The European Commission should also promote standardisation for interoperability of datasets in consultation with the industry. 16
pairs.article_text:
Article 55 — Interim measures
1. In the context of proceedings which may lead to the adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 58(1), where there is an urgency due to the risk of serious damage for the recipients of the service, the Commission may, by decision, order interim measures against the very large online platform concerned on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement.

2. A decision under paragraph 1 shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
pairs.provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to order interim measures against very large online platforms on a prima facie finding of infringement where there is urgency due to risk of serious damage to recipients of the service.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: Adigital explicitly advocates for the Commission to "use more extensively interim measures limited in time to prevent serious damage for competition," which directly mirrors Article 55's mechanism allowing the Commission to order time-limited interim measures on a prima facie finding of infringement where there is urgency due to risk of serious damage. The provision delivers precisely what the organisation requested — a formal, time-limited interim measures tool for urgent situations.

pairs.organisation: Department of Business, Enterprise and Innovation
pairs.source_text: 1. The e-Commerce Directive related strand Key Points Ireland supports an approach to the development of a Digital Services Act package which supports business and innovation while protecting consumers. We urge that measures taken should be clearly evidence based, targeted to deal with specific concerns, and proportionate to the harms that they wish to prevent. We believe that the appropriate governance framework has at its head a European oversight entity capable of ensuring cohesion and collaboration between Member States and consistency of approach between all measures taken without any MS having a disproportionate administrative and regulatory burden. We believe that the main principles of the e-Commerce Directive have stood the test of time and that the framework does not need to be changed but to be added to with specific requirements relevant to the types of services and issues that have presented themselves. In order to address the tensions that naturally arise between the country of origin principle and the measures within national legislation of destination countries, we suggest that the system that is adopted to achieve cooperation should also involve collaboration between Member States and recourse to European oversight in order to ensure that no disproportionate burden falls on the regulatory body of one, or a small number of, Member States
pairs.context_before: Ref. Ares(2020)4777648 - 12/09/2020 The Irish position on the EU Commission’s proposed Digital Services Act package - Submission to the Public Consultation 8 September 2020 Prepared by the Department of Business, Enterprise and Innovation dbei.gov.ie
pairs.context_after: Ireland believes that an EU Authority or Commission based entity will help to provide much needed cohesion, collaboration and consistency and that the Commission should act in accordance with these goals in devising the Digital Services Act package. It is felt that it is important that the Commission appreciates real issues that arise from the specifics of individual Member States legal systems when implementing enforcement elements of its EU Regulations and learnings from these should be incorporated into the Digital Services Act Package proposals. We would encourage the Commission to listen to and take on board Member States concerns in this regard and to accept the need to actively manage areas where there is likely to be division between MS in order to promote the EU as an effective Union. The need for a DSA Ireland appreciates the need for the revisiting of the e-Commerce Directive. While we believe that the main principles of the Directive have stood the test of time, the environment to which it applies has evolved considerably in the past twenty years. Information Society Service Providers (ISSPs) are no longer merely in the business of facilitating the posting of information online by their users. A significant number of new uses have developed on the internet and with those have come varying characteristics for the ISSPs providing them. This gives rise to new considerations that need to be taken into account when applying both —— 2
pairs.article_title: Cross-border cooperation among Digital Services Coordinators
pairs.article_text:
Article 45 — Cross-border cooperation among Digital Services Coordinators
1. Where a Digital Services Coordinator has reasons to suspect that a provider of an intermediary service, not under the jurisdiction of the Member State concerned, infringed this Regulation, it shall request the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment to assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation.
Where the Board has reasons to suspect that a provider of intermediary services infringed this Regulation in a manner involving at least three Member States, it may recommend the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment to assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation.

2. A request or recommendation pursuant to paragraph 1 shall at least indicate:
(a)the point of contact of the provider of the intermediary services concerned as provided for in Article 10;
(b)a description of the relevant facts, the provisions of this Regulation concerned and the reasons why the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, suspects that the provider infringed this Regulation;
(c)any other information that the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, considers relevant, including, where appropriate, information gathered on its own initiative or suggestions for specific investigatory or enforcement measures to be taken, including interim measures.

3. The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall take into utmost account the request or recommendation pursuant to paragraph 1. Where it considers that it has insufficient information to act upon the request or recommendation and has reasons to consider that the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, could provide additional information, it may request such information. The time period laid down in paragraph 4 shall be suspended until that additional information is provided.

4. The Digital Services Coordinator of establishment shall, without undue delay and in any event not later than two months following receipt of the request or recommendation, communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or the Board, its assessment of the suspected infringement, or that of any other competent authority pursuant to national law where relevant, and an explanation of any investigatory or enforcement measures taken or envisaged in relation thereto to ensure compliance with this Regulation.

5. Where the Digital Services Coordinator that sent the request, or, where appropriate, the Board, did not receive a reply within the time period laid down in paragraph 4 or where it does not agree with the assessment of the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, it may refer the matter to the Commission, providing all relevant information. That information shall include at least the request or recommendation sent to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, any additional information provided pursuant to paragraph 3 and the communication referred to in paragraph 4.

6. The Commission shall assess the matter within three months following the referral of the matter pursuant to paragraph 5, after having consulted the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment and, unless it referred the matter itself, the Board.

7. Where, pursuant to paragraph 6, the Commission concludes that the assessment or the investigatory or enforcement measures taken or envisaged pursuant to paragraph 4 are incompatible with this Regulation, it shall request the Digital Service Coordinator of establishment to further assess the matter and take the necessary investigatory or enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation, and to inform it about those measures taken within two months from that request.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a cross-border cooperation mechanism among Digital Services Coordinators, including escalation to the Commission, to ensure enforcement of the Regulation across Member States.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a cooperation system involving collaboration between Member States and recourse to European oversight to prevent a disproportionate regulatory burden falling on any one Member State — directly mirroring the provision's cross-border cooperation framework with escalation to the Commission. The provision concretely delivers the mechanism the organisation called for, including European-level oversight (via the Commission) to resolve disputes between national Digital Services Coordinators.

pairs.article_text:
Article 39 — Requirements for Digital Services Coordinators
1. Member States shall ensure that their Digital Services Coordinators perform their tasks under this Regulation in an impartial, transparent and timely manner. Member States shall ensure that their Digital Services Coordinators have adequate technical, financial and human resources to carry out their tasks.

2. When carrying out their tasks and exercising their powers in accordance with this Regulation, the Digital Services Coordinators shall act with complete independence. They shall remain free from any external influence, whether direct or indirect, and shall neither seek nor take instructions from any other public authority or any private party.

3. Paragraph 2 is without prejudice to the tasks of Digital Services Coordinators within the system of supervision and enforcement provided for in this Regulation and the cooperation with other competent authorities in accordance with Article 38(2). Paragraph 2 shall not prevent supervision of the authorities concerned in accordance with national constitutional law.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes requirements for Digital Services Coordinators, including impartiality, transparency, timeliness, adequate resourcing, and full independence from external influence.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on high-level governance principles — European oversight, collaboration between Member States, proportionality, and avoiding disproportionate burdens on any single Member State's regulatory body — without taking any specific stance on the independence, resourcing, or operational requirements of Digital Services Coordinators. The org text is too general and directionally broad to constitute a specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against this provision's concrete requirements.

pairs.article_title: Joint investigations and requests for Commission intervention
pairs.article_text:
Article 46 — Joint investigations and requests for Commission intervention
1. Digital Services Coordinators may participate in joint investigations, which may be coordinated with the support of the Board, with regard to matters covered by this Regulation, concerning providers of intermediary services operating in several Member States.
Such joint investigations are without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the participating Digital Coordinators and the requirements applicable to the performance of those tasks and exercise of those powers provided in this Regulation. The participating Digital Services Coordinators shall make the results of the joint investigations available to other Digital Services Coordinators, the Commission and the Board through the system provided for in Article 67 for the fulfilment of their respective tasks under this Regulation.

2. Where a Digital Services Coordinator of establishment has reasons to suspect that a very large online platform infringed this Regulation, it may request the Commission to take the necessary investigatory and enforcement measures to ensure compliance with this Regulation in accordance with Section 3. Such a request shall contain all information listed in Article 45(2) and set out the reasons for requesting the Commission to intervene.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for joint investigations by Digital Services Coordinators across Member States and allows a DSC to request Commission intervention for enforcement against very large online platforms.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a cooperative system involving collaboration between Member States and recourse to European oversight to prevent a disproportionate burden falling on the regulatory body of one or a small number of Member States. Article 46 directly delivers this by enabling joint investigations coordinated across DSCs with Board support, and by allowing a DSC to escalate enforcement to the Commission — precisely the kind of European-level oversight and burden-sharing the organisation called for.

pairs.article_text:
Article 43 — Right to lodge a complaint
1. Recipients of the service shall have the right to lodge a complaint against providers of intermediary services alleging an infringement of this Regulation with the Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State where the recipient resides or is established. The Digital Services Coordinator shall assess the complaint and, where appropriate, transmit it to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment. Where the complaint falls under the responsibility of another competent authority in its Member State, the Digital Service Coordinator receiving the complaint shall transmit it to that authority.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the right for recipients of intermediary services to lodge complaints against providers with the Digital Services Coordinator of their Member State of residence or establishment, which then routes the complaint appropriately.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text expresses general support for a well-governed Digital Services Act with European oversight, proportionality, and balanced burden-sharing between Member States, but does not take any specific directional stance on complaint mechanisms or recipients' rights to lodge complaints. The position is too broad and general to constitute a specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against this particular provision.

pairs.source_text: These minimum technical standards should be developed through a multistakeholder process, and help overcome the numerous problems and bugs that render the existing ad libraries meaningless as transparency tools. The ad repositories should comply with well-defined accessibility and technical standards standards. We suggest the following starting point for these standards9: 4 Ibid. 5 See for instance the following studies by the EU DisinfoLab. EU DisinfoLab (2019): How you thought you support the animals and you ended up funding white supremacists. Available here. EU DisinfoLab (2020): Intra-EU Disinformation: the French website managed by a Polish far-right network. Available here. 6 See for instance this recent EU DisinfoLab investigation: How two information portals hide their ties to the Russian news agency InfoRos. Available here. 7 See Paddy Leerssen in Panoptykon (2020): Who really targets you? Facebook in Polish election campaigns. Available here. 8 For further information about these ideas, contact the Panoptykon Foundation. 9 These recommendations are based on the guidelines for effective ad archives issued by Mozilla and a cohort of independent researchers. Available here.
pairs.context_before: Transparency on the advertiser, engagement and targeting criteria is only one part of a company’s advertising strategy and therefore does not imply a disclosure of trade secrets. Transparency mechanisms would have to be built into the platforms in an easy-to-use format so that it doesn’t prove a burden for advertisers. Such public-facing transparency is a necessary yet in itself insufficient first step for enhancing the accountability of platforms and advertisers. While the measures described below will enhance the transparency of advertisers, this needs to go alongside transparency of the ad optimisation processes on the part of the platforms, as well as user-level transparency explaining why exactly an ad is reaching them individually.8 Transparency in and of itself is only instrumental to accountability and needs to therefore be backed up with further action to safeguard rights and democratic processes online. For example, transparency may reveal widespread harmful practices that may in fact be prohibited but have escaped meaningful enforcement, or even novel practices that should be regulated. WHAT UNIVERSAL TRANSPARENCY BY DEFAULT LOOKS LIKE Mandatory, functional ad libraries: The European Commission should foster the development of and issue minimum technical standards for advertisement libraries for digital platforms, covering both the design and functioning of ad libraries
pairs.context_after: » Libraries provided by each platform should be compatible with each other. There should be a unique set of standards and protocols provided by the Commission that all platforms are required to use.10 » Platforms should assign unique identifiers to each advertisement and advertiser to allow for trend analysis over time and across platforms. Advertisers should keep the same unique identifier no matter what platform they’re using. » All images, videos, and other content should be provided in a machine-readable format accessible via an application programmatic interface. This should include any words used in images or in audio provided as searchable text. » The ability to download a week’s worth of data in less than 12 hours and a day’s worth of data in less than two hours. » Bulk downloading functionality of all relevant content. It should be feasible to download all historical data within one week. » Search functionality by the text of the content itself, by the content author or by date range. » Up-to-date and historical data access, including the availability of advertisements within 24 hours of publication; the availability of advertisements going back 10 years. In addition, APIs should be promptly fixed when they are broken and APIs should be designed so that they either support or at least do not impede long-term studies » The API itself and any data collected from the API should be accessible to and shareable with the general public
pairs.provision_effect: Requires the Commission to support and promote the development and implementation of voluntary industry standards for ad repositories, interoperability, and related transparency mechanisms.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for minimum technical standards for ad repositories developed through a multistakeholder process, with well-defined accessibility and technical standards to make ad libraries functional as transparency tools. The provision directly responds to this by mandating Commission support for the development of voluntary standards covering ad repositories and interoperability (Article 34(1)(e)), aligning with the org's specific ask for standardisation of these tools.

pairs.source_text: » Targeting mechanism: use of lookalike audiences, and which audiences they chose; use of profiling based on imported datasets and the source of this data (such as a newsletter platform, for instance); or other similar mechanisms to improve targeting by the platforms. » Targeting and delivery criteria, with the same level of granularity as the advertiser can choose from. This must include the optimisation goal selected by the advertiser and general information on the optimisation logic used by the platform (possibly in another layer/interface that is accessible from the ad library). » Audience reached » Engagement and reach in absolute and relative terms, e.g. likes, shares, comments » The ad library should include this information for all ads, including the ads taken down by the platforms because they did not adhere to community standards. With the exception of content judged illegal by the relevant state authorities, banned ads should remain in the ad repository for public scrutiny. For ads that were taken down, information about the kind and category of content, and the reasons and process for take-down should be displayed. » None of these measures should reveal personal user information and all of them should be GDPR compliant. Real-time transparency disclosures for individual users: There should be clear, consistently applied on- screen designations of ads, distinguishing them from other content
pairs.context_before: Information on the funding entity should also be disclosed and verified. » Advertiser identification: Platforms should facilitate the linkage with other databases that support verification by displaying official identification such as corporate registers, advertisers’ tax ID, political candidates’ electoral court declaration, or any other identification number that facilitates enforcement and verification of the advertisers’ identity. 10 For an example, see this universal transparency schema Google has created here. 11 For an example, see this definition by European Digital Rights of dominant platforms (p.16) here.
pairs.context_after: Real-time transparency disclosures for individual users: There should be clear, consistently applied on- screen designations of ads, distinguishing them from other content. Users should have easy access to easily comprehensible basic transparency information, as well as easy access to the above-mentioned transparency information. Furthermore, access to a personal ad library showing users who is targeting them and how, would allow users to better hold platforms to account. The design of this access and the information presented should be at least as accessible as other platform features. Companies should show evidence of the design process and provide information on user interactions with it on request. Verification of advertisers: Platforms and political advertisers need to be held to account for verifying all advertisers’ real identity, who’s paying (indirectly) for the ads, contact details and for political advertisers a reference to their declaration to the electoral authorities (when applicable in the country context). Such verification needs to be quick and mandatory. It should not rely on self-declaration by the advertiser but require the platforms to verify the information provided. It also needs to be more closely monitored by authorities, to ensure platforms perform better than they did as part of their efforts for the Code of Practice, with appropriate sanctions available for advertisers and platforms that do not stick to the rules
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to maintain and publicly expose (via API) an ad repository containing advertiser identity, display period, targeting parameters, and audience reach figures, without personal data of recipients.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a public ad repository covering targeting criteria, audience reached, advertiser identity, and GDPR-compliant disclosure — all of which the provision directly delivers. The provision's requirements (Article 30(2)(a)–(e)) map closely onto the organisation's specific asks: advertiser identity (b), targeting parameters (d), audience reached (e), display period (c), and the exclusion of personal data, reflecting the org's GDPR compliance demand.

pairs.context_after: Anonymity where needed to protect safety: We encourage the European Commission to issue guidelines for platforms to protect advertisers in high risk contexts. The European Commission could make suggestions for a mechanism for advertisers to anonymise their identity on the basis of political threats and risk, for public interest actors such as human rights defenders and activists. Such an anonymity mechanism would for instance protect organisations raising awareness on LGBTQ+ rights in countries where those rights cannot be taken for granted. This mechanism, meant to protect those in need of anonymity, could be abused as a loophole by advertisers trying to hide their identity, even though they do not have to fear prosecution. Therefore, consideration should be given to independent mechanisms to oversee the granting of anonymity. Exemption applications should be carefully scrutinised according to a transparent set of criteria and information should be made publicly available on the number of exemptions requested and granted on an annual/quarterly basis. Binding requirement and enforcement: The Commission should develop a mechanism for ensuring universal transparency for online advertising meets the standards set out above. This should include relevant penalties for non-compliance up to and including preventing a platform from running any ads if their efforts in this area are deemed insufficient.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to ensure recipients can identify in real time, for each specific advertisement, that it is an advertisement, who is behind it, and the main parameters used to target them.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for real-time, on-screen transparency disclosures for individual users identifying ads, who is behind them, and how they are targeted — which maps directly onto Article 24's three requirements (ad identification, advertiser identity, and targeting parameters). The provision delivers concretely what the org asked for in terms of individual-level, real-time ad transparency.

pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)4777638 - 12/09/2020 Konsultation zum Digital Services Act Stellungnahme des Ausschusses „Europäische Union“ der deutschen Justizminister- konferenz (gefasst mit 13 der 16 Stimmen) A. Umgang mit strafbaren Inhalten 1. Aktuelle Rechtslage im deutschen Recht a. Netzwerkdurchsetzungsgesetz (NetzDG) Die Behandlung von strafbaren Inhalten auf Internetplattformen wird aktuell im deut- schen Recht in den von der E-Commerce-Richtlinie vorgegebenen Grenzen u.a. durch das Netzwerkdurchsetzungsgesetz (NetzDG) geregelt: Das Gesetz richtet sich an soziale Netzwerke, d.h. Telemediendiensteanbieter, die mit Gewinnerzielungsabsicht Plattformen im Internet betreiben, die dazu bestimmt sind, dass Nutzer beliebige Inhalte mit anderen Nutzern teilen oder der Öffentlichkeit zugänglich machen. Soziale Netzwerke mit mehr als zwei Millionen Nutzern im Inland sind danach insbe- sondere verpflichtet, ein wirksames und transparentes Verfahren für den Umgang mit und die Übermittlung von Beschwerden von Nutzern über rechtswidrige Inhalte vorzuhalten. Ein Inhalt ist dann rechtswidrig im Sinne des NetzDG, wenn er einen der im Gesetz ausdrücklich aufgelisteten Straftatbestände erfüllt (u.a. Volksverhetzung, Beleidigung oder Kinderpornografie). Das soziale Netzwerk ist verpflichtet, jede Beschwerde zu prüfen und offensichtlich rechtswidrige Inhalte innerhalb von 24 Stunden sowie sonstige rechtswidrige Inhalte innerhalb von sieben Tagen zu löschen oder den Zugang hierzu zu sperren
pairs.context_after: Bußgeldbewehrt sind dabei nur systemische Verstöße gegen die Regeln zum Be- schwerde- und Löschungsverfahren, nicht dagegen die Unterlassung einer an sich gebotenen Löschung im Einzelfall. Nutzern, die mit der Löschung oder der unterblie- benen Löschung eines Inhalts nicht einverstanden sind, steht der Rechtsweg zu den staatlichen Gerichten offen. b. pairs.article_title: Orders to act against illegal content
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes requirements for national judicial/administrative orders to act against illegal content, including mandatory elements such as statements of reasons, URL identification, redress information, territorial scope limits, and notification obligations for intermediary service providers.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text describes the current state of German law (NetzDG) regarding illegal content on platforms — specifically user complaint procedures and takedown timelines — without expressing a specific advocacy position on how orders from judicial or administrative authorities to intermediary services should be structured. The text is descriptive background on existing national law, not a directional stance on the provision's subject matter of formal authority orders and their procedural requirements.

pairs.source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)4777638 - 12/09/2020 Konsultation zum Digital Services Act Stellungnahme des Ausschusses „Europäische Union“ der deutschen Justizminister- konferenz (gefasst mit 13 der 16 Stimmen) A. Umgang mit strafbaren Inhalten 1. Aktuelle Rechtslage im deutschen Recht a. Netzwerkdurchsetzungsgesetz (NetzDG) Die Behandlung von strafbaren Inhalten auf Internetplattformen wird aktuell im deut- schen Recht in den von der E-Commerce-Richtlinie vorgegebenen Grenzen u.a. durch das Netzwerkdurchsetzungsgesetz (NetzDG) geregelt: Das Gesetz richtet sich an soziale Netzwerke, d.h. Telemediendiensteanbieter, die mit Gewinnerzielungsabsicht Plattformen im Internet betreiben, die dazu bestimmt sind, dass Nutzer beliebige Inhalte mit anderen Nutzern teilen oder der Öffentlichkeit zugänglich machen. Soziale Netzwerke mit mehr als zwei Millionen Nutzern im Inland sind danach insbe- sondere verpflichtet, ein wirksames und transparentes Verfahren für den Umgang mit und die Übermittlung von Beschwerden von Nutzern über rechtswidrige Inhalte vorzuhalten. Ein Inhalt ist dann rechtswidrig im Sinne des NetzDG, wenn er einen der im Gesetz ausdrücklich aufgelisteten Straftatbestände erfüllt (u.a. Volksverhetzung, Beleidigung oder Kinderpornografie). Das soziale Netzwerk ist verpflichtet, jede Beschwerde zu prüfen und offensichtlich rechtswidrige Inhalte innerhalb von 24 Stunden sowie sonstige rechtswidrige Inhalte innerhalb von sieben Tagen zu löschen oder den Zugang hierzu zu sperren
pairs.context_after: Bußgeldbewehrt sind dabei nur systemische Verstöße gegen die Regeln zum Be- schwerde- und Löschungsverfahren, nicht dagegen die Unterlassung einer an sich gebotenen Löschung im Einzelfall. Nutzern, die mit der Löschung oder der unterblie- benen Löschung eines Inhalts nicht einverstanden sind, steht der Rechtsweg zu den staatlichen Gerichten offen. b. Gesetzesnovellierungen Aktuell wird das NetzDG an verschiedenen Stellen überarbeitet. Hervorzuheben ist insbesondere, dass zur Intensivierung der Strafverfolgung bei
pairs.article_text:
Article 17 — Internal complaint-handling system
1. Online platforms shall provide recipients of the service, for a period of at least six months following the decision referred to in this paragraph, the access to an effective internal complaint-handling system, which enables the complaints to be lodged electronically and free of charge, against the following decisions taken by the online platform on the ground that the information provided by the recipients is illegal content or incompatible with its terms and conditions:
(a)decisions to remove or disable access to the information;
(b)decisions to suspend or terminate the provision of the service, in whole or in part, to the recipients;
(c)decisions to suspend or terminate the recipients’ account.

2. Online platforms shall ensure that their internal complaint-handling systems are easy to access, user-friendly and enable and facilitate the submission of sufficiently precise and adequately substantiated complaints.

3. Online platforms shall handle complaints submitted through their internal complaint-handling system in a timely, diligent and objective manner. Where a complaint contains sufficient grounds for the online platform to consider that the information to which the complaint relates is not illegal and is not incompatible with its terms and conditions, or contains information indicating that the complainant’s conduct does not warrant the suspension or termination of the service or the account, it shall reverse its decision referred to in paragraph 1 without undue delay.

4. Online platforms shall inform complainants without undue delay of the decision they have taken in respect of the information to which the complaint relates and shall inform complainants of the possibility of out-of-court dispute settlement provided for in Article 18 and other available redress possibilities.

5. Online platforms shall ensure that the decisions, referred to in paragraph 4, are not solely taken on the basis of automated means.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to provide recipients with an effective internal complaint-handling system for decisions to remove, disable, suspend, or terminate access/accounts, ensuring timely handling and informing complainants of outcomes and further redress options.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's submission describes and endorses the NetzDG requirement that social networks maintain an effective and transparent complaint procedure for users regarding illegal content — directly mirroring what Article 17 establishes at EU level. The org's description of the NetzDG framework (mandatory complaint systems, review obligations, removal/blocking duties) aligns with the provision's concrete requirement for internal complaint-handling systems, suggesting advocacy for such mechanisms to be carried over into the DSA.

pairs.source_text: While we are aware of these challenges, we argue that the GDPR should not be reopened. Instead, a comprehensive regulation targeted at large online platforms, as contemplated by the European Commission in pending consultations, provides a much better chance to tackle them. It is in this context that we present our recommendations below. II. Recommendations Guiding principles for platforms regulation Before we present detailed recommendations, which will address specific challenges related to large platforms’ power to moderate, recommend, personalise and target content, we want to reinforce the following principles, which should apply to all online platforms, regardless of their size and business model: • Limitation of platforms’ liability for user-generated content Platform operators that act as intermediaries in two- and multi-sided markets should not be liable for illegal activity or content of which they do not have actual knowledge. Liability for any user-generated or user-uploaded content should primarily lie with the uploader, unless hosting intermediaries are co-creators of such content, provide content themselves or have actual knowledge of illegal content. It is important that we uphold this principle for the benefit of free expression and access to information. For the same reason, platform operators should not be obliged by the law to generally monitor content uploaded on their platforms
pairs.context_before: type of content that is presented to them or determination of their marketing profile). At the same time businesses argue that their analysis of statistical data falls outside of the scope of the GDPR and, as such, triggers no legal protection against potential harms or errors. In practice it remains difficult to track how exactly big data has been used to enrich individual profiles or inform individual decisions. • Profiling and targeting not covered by Article 22 of the GDPR: This provision regulates only fully automated decisions that produce legally binding or other significant effects for an individual. In consequence, it can hardly be used to ensure user’s control over content targeting/personalisation. While effects of targeting and personalisation are experienced by an individual, it remains difficult to “single out” a data processing operation that relates to a given individual (for marketing purposes users are “packed” into broader groups and categories). For example, in the context of behavioural advertising it is nearly impossible for an individual to prove that she or he has been targeted based on a specific set of criteria. For the same reasons it will be difficult to demand explanation of the logic behind content recommendations or content personalisation based on Article 22 of the GDPR . While we are aware of these challenges, we argue that the GDPR should not be reopened
pairs.context_after: For the same reason, platform operators should not be obliged by the law to generally monitor content uploaded on their platforms. • Full legal responsibility for platforms’ own actions At the same time platforms should bear full legal responsibility for their own actions that affect accessibility and visibility of user-generated content (such as its moderation and algorithmic curation) and users' experience on the platform. In particular such actions should trigger legal obligations that will ensure transparency (e.g. revealing the logic behind algorithmic content curation) and accountability (e.g. due process for users affected by content moderation). 6
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to implement reasonable and proportionate mitigation measures for systemic risks, including adapting content moderation and recommender systems, and mandates annual reports on systemic risks and best practices.
pairs.label: UNDETECTABLE
pairs.reasoning: Panoptykon advocates against general monitoring obligations and for limiting platform liability for user-generated content, which touches on content moderation obligations. Article 27 imposes risk mitigation measures including adapting content moderation systems on very large platforms, which could be seen as creating moderation obligations — but the provision is targeted and risk-specific rather than a general monitoring obligation, making it genuinely unclear whether it satisfies, contradicts, or is neutral to Panoptykon's specific concerns about general monitoring and liability.

The provision concerns the institutional tasks of the Board (coordination, audits, opinions, standards), which has no substantive connection to the org's specific advocacy on liability limitations and no-monitoring principles.

pairs.article_text:
Article 29 — Recommender systems
1. Very large online platforms that use recommender systems shall set out in their terms and conditions, in a clear, accessible and easily comprehensible manner, the main parameters used in their recommender systems, as well as any options for the recipients of the service to modify or influence those main parameters that they may have made available, including at least one option which is not based on profiling, within the meaning of Article 4 (4) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

2. Where several options are available pursuant to paragraph 1, very large online platforms shall provide an easily accessible functionality on their online interface allowing the recipient of the service to select and to modify at any time their preferred option for each of the recommender systems that determines the relative order of information presented to them.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to disclose the main parameters of their recommender systems in terms and conditions and provide users with at least one non-profiling-based option to modify those parameters, with an accessible interface to do so.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's text focuses on platform liability for user-generated content and the principle against general monitoring obligations — it does not express any specific advocacy position on recommender systems, their transparency, or user controls over algorithmic parameters. The excerpt contains no directional stance on the subject matter of Article 29.

pairs.article_text:
Article 56 — Commitments
1. If, during proceedings under this Section, the very large online platform concerned offers commitments to ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, the Commission may by decision make those commitments binding on the very large online platform concerned and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen the proceedings:
(a)where there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)where the very large online platform concerned acts contrary to its commitments; or
(c)where the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

3. Where the Commission considers that the commitments offered by the very large online platform concerned are unable to ensure effective compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, it shall reject those commitments in a reasoned decision when concluding the proceedings.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a commitments mechanism whereby very large online platforms can offer binding compliance commitments to the Commission, which may close proceedings, with provisions for reopening if circumstances change or commitments are breached.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on principles such as liability limitations for user-generated content, no general monitoring obligations, and the overall framework for platform regulation — it does not express any specific stance on a commitments/settlement mechanism in enforcement proceedings. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the provision's subject matter of binding commitments and procedural enforcement tools.
