pairs.article_text:
Article 54 — Power to conduct on-site inspections
1. In order to carry out the tasks assigned to it under this Section, the Commission may conduct on-site inspections at the premises of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

2. On-site inspections may also be carried out with the assistance of auditors or experts appointed by the Commission pursuant to Article 57(2).

3. During on-site inspections the Commission and auditors or experts appointed by it may require the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) to provide explanations on its organisation, functioning, IT system, algorithms, data-handling and business conducts. The Commission and auditors or experts appointed by it may address questions to key personnel of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

4. The very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) is required to submit to an on-site inspection ordered by decision of the Commission. The decision shall specify the subject matter and purpose of the visit, set the date on which it is to begin and indicate the penalties provided for in Articles 59 and 60 and the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice of the European Union.
pairs.provision_effect: Grants the Commission power to conduct on-site inspections at the premises of very large online platforms, including requiring explanations on organisation, IT systems, algorithms, and data handling.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: EuroCommerce's position focuses on the concept of "gatekeeping" and the scope/designation criteria for large online platforms, advocating for a case-by-case dominance-based approach rather than a new regulatory framework. It does not express any specific stance on the Commission's power to conduct on-site inspections, which is what Article 54 establishes.

pairs.source_text: It provides a solid basis to address potential frictions in the online economy and gain insights and experience into the functioning of various types of online models and the existence of market failures. The P2B Regulation and the initial review of its functioning, foreseen in 2022, provide a sound background for assessing whether targeted ex ante regulation is required to address specific and identified market failures. We do not see the need to rush through a new instrument before the effects of the P2B regulation are known. 3.2. Data access 11. Data plays a key role in the functioning of online platforms and eco-systems and the basis for competition and innovation in retail and wholesale as in many other sectors of the economy. We ask the Commission to consider, as part of their evaluation, the need for a case by case analysis, the possibility of adapting existing competition law tools and ensuring consistency in policy making: • EU competition rules help identify where requiring a dominant firm to grant access to specific data can be justified and where interoperability is indispensable to ensuring effective competition. EU competition rules already allow for obligatory data access if it can be shown that the dominant player, or the data it holds, amounts to an “essential facility” in competition law terms. 17 Case C-228/18, Budapest Bank, EU:C:2020:265, para. 76. 18 See Online Platform Observatory preliminary report on differentiated treatment p
pairs.context_before: 9. The impact of practices such as tying and self-preferencing very much depends on the specificities of individual markers and thus need to be analysed in their own context and circumstances. As indicated in the Online Platform Observatory’s preliminary report on differentiated treatment, differentiated treatment is present in many markets and is not problematic in itself18. There is no basis in case law for establishing a presumption that harm is caused whenever an online platform gives preference to its own products and services on its site. Such a rule would discourage investment and create disincentives to opening up the platform to third-parties. The Online Platform Observatory’s preliminary report on differentiated treatment similarly highlights the importance of a case-by-case analysis, and concludes that existing competition law is ‘capable to target the exclusionary forms of differentiated treatment, such as pure self-favouring and hybrid differentiation.’19 10. The relationships between marketplaces and their business users is furthermore already regulated under the Platform to Business Regulation (P2B regulation) which only recently entered into force. The P2B Regulation provides far-reaching transparency obligations on data sharing, ranking criteria and platforms’ terms and conditions and aims to address many of the concerns raised in the Commission inception impact assessments on both the ex ante instrument and the new competition tool
pairs.context_after: 17 Case C-228/18, Budapest Bank, EU:C:2020:265, para. 76. 18 See Online Platform Observatory preliminary report on differentiated treatment p. 5: In the online platform economy, differentiation is inherent in rankings, whose exact function is to list content in an order of importance or relevance in view of the platform’s offering to end-users 19 See Online Platform Observatory preliminary report on differentiated treatment p.28 ••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••• 9
pairs.article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
pairs.article_text:
Article 63 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Articles 58(1), 59 or 60, the Commission shall give the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections; and
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings referred to point (a).

2. The very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) may submit their observations on the Commission’s preliminary findings within a reasonable time period set by the Commission in its preliminary findings, which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which the parties concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the parties concerned shall be fully respected in the proceedings. They shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or Member States’ authorities. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and those authorities. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.

5. The information collected pursuant to Articles 52, 53 and 54 shall be used only for the purpose of this Regulation.

6. Without prejudice to the exchange and to the use of information referred to in Articles 51(3) and 52(5), the Commission, the Board, Member States’ authorities and their respective officials, servants and other persons working under their supervision,; and any other natural or legal person involved, including auditors and experts appointed pursuant to Article 57(2) shall not disclose information acquired or exchanged by them pursuant to this Section and of the kind covered by the obligation of professional secrecy.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes procedural rights for very large online platforms in Commission enforcement proceedings, including the right to be heard, access to the file, and professional secrecy obligations.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's position focuses on whether ex ante regulation is needed at all (arguing for caution pending P2B Regulation review) and on data access/competition law considerations. It expresses no specific advocacy position on procedural rights, the right to be heard, access to the file, or professional secrecy in enforcement proceedings — the subjects of Article 63. The org text is therefore unrelated to this provision's subject matter.

pairs.source_text: 4. Regulation of large platforms 4.1. The need for a new regulation is questionable 13. The ex ante regulatory instrument inception impact assessment and the consultation questionnaire seem to be based on the premise that an ex ante regulatory tool is needed to address conduct engaged in by “large online platforms with a gatekeeping role”. We agree that competition problems can arise with regards to online platforms, we however question that there is a need for any such new and “horizontal” instrument. Instead of seeking to create an entirely new enforcement system based on novel concepts, we would encourage the Commission to consider the possibility of refining existing EU competition rules to address new business models and practices enabled by digitalisation: • Competition law is there to address any conduct engaged in by market operators, including online services, that may interfere with the proper functioning of a market; sector specific regulation has been introduced in cases of proven structural market failure e.g. to liberalise sectors traditionally reserved to public monopolies (e.g. telecoms)24. By contrast, online marketplaces operate in markets that already exist and are vibrant and open. • Competition law is specifically designed to protect competition based on a case-by-case assessment that takes a wide range of factors into account
pairs.context_before: Retailers and wholesalers should be free to sell their products across various online and offline channels to meet the consumers need for a seamless multichannel experience; • prohibiting practices widely recognised as mutually beneficial economically on the basis of an economic dependency will result in a de-facto prohibition of these practices in dealings with SME suppliers. 20 See Online Platform Observatory preliminary report on data p.40 21 See Online Platform Observatory preliminary report on data p.40 22 See para. 22 of our response to the consultation on HBER: “In the Guidelines, the Commission should also address information exchange in (increasingly horizontal) relationships with suppliers that sell to customers directly online or via their own retail outlets or with the help of agency relationships.” 23 See our contribution to the consultation on VBER ••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••• 10
pairs.context_after: • Competition law is specifically designed to protect competition based on a case-by-case assessment that takes a wide range of factors into account. As stated above, after decades of case law, concepts such as “dominance” and ”abuse”’ have defined meanings, as opposed to “large” and “gatekeeper”. The envisaged ex ante regulation does not allow targeting specific and identified market failures that require a regulatory response, but rather singles out some subset of online services on the basis that they are “large” and are perceived to act as gatekeepers because they have many users. 14. The P2B Regulation and the initial review of its functioning can provide a sound basis for assessing whether targeted ex ante regulation is required to address specific and identified market failures. We would encourage the Commission to consider using the P2B Regulation as a tool for gaining experience with the great variety of online services and business models, and consider updating the existing competition law to the digital environment by: • Tightening the definition of markets e.g. in respect of product substitutability, developing a common methodology to assess multi-sided markets including zero-price markets and channel substitutability. • Updating the existing guidance on abusive exclusionary conduct by dominant players25 to reflect the digital environment. • Strengthening existing interim measures to ensure that infringements of Art. 101 and/or Art
pairs.article_text:
Article 35 — Codes of conduct
1. The Commission and the Board shall encourage and facilitate the drawing up of codes of conduct at Union level to contribute to the proper application of this Regulation, taking into account in particular the specific challenges of tackling different types of illegal content and systemic risks, in accordance with Union law, in particular on competition and the protection of personal data.

2. Where significant systemic risk within the meaning of Article 26(1) emerge and concern several very large online platforms, the Commission may invite the very large online platforms concerned, other very large online platforms, other online platforms and other providers of intermediary services, as appropriate, as well as civil society organisations and other interested parties, to participate in the drawing up of codes of conduct, including by setting out commitments to take specific risk mitigation measures, as well as a regular reporting framework on any measures taken and their outcomes.

3. When giving effect to paragraphs 1 and 2, the Commission and the Board shall aim to ensure that the codes of conduct clearly set out their objectives, contain key performance indicators to measure the achievement of those objectives and take due account of the needs and interests of all interested parties, including citizens, at Union level. The Commission and the Board shall also aim to ensure that participants report regularly to the Commission and their respective Digital Service Coordinators of establishment on any measures taken and their outcomes, as measured against the key performance indicators that they contain.

4. The Commission and the Board shall assess whether the codes of conduct meet the aims specified in paragraphs 1 and 3, and shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of their objectives. They shall publish their conclusions.

5. The Board shall regularly monitor and evaluate the achievement of the objectives of the codes of conduct, having regard to the key performance indicators that they may contain.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a framework for the Commission and the Board to encourage, facilitate, and monitor codes of conduct at Union level to address illegal content and systemic risks from very large online platforms.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's position focuses on questioning the need for new ex ante horizontal regulation of large platforms and advocating for refinement of existing competition law instead. The provision on codes of conduct is a specific mechanism within the DSA framework, but the org text does not address codes of conduct specifically — it only makes a broad argument against new regulatory instruments in favour of competition law. This is too general and unrelated to the specific codes of conduct mechanism to constitute a substantive advocacy position on this provision.

We would encourage the Commission to consider using the P2B Regulation as a tool for gaining experience with the great variety of online services and business models, and consider updating the existing competition law to the digital environment by: • Tightening the definition of markets e.g. in respect of product substitutability, developing a common methodology to assess multi-sided markets including zero-price markets and channel substitutability. • Updating the existing guidance on abusive exclusionary conduct by dominant players25 to reflect the digital environment. • Strengthening existing interim measures to ensure that infringements of Art. 101 and/or Art
pairs.article_text:
Article 56 — Commitments
1. If, during proceedings under this Section, the very large online platform concerned offers commitments to ensure compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, the Commission may by decision make those commitments binding on the very large online platform concerned and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen the proceedings:
(a)where there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)where the very large online platform concerned acts contrary to its commitments; or
(c)where the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

3. Where the Commission considers that the commitments offered by the very large online platform concerned are unable to ensure effective compliance with the relevant provisions of this Regulation, it shall reject those commitments in a reasoned decision when concluding the proceedings.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a commitments mechanism whereby very large online platforms can offer binding commitments to ensure compliance, allowing the Commission to close proceedings, with provisions for reopening and rejection of inadequate commitments.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: EuroCommerce explicitly questions the need for any new ex ante regulatory instrument targeting large online platforms, advocating instead for refinement of existing EU competition rules rather than creating an entirely new enforcement system. Article 56 is a procedural component of precisely that new enforcement system (the DSA/DMA-type regime), which EuroCommerce argues should not exist in the first place — making their position directly opposing the provision's existence.

pairs.source_text: 4. Regulation of large platforms 4.1. The need for a new regulation is questionable 13. The ex ante regulatory instrument inception impact assessment and the consultation questionnaire seem to be based on the premise that an ex ante regulatory tool is needed to address conduct engaged in by “large online platforms with a gatekeeping role”. We agree that competition problems can arise with regards to online platforms, we however question that there is a need for any such new and “horizontal” instrument. Instead of seeking to create an entirely new enforcement system based on novel concepts, we would encourage the Commission to consider the possibility of refining existing EU competition rules to address new business models and practices enabled by digitalisation: • Competition law is there to address any conduct engaged in by market operators, including online services, that may interfere with the proper functioning of a market; sector specific regulation has been introduced in cases of proven structural market failure e.g. to liberalise sectors traditionally reserved to public monopolies (e.g. telecoms)24. By contrast, online marketplaces operate in markets that already exist and are vibrant and open. • Competition law is specifically designed to protect competition based on a case-by-case assessment that takes a wide range of factors into account

As stated above, after decades of case law, concepts such as “dominance” and ”abuse”’ have defined meanings, as opposed to “large” and “gatekeeper”. The envisaged ex ante regulation does not allow targeting specific and identified market failures that require a regulatory response, but rather singles out some subset of online services on the basis that they are “large” and are perceived to act as gatekeepers because they have many users. 14. pairs.article_text:
Article 58 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision where it finds that the very large online platform concerned does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)the relevant provisions of this Regulation;
(b)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 55;
(c)commitments made binding pursuant to Article 56,

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the very large online platform concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures that it considers taking, or that it considers that the very large online platform concerned should take, in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1 the Commission shall order the very large online platform concerned to take the necessary measures to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 within a reasonable time period and to provide information on the measures that that platform intends to take to comply with the decision.

4. The very large online platform concerned shall provide the Commission with a description of the measures it has taken to ensure compliance with the decision pursuant to paragraph 1 upon their implementation.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a non-compliance decision mechanism by which the Commission can find and order very large online platforms to remedy breaches of the Regulation's provisions, interim measures, or binding commitments.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: EuroCommerce explicitly questions the need for a new ex ante regulatory instrument targeting large online platforms, arguing instead that existing EU competition law should be refined rather than creating an entirely new enforcement system. Article 58 is a core enforcement mechanism of precisely that new regulatory system, directly contradicting EuroCommerce's position that such a novel horizontal instrument should not exist.

pairs.article_text:
Article 59 — Fines
1. In the decision pursuant to Article 58, the Commission may impose on the very large online platform concerned fines not exceeding 6% of its total turnover in the preceding financial year where it finds that that platform, intentionally or negligently:
(a)infringes the relevant provisions of this Regulation;
(b)fails to comply with a decision ordering interim measures under Article 55; or
(c)fails to comply with a voluntary measure made binding by a decision pursuant to Articles 56.

2. The Commission may by decision impose on the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) fines not exceeding 1% of the total turnover in the preceding financial year, where they intentionally or negligently:
(a)supply incorrect, incomplete or misleading information in response to a request pursuant to Article 52 or, when the information is requested by decision, fail to reply to the request within the set time period;
(b)fail to rectify within the time period set by the Commission, incorrect, incomplete or misleading information given by a member of staff, or fail or refuse to provide complete information;
(c)refuse to submit to an on-site inspection pursuant to Article 54.

3. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 2, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1).

4. In fixing the amount of the fine, the Commission shall have regard to the nature, gravity, duration and recurrence of the infringement and, for fines imposed pursuant to paragraph 2, the delay caused to the proceedings.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a fine regime for very large online platforms, with fines up to 6% of total turnover for infringements of the Regulation and up to 1% for procedural violations such as supplying incorrect information or refusing inspections.
pairs.label: OPPOSING
pairs.reasoning: EuroCommerce explicitly questions the need for a new ex ante regulatory instrument for large online platforms, arguing that existing EU competition law should be refined instead of creating an entirely new enforcement system. Article 59 is a core enforcement mechanism of precisely that new regulatory system, imposing substantial fines — its existence presupposes the new horizontal instrument EuroCommerce argues should not exist, making the organisation's position directly opposed to this provision.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")
pairs.source_text: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 Fundamental rights considerations 6. Online platforms play a crucial role in allowing users to access the benefits of the information society, including, in particular, to seek and impart information and ideas.1 Indeed, it has been observed that “the provision of Internet services is increasingly becoming a prerequisite for a comprehensive participatory democracy.”2 It follows that although online platforms are private actors, they are capable of affecting the fundamental rights of internet users. 7. A number of organisations, including the Council of Europe, have highlighted the “chilling effect” that online platforms are capable of having on the freedom of expression of internet users. The need to balance freedom of speech against the potential risk of significant harm to consumers and society, resulting from the exceptionally wide reach of the major platforms and potential abuse by authors of unlawful content, is a classic issue of public policy that should be addressed by legislation and public agencies rather than delegated to private actors, however powerful. 8. Online platforms are already engaged in removing and disabling content that is alleged to be unlawful
pairs.context_before: 4. The position paper adopts the structure of the Consultation, providing answers to the questions posed in the following sections: I. How to effectively keep users safe online II. Reviewing the liability regime of digital services acting as intermediaries III. What issues derive from the gatekeeper power of digital platforms? We are not responding to Sections IV, V and VI. The specific questions posed in the Consultation are reproduced in italics, with our views set out in numbered paragraphs. Introductory remarks 5. The Bar Council welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the discussion on the desirability and viability of regulatory measures in relation to online platforms. Before proceeding to the specific questions posed in the Consultation, we wish to make the following observations. 1
pairs.context_after: 8. Online platforms are already engaged in removing and disabling content that is alleged to be unlawful. The current regime under Directive 2000/31/EC (the E-Commerce Directive) affords liability exemptions to online platforms only insofar as they act expeditiously in removing any unlawful content of which they become aware (the so-called “notice and action” mechanism). In addition, online platforms have come under considerable pressure in recent years to take proactive steps to tackle unlawful (as well as, in some cases, ‘harmful’) content, in accordance with ‘self-regulatory’ initiatives, or with their own terms of use. 9. The fundamental rights implications of tasking private actors, such as online platforms, with the enforcement of public policy objectives are well-documented.3 Not only does this arrangement sidestep the safeguards that apply to direct state interference,4 the notice and action mechanism creates “an incentive to systematically take down material, without hearing from the party whose material is removed, thus preventing such a party from its right to 1 This appears to be recognised in the Impact Assessment, which states that online platforms “play a particularly important role in how citizens communicate, share and consume information…” 2 Council of Europe, Human rights guidelines for Internet service providers (available here: https://rm.coe.int/16805a39d5), para. 2
pairs.article_title: Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 23 — Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
1. In addition to the information referred to in Article 13, online platforms shall include in the reports referred to in that Article information on the following:
(a)the number of disputes submitted to the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies referred to in Article 18, the outcomes of the dispute settlement and the average time needed for completing the dispute settlement procedures;
(b)the number of suspensions imposed pursuant to Article 20, distinguishing between suspensions enacted for the provision of manifestly illegal content, the submission of manifestly unfounded notices and the submission of manifestly unfounded complaints;
(c)any use made of automatic means for the purpose of content moderation, including a specification of the precise purposes, indicators of the accuracy of the automated means in fulfilling those purposes and any safeguards applied.

2. Online platforms shall publish, at least once every six months, information on the average monthly active recipients of the service in each Member State, calculated as an average over the period of the past six months, in accordance with the methodology laid down in the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 25(2).

3. Online platforms shall communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, upon its request, the information referred to in paragraph 2, updated to the moment of such request. That Digital Services Coordinator may require the online platform to provide additional information as regards the calculation referred to in that paragraph, including explanations and substantiation in respect of the data used. That information shall not include personal data.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to lay down templates concerning the form, content and other details of reports pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to report on out-of-court dispute settlements, suspensions, use of automated content moderation tools, and average monthly active recipients, with publication and communication obligations to Digital Services Coordinators.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council's text expresses general concern about the chilling effect of online platforms on freedom of expression and argues that content moderation decisions should be governed by legislation and public agencies rather than delegated to private actors. This is a broad values-based position without a specific directional stance on transparency reporting obligations for platforms. The org text does not advocate for or against the specific transparency and reporting mechanisms established in Article 23.

pairs.provision_effect: Requires very large online platforms to conduct annual systemic risk assessments covering illegal content dissemination, fundamental rights impacts (including freedom of expression), and intentional manipulation of their services.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly advocates that online platforms are capable of affecting fundamental rights — particularly freedom of expression — and that these risks should be addressed by legislation and public agencies rather than left to private actors. Article 26 directly responds to this by mandating that very large online platforms formally assess systemic risks to fundamental rights, including freedom of expression (Article 11 of the Charter), through a legislatively required annual process. This is a concrete regulatory action in the direction the Bar Council advocated for.

pairs.article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
pairs.article_text:
Article 63 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Articles 58(1), 59 or 60, the Commission shall give the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections; and
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings referred to point (a).

2. The very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) may submit their observations on the Commission’s preliminary findings within a reasonable time period set by the Commission in its preliminary findings, which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which the parties concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the parties concerned shall be fully respected in the proceedings. They shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or Member States’ authorities. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and those authorities. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.

5. The information collected pursuant to Articles 52, 53 and 54 shall be used only for the purpose of this Regulation.

6. Without prejudice to the exchange and to the use of information referred to in Articles 51(3) and 52(5), the Commission, the Board, Member States’ authorities and their respective officials, servants and other persons working under their supervision,; and any other natural or legal person involved, including auditors and experts appointed pursuant to Article 57(2) shall not disclose information acquired or exchanged by them pursuant to this Section and of the kind covered by the obligation of professional secrecy.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes procedural rights for very large online platforms in Commission enforcement proceedings, including the right to be heard, access to the file, and professional secrecy obligations.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council's text focuses on fundamental rights of internet users (freedom of expression, chilling effects) and the appropriate role of public agencies versus private actors in content moderation — it does not express any specific advocacy position on procedural rights for platforms in Commission enforcement proceedings. The org's position is unrelated to the subject matter of Article 63's procedural safeguards.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")
pairs.source_text: Whilst online platforms possess the means to eliminate access to objectionable material, it does not necessarily follow that they should be instructed to do so. Insofar as ‘harmful’ content merits regulation, we consider that this should be done at Member State or EU level through established legislative and administrative processes, and not left entirely to the discretion of private individuals working for the online platforms. 12. Insofar as it is deemed that the imposition of additional obligations is necessary, we urge the EU to ensure that adequate safeguards are put in place to guarantee due process and respect for the fundamental rights of users. For instance, (with some exceptions) users should be given the opportunity to submit a counter-notice prior to any removal decision (please see para. 36 below). Moreover, the system must allow recourse to the judicial system. The risks of a “horizontal” approach 13. The Impact Assessment sets out the justification for a horizontal approach (i.e. a regulatory approach that delegates discretion to private actors in respect of unlawful and ‘harmful’ content) [on p. 2]: “This digital transformation has brought major benefits but also challenges. For example, the online sale of counterfeit, dangerous products or other illegal goods puts citizens at risk and harms legitimate businesses. Digital services are exploited by certain users to spread illegal content online such as child sexual abuse material or illegal hate speech
pairs.context_before: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 evidence its lawful use of the material”.5 Moreover, the notice and action mechanism is vulnerable to abuse by, for instance, competitors and political adversaries.6 10. In light of the above, we consider that any legislative proposals must expressly address the Article 10 ECHR implications of imposing further duties on online platforms. We caution against the imposition of any additional duties going beyond a prohibition of content that is manifestly unlawful (such as child sexual abuse material and advertisement of dangerous or unlawful products or services), thereby leaving too much discretion in the hands of intermediaries. In particular, we consider that the regulation of matters such as hate speech, which require an understanding of linguistic and cultural nuance,7 is best left to enforcement and judicial authorities, who should be entitled to rely on the cooperation of (as opposed to delegation of authority to) online platforms in appropriate cases. 11. Moreover, we urge caution as regards the imposition of duties in relation to ‘harmful’ (as opposed to unlawful) content. Whilst online platforms possess the means to eliminate access to objectionable material, it does not necessarily follow that they should be instructed to do so
pairs.context_after: Digital services are exploited by certain users to spread illegal content online such as child sexual abuse material or illegal hate speech. Infringements to 5 Barcelo and Koelman, Intermediary liability in the E-Commerce Directive: so far so good, but it’s not enough, p. 231. 6 Kuczerawy, p. 6. 7 See J Koebler and J Cox, The Impossible Job: Inside Facebook’s Struggle to Moderate Two Billion People, (23 Aug 2018) Motherboard, https://motherboard.vice.com/en_us/article/xwk9zd/how-facebook-content- moderation-works: according to Facebook “its AI tools—many of which are trained with data from its human moderation team—detect nearly 100 percent of spam, and that 99.5 percent of terrorist-related removals, 98.5 percent of fake accounts, 96 percent of adult nudity and sexual activity, and 86 percent of graphic violence-related removals are detected by AI, not users.” However, when it comes to hate speech Facebook’s AI detects: “just 38 percent of the hate speech-related posts it ultimately removes, and at the moment it doesn’t have enough training data for the AI to be very effective outside of English and Portuguese”. 3
pairs.article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
pairs.article_text:
Article 63 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Articles 58(1), 59 or 60, the Commission shall give the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections; and
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings referred to point (a).

2. The very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) may submit their observations on the Commission’s preliminary findings within a reasonable time period set by the Commission in its preliminary findings, which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which the parties concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the parties concerned shall be fully respected in the proceedings. They shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or Member States’ authorities. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and those authorities. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.

5. The information collected pursuant to Articles 52, 53 and 54 shall be used only for the purpose of this Regulation.

6. Without prejudice to the exchange and to the use of information referred to in Articles 51(3) and 52(5), the Commission, the Board, Member States’ authorities and their respective officials, servants and other persons working under their supervision,; and any other natural or legal person involved, including auditors and experts appointed pursuant to Article 57(2) shall not disclose information acquired or exchanged by them pursuant to this Section and of the kind covered by the obligation of professional secrecy.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes procedural rights for very large online platforms in Commission enforcement proceedings, including the right to be heard, access to the file, and protection of business secrets and professional secrecy obligations.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly urges the EU to ensure "adequate safeguards are put in place to guarantee due process and respect for the fundamental rights of users" and that the system must allow for procedural protections such as counter-notices and recourse before removal decisions. Article 63 directly establishes due process safeguards — the right to be heard, the right to comment on preliminary findings, and full respect for rights of defence — which aligns with the Bar Council's specific advocacy for procedural guarantees and due process protections in the DSA enforcement framework.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")
pairs.source_text: Section I. How to effectively keep users safe online 16. This section addresses the various measures that the Consultation proposes to impose on online platforms to increase user safety. The suggested measures are set out in italics below, with the Bar Council’s response in numbered paragraphs. “2. Clarifying responsibilities for online platforms and other digital services. 1. What responsibilities (i.e. legal obligations) should be imposed on online platforms and under what conditions?” “Maintain an effective ‘notice and action’ system for reporting illegal goods or content” 17. We agree that online platforms should have in place effective and easy-to-access complaints functions, which intermediaries should adequately monitor. 4
pairs.context_before: This Inception Impact Assessment sets out this intervention logic.” 14. The EU, in common with some of its Member States (and the UK), is seeking to address all the ills of the internet in one go. We consider this approach to be problematic, not least because of (a) the diversity of online platforms; and (b) the various concerns listed in the passage quoted above, which raise discrete issues. If the EU pursues this, then it will be necessary to adopt a multi-tiered approach that fully respects the public policy issues set out above and takes account of the risks associated with delegating significant discretion to restrict freedom of speech to private actors. In so far as this is adopted as a legislative strategy, any such discretion should be closely defined and should have significant regulatory and procedural protections to ensure that the necessary balance is struck. 15. In addition, a sectoral approach may be preferable in certain cases. For instance, we think that unfair business-to-consumer commercial practices are best dealt with within the remit of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (the UCPD), possibly with modifications or amendments to address this specific area of policy concern. Section I. How to effectively keep users safe online 16. This section addresses the various measures that the Consultation proposes to impose on online platforms to increase user safety
pairs.context_after: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 “Maintain a system for assessing the risk of exposure to illegal goods or content” 18. It is not entirely clear what is being proposed by this question. “Have content moderation teams, appropriately trained and resourced” 19. We agree that, in principle, any content moderation teams must be appropriately trained and well resourced, so as inter alia to reduce the incidence of removal of legitimate content. 20. It is worth noting, however, that there are concerns amongst certain online platforms that engaging in content moderation will result in losing any protection they would otherwise have under Article 14 of the E-Commerce Directive, following the Grand Chamber’s judgment in L’Oréal SA v eBay International AG (see para 69 et seq below).8 21. Whether this is based on a misunderstanding of the relevant principles (as certain respondents have suggested in their initial feedback (see e.g. from the Motion Picture Association9), any imposition of moderation duties must, in our opinion, be accompanied by clarification as to the applicability of existing safe harbour rules (assuming these are retained). “Systematically respond to requests from law enforcement authorities” 22. We agree with this proposal. “Cooperate with national authorities and law enforcement, in accordance with clear procedures” 23
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to suspend services to users who frequently provide manifestly illegal content, and to suspend processing of notices/complaints from those who frequently submit manifestly unfounded reports, with case-by-case assessment and transparent policies.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council's position is a general endorsement that online platforms should have "effective and easy-to-access complaints functions" that are "adequately monitored" — this is a broad, directional value statement without a specific stance on suspension mechanisms, anti-misuse rules, or the particular safeguards and obligations Article 20 establishes. An organisation with the opposite view on misuse/suspension mechanisms could equally produce a text supporting effective notice-and-action systems, so the position is too general to constitute specific alignment with this provision.

pairs.context_after: “Systematically respond to requests from law enforcement authorities” 22. We agree that cooperation with enforcement authorities is important and that clear procedures are required to give effect to such cooperation. However, we consider that online platforms are best placed to determine the nature of such procedures, which will necessarily (i) depend on the size of the intermediary, (ii) the nature of the intermediary’s activities, and (iii) should be proportionate to the risk of harm. “Cooperate with trusted organisations with proven expertise that can report illegal activities for fast analysis ('trusted flaggers')” 24. pairs.article_title: Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 23 — Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
1. In addition to the information referred to in Article 13, online platforms shall include in the reports referred to in that Article information on the following:
(a)the number of disputes submitted to the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies referred to in Article 18, the outcomes of the dispute settlement and the average time needed for completing the dispute settlement procedures;
(b)the number of suspensions imposed pursuant to Article 20, distinguishing between suspensions enacted for the provision of manifestly illegal content, the submission of manifestly unfounded notices and the submission of manifestly unfounded complaints;
(c)any use made of automatic means for the purpose of content moderation, including a specification of the precise purposes, indicators of the accuracy of the automated means in fulfilling those purposes and any safeguards applied.

2. Online platforms shall publish, at least once every six months, information on the average monthly active recipients of the service in each Member State, calculated as an average over the period of the past six months, in accordance with the methodology laid down in the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 25(2).

3. Online platforms shall communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, upon its request, the information referred to in paragraph 2, updated to the moment of such request. That Digital Services Coordinator may require the online platform to provide additional information as regards the calculation referred to in that paragraph, including explanations and substantiation in respect of the data used. That information shall not include personal data.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to lay down templates concerning the form, content and other details of reports pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to report on content moderation activities, including use of automated means, accuracy indicators, and safeguards applied, as part of transparency reporting obligations.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly agrees that content moderation teams must be appropriately trained and well-resourced, and specifically calls for safeguards (including clarification on safe harbour rules) to accompany any moderation duties. Article 23(1)(c) directly requires transparency reporting on automated content moderation, including the safeguards applied, which responds to the Bar Council's advocacy for accountability and safeguards around content moderation practices.

pairs.article_text:
Article 19 — Trusted flaggers
1. Online platforms shall take the necessary technical and organisational measures to ensure that notices submitted by trusted flaggers through the mechanisms referred to in Article 14, are processed and decided upon with priority and without delay.

2. The status of trusted flaggers under this Regulation shall be awarded, upon application by any entities, by the Digital Services Coordinator of the Member State in which the applicant is established, where the applicant has demonstrated to meet all of the following conditions:
(a)it has particular expertise and competence for the purposes of detecting, identifying and notifying illegal content;
(b)it represents collective interests and is independent from any online platform;
(c) it carries out its activities for the purposes of submitting notices in a timely, diligent and objective manner.

3. Digital Services Coordinators shall communicate to the Commission and the Board the names, addresses and electronic mail addresses of the entities to which they have awarded the status of the trusted flagger in accordance with paragraph 2.

4. The Commission shall publish the information referred to in paragraph 3 in a publicly available database and keep the database updated.

5. Where an online platform has information indicating that a trusted flagger submitted a significant number of insufficiently precise or inadequately substantiated notices through the mechanisms referred to in Article 14, including information gathered in connection to the processing of complaints through the internal complaint-handling systems referred to in Article 17(3), it shall communicate that information to the Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to the entity concerned, providing the necessary explanations and supporting documents.

6. The Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to an entity shall revoke that status if it determines, following an investigation either on its own initiative or on the basis information received by third parties, including the information provided by an online platform pursuant to paragraph 5, that the entity no longer meets the conditions set out in paragraph 2. Before revoking that status, the Digital Services Coordinator shall afford the entity an opportunity to react to the findings of its investigation and its intention to revoke the entity’s status as trusted flagger

7. The Commission, after consulting the Board, may issue guidance to assist online platforms and Digital Services Coordinators in the application of paragraphs 5 and 6.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a formal trusted flagger system whereby designated entities receive priority processing of their notices by online platforms, with conditions for awarding, maintaining, and revoking trusted flagger status managed by Digital Services Coordinators.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly supports the concept of trusted flaggers receiving priority treatment over ordinary public notices, which is precisely what Article 19 establishes. However, the Bar Council also cautions that trusted flagger notices should not be "accepted uncritically or at face value," and the provision addresses this concern by including mechanisms for revoking trusted flagger status when notices are insufficiently substantiated — making the provision broadly aligned with the organisation's stated position.

pairs.provision_effect: Requires the Commission to support and promote the development of voluntary industry standards for electronic submission of notices, APIs, auditing, interoperability, and data transmission related to platform obligations.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's position addresses trusted flaggers in terms of prioritisation and critical scrutiny of their notices, not the technical standardisation of electronic submission interfaces or APIs. The provision concerns voluntary technical standards for platform compliance mechanisms, which is a distinct subject from the org's substantive advocacy on trusted flagger procedures and law enforcement cooperation. There is no specific advocacy position in the org text that relates to the standardisation measures this provision establishes.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")
pairs.source_text: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 “Request professional users to identify themselves clearly (‘know your customer’ policy)” 30. We agree with this proposal. “Provide technical means allowing professional users to comply with their obligations (e.g. enable them to publish on the platform the pre-contractual information consumers need to receive in accordance with applicable consumer law)” 31. Insofar as this question is concerned with consumer law, the European Commission has already expressed the view that providing the technical means that enable traders to comply with their consumer law obligations is a requirement under the UCPD. As noted in its guidance on the UCPD [at 5.2.2] (with emphasis added):10 “Platforms which are considered "traders", should take appropriate measures which – without amounting to a general obligation to monitor or carry out fact-finding (see (1) e-Commerce Directive) – enable relevant third party traders to comply with EU consumer and marketing law requirements and users to clearly understand with whom they are possibly concluding contracts
pairs.context_before: 29. If this objective is pursued, then it seems to us that it should go no further than an obligation to refer professional users to the relevant regulatory provisions in the relevant jurisdictions, although we remain sceptical that this would provide significant protections beyond those imposed on suppliers directly. 6
pairs.context_after: For example, such measures could imply: • Enabling relevant third party traders to clearly indicate that they act, vis-à-vis the platform users, as traders; • Clearly indicating to all platform users that they will only benefit from protection under EU consumer and marketing laws in their relations with those suppliers who are traders; • Designing their web-structure in a way that enables third party traders to present information to platform users in a way that enables third party traders to comply with EU marketing and consumer law – in particular, information required by Article 7(4) UCPD in the case of invitations to purchase.”11 32. The UCPD was recently subject to revision in the form of Directive 2019/2161 (the Omnibus Directive). If it is considered that any gaps remain in the protection offered to consumers, this should be addressed as part of future revisions of the UCPD (if any), and only after the impact of changes made through the Omnibus Directive become clear
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to collect and verify trader identification information before allowing them to offer products/services to consumers, and to provide technical means enabling traders to comply with pre-contractual and product safety information obligations.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly agrees with requiring professional users to identify themselves clearly ("know your customer" policy) and with providing technical means for traders to comply with their obligations — both of which are directly delivered by Article 22, which mandates trader identification/verification (paragraphs 1–3) and requires platforms to design their interfaces to enable traders to meet pre-contractual information obligations (paragraph 7). The provision acts precisely in the direction the organisation advocated for.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")

pairs.context_after: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 “Inform consumers when they become aware of product recalls or sales of illegal goods” 33. We agree with this proposal, but consider that any such duty should be limited to unsafe goods. “Cooperate with other online platforms for exchanging best practices, sharing information or tools to tackle illegal activities” 34. We consider that such cooperation should be encouraged but do not see any justification for the imposition of a legal duty to this effect. “Be transparent about their content policies, measures and their effects” 35. We agree that online platforms should be transparent about their content policies, albeit we have not seen evidence to suggest that this is not currently the case. It is not entirely clear what is being suggested by transparency in relation to “measures and their effects”. “Maintain an effective ‘counter-notice’ system for users whose goods or content is removed to dispute erroneous decisions” 36. It appears, from the way the question is posed, that it is envisaged that the opportunity to submit a counter-notice will arise only once the impugned content is removed. In our opinion, save in exceptional circumstances, such as the ones set out in Recital 21 of the Commission Recommendation on Measures to Effectively Tackle Illegal Content Online (the 2018 Commission Recommendation)12, a content provider should be given the opportunity to submit a counter-notice before the removal decision is taken
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to collect, verify, store, and publicly display trader identification and compliance information before allowing traders to offer products or services to consumers in the Union.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council's text explicitly references the obligation for online platforms to enable traders to comply with their legal obligations — including displaying trader status and business address — citing the UCPD and CMA guidance. Article 22 directly operationalises this by mandating that platforms collect trader identification details and make them available to consumers, which is precisely the mechanism the org's cited position advocates for.

Such a process is to be preferred for the following reasons: (i) In determining whether content is unlawful, it is often necessary to have a good grasp of the relevant context. For instance, in defamation law in England and Wales, a statement may be defamatory, but it will not be unlawful if it is true or if it is a statement of honest opinion.13 (ii) Giving a content provider the opportunity to contest an allegation is consistent with freedom of speech, the freedom to conduct a business and due process; 12 Brussels, 1.3.2018 C(2018) 1177 final, Recital 28 13 For instance, in Bunt v Tilley [2007] 1 WLR 1243 the High Court observed (in the context of the availability of the defence under Article 14 of the E-Commerce Directive) that “in order to be able to characterise something as ‘unlawful’ a person would need to know something of the strength or weakness of available defences” [at para. 72]. (a)it has particular expertise and competence for the purposes of detecting, identifying and notifying illegal content;
(b)it represents collective interests and is independent from any online platform;
(c) it carries out its activities for the purposes of submitting notices in a timely, diligent and objective manner.

3. Digital Services Coordinators shall communicate to the Commission and the Board the names, addresses and electronic mail addresses of the entities to which they have awarded the status of the trusted flagger in accordance with paragraph 2.

4. The Commission shall publish the information referred to in paragraph 3 in a publicly available database and keep the database updated.

5. Where an online platform has information indicating that a trusted flagger submitted a significant number of insufficiently precise or inadequately substantiated notices through the mechanisms referred to in Article 14, including information gathered in connection to the processing of complaints through the internal complaint-handling systems referred to in Article 17(3), it shall communicate that information to the Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to the entity concerned, providing the necessary explanations and supporting documents.

6. The Digital Services Coordinator that awarded the status of trusted flagger to an entity shall revoke that status if it determines, following an investigation either on its own initiative or on the basis information received by third parties, including the information provided by an online platform pursuant to paragraph 5, that the entity no longer meets the conditions set out in paragraph 2. Before revoking that status, the Digital Services Coordinator shall afford the entity an opportunity to react to the findings of its investigation and its intention to revoke the entity’s status as trusted flagger

7. The Commission, after consulting the Board, may issue guidance to assist online platforms and Digital Services Coordinators in the application of paragraphs 5 and 6.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes a trusted flagger system whereby designated entities receive priority processing of their illegal content notices by online platforms, with conditions for awarding and revoking trusted flagger status managed by Digital Services Coordinators.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The organisation's text addresses topics such as product recalls, cooperation between platforms, content policy transparency, and counter-notice systems for users whose content is removed — none of which relate to the trusted flagger mechanism established in Article 19. There is no substantive advocacy position expressed on the creation or operation of a trusted flagger system.

pairs.organisation: General Council of the Bar of England and Wales ("The Bar Council")
pairs.source_text: EU Transparency Register ID Number: 39850528734-23 “Inform consumers when they become aware of product recalls or sales of illegal goods” 33. We agree with this proposal, but consider that any such duty should be limited to unsafe goods. “Cooperate with other online platforms for exchanging best practices, sharing information or tools to tackle illegal activities” 34. We consider that such cooperation should be encouraged but do not see any justification for the imposition of a legal duty to this effect. “Be transparent about their content policies, measures and their effects” 35. We agree that online platforms should be transparent about their content policies, albeit we have not seen evidence to suggest that this is not currently the case. It is not entirely clear what is being suggested by transparency in relation to “measures and their effects”. “Maintain an effective ‘counter-notice’ system for users whose goods or content is removed to dispute erroneous decisions” 36. It appears, from the way the question is posed, that it is envisaged that the opportunity to submit a counter-notice will arise only once the impugned content is removed. In our opinion, save in exceptional circumstances, such as the ones set out in of the Commission Recommendation on Measures to Effectively Tackle Illegal Content Online (the 2018 Commission Recommendation)12, a content provider should be given the opportunity to submit a counter-notice before the removal decision is taken

For instance, in defamation law in England and Wales, a statement may be defamatory, but it will not be unlawful if it is true or if it is a statement of honest opinion.13 (ii) Giving a content provider the opportunity to contest an allegation is consistent with freedom of speech, the freedom to conduct a business and due process; 12 Brussels, 1.3.2018 C(2018) 1177 final, Recital 28 13 For instance, in Bunt v Tilley [2007] 1 WLR 1243 the High Court observed (in the context of the availability of the defence under Article 14 of the E-Commerce Directive) that “in order to be able to characterise something as ‘unlawful’ a person would need to know something of the strength or weakness of available defences” [at para. 72]. As observed by the UN Special Rapporteur on the promotion and protection of the right to freedom of opinion and expression, “Companies emphasize the importance of context when assessing the applicability of general restrictions

pairs.article_text:
Article 14 — Notice and action mechanisms
1. Providers of hosting services shall put mechanisms in place to allow any individual or entity to notify them of the presence on their service of specific items of information that the individual or entity considers to be illegal content. Those mechanisms shall be easy to access, user-friendly, and allow for the submission of notices exclusively by electronic means.

2. The mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1 shall be such as to facilitate the submission of sufficiently precise and adequately substantiated notices, on the basis of which a diligent economic operator can identify the illegality of the content in question. To that end, the providers shall take the necessary measures to enable and facilitate the submission of notices containing all of the following elements:
(a)an explanation of the reasons why the individual or entity considers the information in question to be illegal content;
(b)a clear indication of the electronic location of that information, in particular the exact URL or URLs, and, where necessary, additional information enabling the identification of the illegal content;
(c)the name and an electronic mail address of the individual or entity submitting the notice, except in the case of information considered to involve one of the offences referred to in Articles 3 to 7 of Directive 2011/93/EU;
(d)a statement confirming the good faith belief of the individual or entity submitting the notice that the information and allegations contained therein are accurate and complete.

3. Notices that include the elements referred to in paragraph 2 shall be considered to give rise to actual knowledge or awareness for the purposes of Article 5 in respect of the specific item of information concerned.

4. Where the notice contains the name and an electronic mail address of the individual or entity that submitted it, the provider of hosting services shall promptly send a confirmation of receipt of the notice to that individual or entity.

5. The provider shall also, without undue delay, notify that individual or entity of its decision in respect of the information to which the notice relates, providing information on the redress possibilities in respect of that decision.

6. Providers of hosting services shall process any notices that they receive under the mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1, and take their decisions in respect of the information to which the notices relate, in a timely, diligent and objective manner. Where they use automated means for that processing or decision-making, they shall include information on such use in the notification referred to in paragraph 4.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes mandatory notice-and-action mechanisms for hosting services, requiring notices to include precise location (URL), reasons for illegality, identity of the notifier (with an exception for child sexual abuse material), and a good-faith statement, and sets out obligations for providers to process and respond to notices.
pairs.label: ALIGNED
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council explicitly advocated that notices alleging unlawful activity must be sufficiently precise and should include all four elements — precise location (URL), precise reason why the activity is considered illegal, description of the activity, and identity of the notifier — which maps directly onto Article 14(2)(a)–(d). The organisation also raised the specific question of conditions under which identity information would not be necessary, which is reflected in the Article 14(2)(c) exception for child sexual abuse offences, showing the provision acts in the same direction as the org's advocacy.

pairs.article_title: Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
pairs.article_text:
Article 23 — Transparency reporting obligations for providers of online platforms
1. In addition to the information referred to in Article 13, online platforms shall include in the reports referred to in that Article information on the following:
(a)the number of disputes submitted to the out-of-court dispute settlement bodies referred to in Article 18, the outcomes of the dispute settlement and the average time needed for completing the dispute settlement procedures;
(b)the number of suspensions imposed pursuant to Article 20, distinguishing between suspensions enacted for the provision of manifestly illegal content, the submission of manifestly unfounded notices and the submission of manifestly unfounded complaints;
(c)any use made of automatic means for the purpose of content moderation, including a specification of the precise purposes, indicators of the accuracy of the automated means in fulfilling those purposes and any safeguards applied.

2. Online platforms shall publish, at least once every six months, information on the average monthly active recipients of the service in each Member State, calculated as an average over the period of the past six months, in accordance with the methodology laid down in the delegated acts adopted pursuant to Article 25(2).

3. Online platforms shall communicate to the Digital Services Coordinator of establishment, upon its request, the information referred to in paragraph 2, updated to the moment of such request. That Digital Services Coordinator may require the online platform to provide additional information as regards the calculation referred to in that paragraph, including explanations and substantiation in respect of the data used. That information shall not include personal data.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts to lay down templates concerning the form, content and other details of reports pursuant to paragraph 1.
pairs.provision_effect: Requires online platforms to report on out-of-court dispute outcomes, suspensions (including for manifestly unfounded notices/complaints), and use of automated content moderation, and to publish data on active recipients every six months.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The Bar Council's position focuses on notice requirements (what information a notifier must provide, identity conditions, over-removal concerns, and prior notification before takedown), not on transparency reporting obligations for platforms. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the reporting and publication requirements established by Article 23.

pairs.article_text:
Article 52 — Requests for information
1. In order to carry out the tasks assigned to it under this Section, the Commission may by simple request or by decision require the very large online platforms concerned, as well as any other persons acting for purposes related to their trade, business, craft or profession that may be reasonably be aware of information relating to the suspected infringement or the infringement, as applicable, including organisations performing the audits referred to in Articles 28 and 50(3), to provide such information within a reasonable time period.

2. When sending a simple request for information to the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1), the Commission shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and set the time period within which the information is to be provided, and the penalties provided for in Article 59 for supplying incorrect or misleading information.

3. Where the Commission requires the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) to supply information by decision, it shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and set the time period within which it is to be provided. It shall also indicate the penalties provided for in Article 59 and indicate or impose the periodic penalty payments provided for in Article 60. It shall further indicate the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice of the European Union.

4. The owners of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1) or their representatives and, in the case of legal persons, companies or firms, or where they have no legal personality, the persons authorised to represent them by law or by their constitution shall supply the information requested on behalf of the very large online platform concerned or other person referred to in Article 52(1). Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply the information on behalf of their clients. The latter shall remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

5. At the request of the Commission, the Digital Services Coordinators and other competent authorities shall provide the Commission with all necessary information to carry out the tasks assigned to it under this Section.
pairs.provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to request information from very large online platforms and other persons in the context of investigating suspected infringements, including procedural requirements for such requests.
pairs.label: NOISE
pairs.reasoning: The org's position focuses on notice-and-takedown procedures, over-removal of content, and the information requirements for users/third parties notifying illegal activity to platforms — not on the Commission's investigatory powers to request information from platforms. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the Commission's information-gathering powers established in Article 52.
